## Phase Purpose

Deeply analyze requirements, edge cases, constraints, assumptions, risks, and open questions, resolving ambiguity left from earlier phases.

## Transcript


### Round 1


**Codex (Product Strategist · User Advocate) — Round 1**

The unresolved part here is not the feature list. It is the trust contract. This app is for someone who may be making a physically costly decision with incomplete data, often while rushed, tired, or already stressed. So the product requirement I would lock in for this phase is simple: every route recommendation has to communicate both usefulness and uncertainty at the same time. If we only optimize for “helpful,” we risk false confidence. If we only optimize for “honest,” we risk a timid app that feels like it never helps. The right balance is: “Here is the best-known route based on currently reported barriers, here is exactly why, and here is how confident we are.”

That changes a few requirements from nice-to-have into hard rules. The route result cannot just show a badge or score. It needs three plain-language pieces every time: what is recommended, why it is recommended, and what the app does not know. For example: “Recommended because it avoids reported stairs and has fewer severe hazards. Confidence: moderate. Last nearby report: 6 days ago.” Without that third piece, the interface will look more certain than the data deserves. That is the main product risk.

I would also narrow the user more aggressively than “mobility-constrained pedestrian” in the abstract. The design target should be a wheelchair or rollator user making a short, repeated essential trip in a bounded urban corridor. That is the user whose needs best discipline scope. If the experience works for that person, it can later stretch to cane users, stroller users, and caregivers. If we design for everyone now, we will smooth over the very constraints that make the app matter.

A few scope decisions need to become explicit assumptions now so architecture does not wobble later. First, the app is pre-trip planning, not in-trip navigation. Once the route is chosen, the product’s core job is done. Second, “reroute” in MVP should mean compare available MapKit walking routes and downgrade or warn on routes intersecting known obstacles; it should not imply sophisticated custom detours around a pin if MapKit does not produce one. Third, the seeded dataset should be intentionally small and geographically coherent, not sprinkled randomly. A fake sense of breadth is worse than a clearly bounded useful zone.

There are several edge cases that matter because they are actually normal use, not corner cases. A user may deny location permission but still want to plan from a manually chosen origin. That should work. A user may be offline after previously saving favorite destinations and reports; the app should still open, show saved context, and explain that fresh routing is unavailable. A route may have no known obstacles; that should not be phrased as “accessible” or “clear,” only “no reported obstacles on this route.” A report may be old enough to be misleading; staleness has to visibly reduce confidence. A report may sit near the path but not actually block it; route impact needs a simple, explainable proximity rule, not just “same map area.” A user may submit conflicting reports on the same obstacle over time; the app needs a way to treat newer reports as more influential without pretending verification.

The privacy posture is straightforward but important. Location is sensitive enough that we should minimize retention by default. The app should store user-created reports, saved destinations, and favorites because those are core value. It should not quietly build a persistent travel diary unless that becomes an explicit feature later. If route requests are kept at all, they should be lightweight and user-visible, more like favorites/recent destinations than passive movement history. Photos are the kind of feature that sounds useful but creates disproportionate privacy and storage complexity; I would keep them out of MVP entirely.

The risk register I would preserve is short and sharp. Biggest product risk: false confidence from incomplete or stale obstacle data. Mitigation: persistent disclaimer, visible confidence/staleness language, and careful wording that distinguishes “no known reports” from “safe.” Biggest UX risk: empty-map syndrome that makes the app feel fake or broken. Mitigation: bounded starter corridor, seeded sample reports clearly labeled, and explicit empty-state copy elsewhere. Biggest scope risk: teams smuggling in live-navigation expectations. Mitigation: define success around route selection and explanation, not active guidance. Biggest monetization risk: paid tier feeling bolted on. Mitigation: keep free tier fully real, and reserve subscription for caregiver sharing plus recurring saved route intelligence, where there is an actual second stakeholder and continuing value.

The detail the final output must preserve is this: Clearpath Access is not selling accessibility certainty. It is selling better pre-trip judgment under uncertainty. If that distinction survives, the app can be honest, useful, and commercially coherent. If it gets lost, the product either becomes unsafe or generic.

**Claude (QA & Risk · Systems Thinker) — Round 1**

Picking up where the last phase left off, I want to push on the places where "we agreed on the shape" quietly hides an unresolved mechanism — because several things that sound settled (recency, matching, favorites, the seed corridor) don't actually have an algorithm or a concrete answer yet, and QA can't test a vibe.

Start with the thing I think is the biggest unstated assumption in everything decided so far: routing itself is not local-first. MKDirections is a network call to Apple's servers. Every prior round has said "local-first" as if it covers the whole app, but it only covers the data layer — reports, favorites, report history. The moment the device is offline, the core loop's first step (fetch a walking route) fails, and no amount of on-device obstacle data fixes that. This needs to be named explicitly as an accepted limitation, not discovered mid-build: offline means "no new route requests," and the app should say that plainly rather than imply routing broke because of something the obstacle layer did. If a previously-fetched route is cached in the session, obstacle scoring can still update against it offline, but a first-time destination search offline is a hard failure state that needs its own honest empty/error UI, distinct from "no obstacles nearby."

Second, the "route crosses a stored obstacle" behavior everyone's been treating as obvious actually requires a geometric decision nobody's made: an obstacle is a point, a route is a polyline, and pedestrian and phone GPS accuracy in dense areas is routinely 5-15 meters. Without a stated matching buffer, this is untestable — a report 8 meters off the path either counts or it doesn't, and that has to be a fixed number (something like a 20-25 meter corridor buffer) that the scoring tests assert against directly, not something left to "however the map looks right." Same problem applies to severity math: "friction score" has been named as the differentiator through three phases now without anyone writing down the formula. I'd propose locking something concrete now — each obstacle type carries a base severity weight, multiple obstacles on one route sum rather than take-the-worst, and a report's contribution decays with age but never hits zero, since an unconfirmed absence of a problem is not the same as a confirmed clear path. If we don't pin numbers down at this phase, the "explainable" claim becomes unfalsifiable and design will invent something ad hoc under time pressure.

Third, favorites — introduced last phase as the retention mechanic — create a subtle staleness bug if nobody decides now how they're stored. If a favorite stores the actual MKRoute/polyline from when it was saved, and MapKit's server-side routing changes its answer for that same origin/destination weeks later (which it does, nondeterministically, based on live conditions), the favorite is now scoring a route that may not be the one MapKit would currently suggest. If instead a favorite stores only origin/destination and re-fetches the route each time it's opened, then "no new issues since your last report" — the exact reassurance beat the product brief leaned on — could quietly be comparing against a different physical path than the user actually walks regularly. I think the correct answer is: favorites store endpoints and re-fetch on open, and the UI is honest that "this is today's suggested route to that destination," not a frozen path — but that's a real product decision hiding inside what looked like a done feature, and it needs to be made on purpose.

Fourth, the demo seed corridor. Using real-world coordinates for a real neighborhood and dressing them up as "starter obstacle data" is a liability landmine even fully on-device — if we pick an actual intersection near an actual clinic and claim a reported set of stairs or construction that doesn't really exist there, that's a false claim about a real place shipped in the App Store binary. This needs to be either an obviously fictional location (a made-up address/neighborhood name) or a real bounded area where the seed data is generic enough (a couple of intersections with plausible, clearly time-stamped, clearly labeled "sample" reports) that nobody could reasonably act on it as fact. I'd rather over-signal fictional than risk it reading as real civic data.

On permissions and edge cases: location-denied cannot be a dead end. If "current location" is the only way to set a trip's start point, then permission denial makes the entire app unusable, which fails gracefully but still fails completely — and it forecloses the caregiver-planning-for-someone-else use case that's already named as a future paid feature. Manual start-point entry (typed address or drop-a-pin) should be in MVP scope for this reason alone, not as a caregiver feature specifically, just as the honest fallback for permission-denied and as the seed of the multi-person planning story. I'd also flag mid-session permission changes (granted, then revoked while the app is backgrounded) as a state transition that needs a defined behavior, not just an initial-launch check.

On accessibility specifically, since this app's primary persona is mobility-constrained but not necessarily blind or low-vision — VoiceOver support has been promised twice now but nobody's said how a screen-reader user is supposed to interact with a pin on a map, which is one of the worst VoiceOver interaction patterns in iOS. That requires an actual accessible list view of the route's flagged segments and obstacles as a parallel, fully navigable representation, not just accessibility labels bolted onto map annotations — otherwise "VoiceOver can reach the warnings" from the earlier success criteria will fail in practice even if every element technically has a label.

On data sensitivity: the obstacle note field is free text, and even though this is single-user local data in v1, it's implicitly future-shareable (caregiver sharing is the named paid feature), and it's tied to health-adjacent locations (clinics, a specific person's regular walks). That's sensitive enough to warrant real on-device file protection (complete-until-first-unlock class, standard SwiftData/CoreData default) rather than treating "it's local so it's automatically safe" as sufficient reasoning — and it means the note field should ship with a short inline hint discouraging personal identifying info, since we can't retroactively scrub it once sharing exists.

Last risk I want on the register even though it's out of scope to solve now: what happens when a user reports the same obstacle twice, or reports something that later gets fixed. If there's no way to mark a report as resolved or expired, reports only ever accumulate and the friction score only ever goes up over time, which will eventually make every regular route look artificially worse than it is and erode exactly the trust the whole product is built on. I don't think this needs a full resolution workflow in MVP, but recency decay has to be doing real work here, and a "mark as no longer present" action on a user's own report is cheap enough that I'd argue it belongs in MVP scope rather than being deferred, since without it the core promise degrades on a predictable timeline.

**Gemini — Round 1 (skipped: CLI unavailable)**

_Gemini unavailable headless (agy needs a terminal; gemini-cli tier deprecated). gemini CLI -> auth/tier error_

**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 1**

Here's where things stand after round one: both sides agree completely on the big-picture framing — this app sells "better judgment under uncertainty," not certainty, every recommendation needs a what/why/confidence explanation, the seed corridor needs to be small and honest rather than sprawling, photos stay out of MVP, and location-denied can't be a dead end (manual origin entry has to work). None of that is in dispute.

But Claude's round did something Codex's round didn't do yet: it turned several things that have been treated as "already decided" for two phases into concrete, checkable mechanisms — and Codex hasn't had a chance to weigh in on any of these specific numbers or designs yet. That's not a disagreement, it's just unfinished business, and this phase's whole job is to kill exactly this kind of hidden ambiguity before design/architecture inherits it. The open items that need an actual answer (or explicit agreement to defer) next round:

- **Obstacle-to-route matching buffer** — Claude proposed a fixed ~20-25m corridor. Needs Codex's sign-off or a counter-number.
- **Friction score formula** — Claude proposed: obstacles sum (not worst-only) per route, each type has a base severity weight, age decays a report's contribution but never to zero. This has been called "the differentiator" for three phases without ever being written down — it can't stay unwritten past this phase.
- **Favorites storage semantics** — store origin/destination and re-fetch the route live each time (not a frozen polyline), with the UI honestly framed as "today's suggested route," since MapKit's answer can drift over time. This directly affects whether the "no new issues since last time" reassurance beat is even true.
- **Report lifecycle** — whether a "mark as no longer present" action on your own report ships in MVP (Claude argues yes, cheaply, or the friction score only ever ratchets upward and erodes trust on a predictable timeline).
- **Seed corridor approach** — fictional place name vs. a real bounded area with deliberately generic/labeled sample data, to avoid the app making false claims about a real intersection.
- **VoiceOver interaction model** — a parallel, fully navigable list of flagged segments/obstacles, not labels bolted onto map pins.
- **Routing itself isn't local-first** — needs to be named explicitly as an accepted limitation (offline = no new route requests, distinct error state from "no obstacles nearby"), not just implied by the data-layer being local-first.

None of these are contested — nobody's arguing against them — but they're one voice's proposals, not yet a confirmed two-way agreement, and Gemini remains unavailable to add a third view.

CONSENSUS: NO

Next round needs Codex to explicitly confirm, adjust, or counter-propose on the seven items above (buffer distance, scoring formula, favorites semantics, report lifecycle, seed corridor treatment, VoiceOver list view, and naming the offline-routing limitation), so this phase can close with real resolved mechanisms rather than restated themes.

### Round 2


**Codex (Product Strategist · User Advocate) — Round 2**

Claude is right to force the mechanism work now, because otherwise we keep congratulating ourselves for having an “explainable friction score” that nobody has actually explained. I’m aligned with most of the seven items, but I want to tune them to preserve the user experience for the specific person we keep claiming to design for: someone standing at the door trying to decide whether today’s clinic route is still worth attempting. That means every rule has to be understandable enough that the app can say, in plain English, why it made the call without sounding like a geometry paper.

So yes: name the offline limitation explicitly. “Local-first” here means reports, favorites, and prior context are local-first; new routing is not. That’s acceptable as long as the app says it cleanly: if you’re offline, you can review saved routes and reports, but you can’t fetch a new walking route right now. That’s honest and still useful. On the matching rule, I think Claude’s instinct is correct but I would lock it at a single fixed corridor, 20 meters from the route polyline, because 25 starts to feel too eager in dense downtown grids where something on the far side of a plaza or parallel sidewalk might get counted. Twenty meters is also easier to explain internally: “reported on or very near this route.” On scoring, I want something concrete but not overfit: obstacles should absolutely add up, because two medium problems often are worse than one bad one for this user, but the score we expose to the user should stay categorical rather than numeric. Internally: base type weight times severity multiplier times recency multiplier, summed across matched obstacles. Recency should decay in steps, not on a smooth curve nobody can reason about: fresh under 7 days = full weight, 8-30 days = reduced, 31-90 days = low, over 90 days = very low but not zero. That gives us testable math and language the UI can mirror: “confidence reduced because reports here are older.” I would also make newer conflicting reports on the same obstacle type in the same small area outweigh older ones rather than simply stack forever, because the product is supposed to help with changing reality, not build a permanent record of past trouble.

Where I want to be firmer than Claude is on the report lifecycle: I do think “mark as no longer present” belongs in MVP, because without it we are knowingly shipping a ratchet that only ever makes routes look worse. That is not an edge case; it will poison trust on repeated trips, which is exactly the app’s core use case. But I would keep it intentionally humble: not a community moderation system, just “update your own report” or “mark as cleared” with a new timestamp, and the app treats that as a lower-friction or resolved state for future scoring. On favorites, I agree they must store endpoints and re-fetch, not freeze a polyline. The promise is not “your historical exact path”; it’s “today’s best-known route to Dr. Patel’s office.” That is the product. The reassurance beat should also be worded carefully for that reason: not “same route still clear,” but “no new reported issues affecting today’s suggested route.” That’s honest.

I also agree with Claude on manual origin entry being MVP, full stop. Not as an expansion feature, but because without it permission denial breaks the app and caregiver planning becomes structurally impossible later. Same story on accessibility: a map alone is not an accessible interaction model. The route result needs a companion list of flagged segments and obstacles that VoiceOver can traverse in order, with text like “Construction blockage, moderate severity, reported 3 days ago, near Pine and 8th, affects this route.” That list is not just an accessibility accommodation, it’s actually better product design for everyone when they want the explanation fast. The one place I’d push back a little on Claude is the seed corridor framing: I think obviously fictional is cleaner than “real area with generic sample data.” The moment we use a real clinic corridor and ship sample obstacles there, we create ambiguity we do not need. Better to say outright that first launch includes a guided demo area with sample reports so you can see how scoring works, and keep the user’s real local value in their own reports and saved destinations. That preserves honesty instead of playing chicken with implied civic truth.

So the version I’d fight for is: this app recommends the least-friction route it can, within a 20-meter route buffer, using summed obstacle weights that decay by age in clear bands, with user-controlled report clearing, favorites that store endpoints not frozen paths, manual origin selection in scope, offline review but not offline new routing, and an accessible list view parallel to the map. Most importantly, every recommendation has to say three things every time: why this route, how fresh the evidence is, and what the app still doesn’t know. If we lock that, design and architecture have real instructions instead of a philosophy poster.

**Claude (QA & Risk · Systems Thinker) — Round 2**

Codex's round actually converges with mine more than the coordinator's list of "open items" suggests — the proximity rule Codex named ("a report may sit near the path but not actually block it; route impact needs a simple, explainable proximity rule, not just 'same map area'") is the same gap I flagged as the missing matching buffer, and "newer reports more influential without pretending verification" is the same gap I flagged as recency decay. So we're not actually disagreeing on whether these need numbers — we agree they do. Codex just hasn't put numbers on the page yet either. So let me put numbers on the page, because the coordinator's right that round two's whole job is closing that gap, not restating that a gap exists.

Matching buffer: I'll hold my ~20-25m proposal, but I want to fold in Codex's proximity-vs-blocking distinction properly — a single buffer radius that determines "counts against this route" is fine, but the plain-language explanation needs to distinguish "directly on your path" from "near your path," which just means two buffer tiers off the same distance calculation: inside ~10m reads as "on this route," 10-25m reads as "nearby, may not affect this exact path." That gives Codex's "why" text something to say beyond a binary flag, and it's still one number to tune, not a new subsystem.

Scoring formula plus confidence: I proposed severity-weighted sum with age decay that never zeroes out — I'm sticking with that, but Codex's requirement that every recommendation show a "Confidence: moderate" style line means confidence can't be a vibe either, it has to fall out of the same data. I'd define it as a function of report density and freshness along the route corridor, not of the obstacle count itself: zero reports within the buffer anywhere along the route (not just zero obstacles) means confidence is "low — limited local data," some reports but mostly old means "moderate," several reports with at least one inside 30 days means "high." That's the mechanism that actually enforces Codex's own rule that "no reported obstacles" must never be phrased as "accessible" — a route with zero data and a route with five recent clean reports both show "no reported obstacles" in the headline, but only one of them earns a high-confidence label, and the copy needs to make that distinction load-bearing, not cosmetic. Worth also locking a tie-break rule now: when two routes score identically, prefer the shorter one, so "recommended route" is a deterministic, testable output rather than an implementation detail that could flip between runs.

On report lifecycle, I want to revise my own proposal after sitting with Codex's "conflicting reports over time, newer more influential" point, because I think it hands us a cleaner answer than a dedicated "mark resolved" button. Instead of a separate resolution workflow, let a new report submitted inside the same matching buffer as an existing obstacle simply supersede it in the score — weighted toward the newer report — while keeping the old one in history for audit/undo. That reuses the exact reporting flow that already exists, costs nothing extra in UI, and gives users a natural way to say "this got fixed" by just... reporting what's there now, including a lightweight "looks clear now" option in the same six-item flow rather than bolting on a seventh action. That's a real answer to the "friction score only ratchets upward" risk I raised last round, and I think it's better than what I originally proposed.

Favorites: no change, I think Codex's "reroute means comparing MapKit's own alternatives, not inventing detours" point actually reinforces my favorites answer rather than being separate from it — store endpoints, re-fetch live, be honest that it's "today's suggested route." If favorites stored a frozen polyline, the "no new issues since last time" reassurance would eventually be comparing against a phantom path MapKit wouldn't even offer anymore, which is a worse trust failure than anything the obstacle data itself could cause.

Seed corridor — this is the one place I want to actually push back on my own instinct from last round. I said "fictional or clearly labeled real," and thinking about it more, a fully fictional place name (made-up neighborhood, made-up streets) risks making the whole demo experience feel like a game board rather than a serious tool, which cuts against the "premium, not civic-tech prototype" bar from the prompt contract. I think the better answer is: invent specific street/corridor names that don't map to any real address, but ground them in a real, named city so the app doesn't feel like it's set in nowhere — something like "Sample data for the Elm Street corridor (demo)" where Elm Street doesn't actually exist in that city. That gets deniability against the liability problem (nobody can point to a real intersection and say "you claimed stairs here that don't exist") while still feeling like a real place a real user would recognize the shape of.

VoiceOver — confirming the parallel list view, and adding one specificity: it has to be ordered by progression along the route (first flagged segment you'd reach, second, etc.), not by severity or alphabetically, because the whole point for this user is "what am I going to hit and in what order," and a severity-sorted list would silently reorder the one piece of information — sequence — that a blind wheelchair user actually needs walking the route.

And I'll just restate the offline-routing limitation plainly since nobody's contested it: it needs its own distinct copy, something like "can't fetch a new route right now — check your connection; your saved routes and reports are still here," so it never gets confused with "no obstacles nearby," which is a completely different and much more reassuring statement. Those two empty-ish states look similar on a blank map and they mean opposite things about how much the user should trust what they're seeing.

**Gemini — Round 2 (skipped: CLI unavailable)**

_Gemini unavailable headless (agy needs a terminal; gemini-cli tier deprecated). gemini CLI -> auth/tier error_

**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 2**

Both voices spent round two doing exactly what needed to happen — turning "we agree this needs a number" into actual numbers — and by the end there's barely daylight between them. Codex signed off on nearly everything Claude proposed (favorites as endpoints-not-frozen-path, manual origin entry, the accessible list view, summed severity scoring with banded decay, offline-review-not-offline-routing), and where Codex pushed back, Claude either folded the pushback in cleanly (the proximity-vs-blocking distinction became a two-tier read on the same buffer number) or genuinely changed its own mind after hearing Codex's point (dropping a dedicated "mark resolved" button in favor of letting a fresh report in the same spot supersede the old one, which is actually a better answer to the "score only ratchets upward" problem than either of them had going in). The seed-corridor question also resolved for real: Claude talked itself out of a fully fictional place (too game-board, undercuts the premium bar) and landed on Codex's underlying worry from a different angle — invented street names inside a real, named city, so it reads as a real place without ever being able to falsely implicate one.

The only loose thread is a genuinely small one: Codex wants the obstacle-matching buffer capped at a flat 20 meters, Claude wants a two-tier version (inside ~10m reads "on this route," 10-25m reads "nearby"). That's not a real fight — Codex's objection was specifically that 25m felt "too eager" in dense grids, and Claude's inner tier doesn't touch that; you can keep Codex's 20m outer cap while keeping Claude's inner 10m sub-threshold for the "on path" vs. "nearby" language. That resolves it without another round.

CONSENSUS: YES

## Final Output

**Resolved requirements:**
- Every route recommendation shows three things every time: what's recommended, why (in plain language), and what the app doesn't know (staleness/confidence) — e.g. "Recommended because it avoids reported stairs and has fewer severe hazards. Confidence: moderate. Nearest report: 6 days ago."
- **Obstacle-to-route matching:** a fixed 20-meter buffer around the route polyline determines whether an obstacle counts against that route at all. Within that, a report inside ~10m reads as "on this route"; 10–20m reads as "nearby, may not affect this exact path." One buffer, one inner sub-threshold, both numbers fixed so scoring tests can assert against them directly.
- **Friction score formula:** for each obstacle matched within the buffer, score = base type weight × severity multiplier × recency multiplier; matched obstacles sum per route (not worst-only). Recency decays in fixed bands, not a smooth curve: <7 days = full weight, 8–30 days = reduced, 31–90 days = low, >90 days = very low but never zero. The score shown to the user is categorical (low/moderate friction language), not a raw number.
- **Confidence** is a separate signal from the friction score, derived from report density and freshness along the route corridor, not from obstacle count: zero reports anywhere in the buffer = "low confidence — limited local data"; some reports but mostly old = "moderate"; several reports with at least one under 30 days = "high." This is what stops "no reported obstacles" from ever being misread as "safe" — a zero-data route and a five-clean-recent-report route both show the same headline but different confidence labels.
- **Tie-break rule:** when two routes score identically on friction, the shorter route wins — a deterministic, testable pick rather than incidental behavior.
- **Report lifecycle:** no separate "mark resolved" workflow. A new report submitted within the same matching buffer as an existing obstacle supersedes it in scoring, weighted toward the newer report, with the old one retained in history. The existing report flow gets one added lightweight option ("looks clear now") rather than a new UI surface. This is the mechanism that keeps the friction score from ratcheting upward forever.
- **Favorites** store origin/destination only and re-fetch the route live on open — never a frozen polyline. UI language is honest about this: "today's suggested route to Dr. Patel's office," and the reassurance copy is "no new reported issues affecting today's suggested route," not "same route still clear."
- **Manual origin entry** (typed address or dropped pin) is in MVP scope — required for permission-denied to degrade gracefully rather than dead-end, and it's the seed of later caregiver/multi-person planning.
- **Accessible list view:** a parallel, fully VoiceOver-navigable list of flagged segments/obstacles alongside the map, ordered by progression along the route (first thing you'd reach, then next), not by severity or alphabetically — sequence is the information this user actually needs.
- **Seed/demo data:** invented street and corridor names (that map to no real address) set inside a real, named city — e.g. "Sample data for the Elm Street corridor (demo)" where Elm Street doesn't exist there — so the demo feels like a real place without ever being able to falsely implicate an actual intersection.
- **Offline behavior named explicitly:** local-first covers reports, favorites, and history — not new routing, since MKDirections is a network call. Offline gives you your saved routes/reports plus a plain, distinct message ("can't fetch a new route right now — check your connection; your saved routes and reports are still here") that is never confused with "no obstacles nearby," which means something very different.

**Edge cases (named, with behavior defined):**
- Location permission denied → manual origin entry, not a dead end.
- Permission granted then revoked mid-session/backgrounded → re-check and fall back to manual entry gracefully, don't crash or silently stop updating.
- Offline on first-time destination search → hard, honestly-labeled failure state, distinct from "no nearby obstacles."
- Offline with a previously-fetched route still in session → obstacle scoring can still update against that cached route.
- Route with zero matched obstacles → labeled "no reported obstacles on this route," never "accessible" or "clear," and confidence is shown separately so an empty-data area doesn't read as a verified-safe one.
- Conflicting/updated reports on the same obstacle → newer supersedes older via the buffer-match rule above, old retained in history.
- Two routes scoring identically → shorter wins, deterministically.

**Data and privacy implications:**
- No photo capture in MVP (confirmed, unanimous, unchanged from prior phases).
- No passive travel-history/diary logging — only user-created reports, favorites, and saved destinations are persisted; route requests themselves aren't turned into a hidden history log.
- Obstacle note field is free text, on-device, but implicitly future-shareable (caregiver sharing is the named paid feature) and tied to health-adjacent locations — ships with standard complete-until-first-unlock file protection and a short inline hint discouraging personal/identifying info in notes, since it can't be retroactively scrubbed once sharing exists.

**Risk register:**
- *False confidence from incomplete/stale data* → mitigated by the mandatory what/why/confidence triad and the banded recency decay; named as the single biggest product risk.
- *Empty-map/vaporware feel* → mitigated by the bounded, clearly-labeled fictional-street demo corridor plus honest "no reports yet — be the first" copy elsewhere.
- *Friction score ratcheting upward forever* → mitigated by the supersession-on-new-report mechanism.
- *Favorites drifting from what MapKit would actually suggest* → mitigated by re-fetching live from stored endpoints rather than freezing a polyline.
- *VoiceOver map-pin dead ends* → mitigated by the sequence-ordered parallel list view.
- *Liability from real-world obstacle claims in seed data* → mitigated by fictional street names inside a real city, never a real intersection.
- *Scope creep into live navigation/turn-by-turn* → stays out per prior-phase non-goals; unchanged and reconfirmed here.

**Final assumptions carried into design/architecture:**
- Route matching buffer: 20m outer / 10m inner tier, fixed constants the scoring engine and its tests are built against.
- Scoring: multiplicative per-obstacle weight (type × severity × recency band), summed per route; confidence computed separately from density/freshness, not obstacle count.
- Favorites are live-re-fetching endpoint pairs, not frozen routes.
- Report "clearing" reuses the existing report-submission flow via buffer-based supersession, not a new UI mechanism.
- Manual origin entry and the accessible list view are both MVP-scope, non-negotiable, not stretch goals.
- Demo/seed data lives in a fictional corridor inside a real named city.
- Local-first is scoped to the data layer; live routing has a named, honest offline limitation with its own distinct UI state.

**Quality Gate (Claude) — after round 2**

QUALITY: PASS

## Feedback

This closes the phase properly. All five required sections (resolved requirements, edge cases, data/privacy implications, risk register, final assumptions) are present and, critically, every item the round-1 coordinator flagged as "unresolved mechanism" — matching buffer, friction formula, confidence signal, tie-break rule, report lifecycle, favorites semantics, seed-corridor treatment, VoiceOver list ordering, offline-routing limitation — now has a concrete, numeric, testable answer rather than a restated theme.

Strengths:
- The buffer resolution (20m outer / 10m inner) is a real synthesis, not a coin-flip: it keeps Codex's objection (25m too eager in dense grids) while preserving Claude's on-path/nearby language distinction, and both agents' round-2 text supports this reading.
- Confidence is explicitly decoupled from the friction score (density/freshness vs. obstacle count) — this directly operationalizes the "no reported obstacles ≠ safe" requirement carried from earlier phases rather than leaving it as a slogan.
- The report-lifecycle answer (supersession-by-new-report instead of a bolted-on "mark resolved" button) is a genuine resolution of the "score only ratchets upward" risk, reusing an existing flow rather than adding UI surface — good scope discipline consistent with prior-phase decision rules.
- Favorites-as-live-refetch, manual origin entry, and the sequence-ordered accessible list view all directly inherit and sharpen commitments from earlier phases (no contradictions found against prompt_contract, initial_discussion, or per_app_product_brief).
- The seed-corridor decision (invented street names inside a real named city) resolves the liability concern without collapsing into a "fictional game board," which was explicitly weighed against the "premium, not civic-tech prototype" bar.
- Risk register entries all have named concrete mitigations, satisfying the acceptance check.

No papered-over disagreement remains — the one open delta (flat 20m vs. two-tier) was correctly identified as reconcilable rather than contested, and the reconciliation is logically sound (one number nests inside the other). Ready to hand to design/architecture.

## Coordinator Decision

See the coordinator's message above.

## Final Output

Both voices spent round two doing exactly what needed to happen — turning "we agree this needs a number" into actual numbers — and by the end there's barely daylight between them. Codex signed off on nearly everything Claude proposed (favorites as endpoints-not-frozen-path, manual origin entry, the accessible list view, summed severity scoring with banded decay, offline-review-not-offline-routing), and where Codex pushed back, Claude either folded the pushback in cleanly (the proximity-vs-blocking distinction became a two-tier read on the same buffer number) or genuinely changed its own mind after hearing Codex's point (dropping a dedicated "mark resolved" button in favor of letting a fresh report in the same spot supersede the old one, which is actually a better answer to the "score only ratchets upward" problem than either of them had going in). The seed-corridor question also resolved for real: Claude talked itself out of a fully fictional place (too game-board, undercuts the premium bar) and landed on Codex's underlying worry from a different angle — invented street names inside a real, named city, so it reads as a real place without ever being able to falsely implicate one.

phase_discussions.title: App Features
phase_discussions.output:
CONSENSUS: YES

This round actually closed the loop. Codex came back and explicitly signed off on all three things the coordinator flagged: the ShareLink-not-push shape for caregiver value, the 2-favorite free cap (with the caveat that it only works if free still feels genuinely useful, not crippled), and treating the boundary-value tests (19m/21m, day 6/8/30/31/90/91) as real acceptance criteria for this phase rather than something to hand-wave as "build-phase testing." Claude then pushed on one more thing — that Codex's won't-build list still had "caregiver sharing itself... stays as the monetization path" sitting there unchanged, which contradicts what Codex had just agreed to two paragraphs earlier (a defined ShareLink export shipping as MVP scope). That's not really a new disagreement, it's Codex's own position catching up with its own wording — Codex's round 2 already describes the ShareLink feature as something "the MVP should share," which only makes sense if it's a must-have, not a deferred non-goal. Claude's other addition — a must-have for the single-route case, where MapKit only returns one walking route and the "why this route beats the alternative" framing has to honestly downgrade to "the only route found" instead of a comparison it can't support — is a small, logical gap-fill on top of Codex's own scoring/comparison requirement, not a contested new mechanism. Nothing here pulls in opposite directions; it's four loose ends getting tied off.

## Final Output

**Must-have features:**

1. **Route planning from current location or manual origin to a searched destination.**
   User story: as a user who may deny location permission or plan for someone else, I need to set both ends of a trip and still get a usable route recommendation.
   Acceptance criteria: current-location or manual/dropped-pin origin both work; destination search/selection works; permission-denied does not dead-end; at least one route renders on success; offline or routing failure produces a distinct, honest error state (never confused with "no obstacles nearby").

2. **Accessibility-friction route scoring across returned walking routes.**
   User story: as a mobility-constrained pedestrian, I need the app to tell me which available route is least risky based on known reported barriers, not just which is shortest.
   Acceptance criteria: scoring uses the locked 20m/10m buffer tiers, type × severity × recency-band formula summed per route, and the shorter-route tie-break; a route with zero matched obstacles is labeled "no reported obstacles," never "safe" or "accessible." Boundary-value tests are explicit and must-pass, not left to interpretation: an obstacle at 19m counts, at 21m does not; at 9m reads "on this route," at 11m reads "nearby"; recency bands are asserted at the exact edges — day 6 vs. 8, day 30 vs. 31, day 90 vs. 91 must land in different decay bands.

3. **Plain-language route explanation with explicit confidence.**
   User story: as someone making a real mobility decision, I need to understand why a route is recommended and how complete the evidence is.
   Acceptance criteria: every result shows what's recommended, why, and confidence/staleness; confidence is derived from report density/freshness, independent of obstacle count; low-data corridors are visibly labeled low-confidence even with zero flagged obstacles.

4. **Single-route-result honesty.**
   User story: as a user in an area where MapKit only returns one walking route, I still need an honest explanation, not a comparison the app can't back up.
   Acceptance criteria: when MapKit returns exactly one walking route, the app still shows the full what/why/confidence triad, but the "why" language is adapted to something like "the only walking route found" rather than implying a comparison against alternatives that don't exist; this case has its own test, not an assumption that multi-route responses are typical.

5. **Obstacle reporting with immediate local effect, including supersession.**
   User story: as a user who hits a barrier or sees one has cleared, I need to record it fast and see the app reflect it right away.
   Acceptance criteria: report creation (type, severity, optional note, "looks clear now" where relevant) completes in under 30 seconds; a report submitted in-session changes the score of a route re-requested immediately after, no restart; a newer report inside an existing obstacle's buffer supersedes the old one in scoring (weighted toward newer), with history retained, verified by an explicit before/after test on the same buffer.

6. **Local persistence for reports, favorites, and saved destinations.**
   User story: as a repeat user with a handful of recurring walks, I need my reports and one-tap routes to survive relaunching the app.
   Acceptance criteria: reports, favorites, and saved endpoints survive an actual force-quit and relaunch (not just trusted at the framework level); favorites store origin/destination only and re-fetch live on open, with UI copy honest that it's "today's suggested route," never a frozen path.

7. **Accessible route details list alongside the map.**
   User story: as a VoiceOver user, or anyone who wants fast clarity, I need a linear list of route issues in travel order instead of interpreting map pins.
   Acceptance criteria: flagged items appear in a list ordered by progression along the route (not severity, not alphabetical); a manual VoiceOver pass reaches route summary, every flagged item, and the report action with zero dead ends; Dynamic Type at the largest accessibility size shows no truncation on any core screen.

8. **Seeded demo corridor plus honest empty states.**
   User story: as a first-time user, I need to see how the product works without the app pretending it has broad real coverage.
   Acceptance criteria: first launch includes clearly labeled sample reports in a fictional corridor (invented street names) inside a real named city; areas without reports say so plainly ("no reports yet — be the first"); the UI never implies live community coverage.

9. **Persistent safety/trust disclosure.**
   User story: as someone relying on this for a physical trip, I need a constant reminder that data is user-submitted and unverified.
   Acceptance criteria: disclaimer shown in onboarding and reachable at any time from the route result screen (footer/info affordance); it can be dismissed as an onboarding step but never fully suppressed from the app.

10. **Caregiver share export (ShareLink, not live sync).**
    User story: as a user who wants a family member or aide to see my planned route, I need to share it without either of us needing an account.
    Acceptance criteria: native `ShareLink` action generates a timestamped, self-contained snapshot (destination, recommendation, confidence/staleness, unverified-data disclaimer) as text/file content; opening it requires no account, no network call, no push infrastructure; this is explicitly not live-updating — no "notify me when this changes" mechanism ships in this MVP.

11. **Two-favorite free cap with StoreKit purchase/restore.**
    User story: as a free user, I should get real value from a couple of saved routes; as a paying user, I should get the recurring-trip and sharing value that matches how I actually use the app.
    Acceptance criteria: free tier allows up to 2 saved favorites; attempting a 3rd triggers an upsell screen, not a silent failure or crash; purchase unlocks unlimited favorites plus the ShareLink export; a working StoreKit purchase and restore flow gates exactly those two things — routing, scoring, and reporting remain free and ungated.

12. **Domain-logic unit tests independent of UI.**
    User story: as the team shipping the app's one truly verifiable subsystem, I need the scoring/obstacle-impact logic tested without relying on a human eyeballing a map.
    Acceptance criteria: the boundary-value tests from item 2, the supersession test from item 5, and the single-route case from item 4 all exist as automated tests, not just "coverage" in the abstract.

**Should-have:**
- One-tap save of a route as a named favorite immediately after viewing results.
- Recent destinations for faster repeat planning.
- Inline guidance discouraging personal/identifying info in obstacle notes.

**Could-have:**
- Rider profile (wheelchair/cane/rollator) reweighting — explicitly not must-have, since the locked scoring model is universal, not profile-based, and reopening it now would unwind tested math for a feature that was never actually spec'd.
- Browsable history view of superseded reports (data model already retains them; a UI to browse is extra surface).
- Lightweight onboarding walkthrough using the seeded corridor to teach "recommended / why / confidence."
- Small trend cues in favorites ("last updated 6 days ago").
- Downloadable curated coverage packs (already flagged in the product brief as secondary, not anchor).
- Haptic/visual emphasis for severe on-route hazards.

**Won't-build (explicit, not implied):**
- Any live sync, push notification, or backend-dependent caregiver feature — caregiver value in this MVP is the ShareLink export only; live "your mom's route just changed" notifications are out.
- Turn-by-turn voice guidance or live GPS tracking during the walk.
- Real multi-user sync, moderation, or actual crowd/community data presence.
- Photo capture, camera-based detection, or ML features.
- Custom pedestrian graph, curb-cut intelligence, or grade modeling.
- Multi-stop itineraries, trip history dashboards, transit integration, or discovery/social feed features.
- Verified partner/curated data layer (secondary paid idea for later, not this build).

**Biggest risk carried forward:** the app has more trust states than feature states — permission-denied, low-confidence, zero-matched-obstacle, offline-new-route-failure, single-route-result, stale/superseded reports, seeded-vs-real data. If these collapse into generic empty/error screens, the product misleads even when the underlying math is correct. The core output of this app is a recommendation sentence with inspectable evidence and explicit uncertainty, not "a route on a map" — that has to survive into design and build untouched.
phase_discussions.discussion_markdown:
# clearpath-access — App Features

_Generated by the autonomous multi-agent orchestrator on 2026-07-07 01:49:50._

## Original Prompt

```
PORTFOLIO_CHILD_PROJECT: true
Parent portfolio: batch-cat-b1
Selected app slug: clearpath-access

Build this app as a working local-first SwiftUI MVP.

## Selected App Spec

# Clearpath Access

Build mode: **MVP build**.

## App Name

Clearpath Access

## Category

Navigation

## One-Sentence Promise

Accessibility-aware pedestrian routing with community-sourced obstacle reporting

## Claude Design Handoff Prompt

(not supplied yet)

## Parent Portfolio Prompt

Build 9 completely separate production-ready iOS apps at the same time: exactly 3 Health & Fitness apps, 3 Medical apps, and 3 Navigation apps.

This is a multi-app PORTFOLIO request: multiple apps in one program. Each selected app becomes its own separate project — one folder per app.


Requirements for every app:
1. Must go from 0 to production-ready, not just a few features.
2. Must be unique, useful, and commercially viable.
3. Must be beautifully designed and feel premium.
4. Must provide real end-user value.
5. Must have a realistic path to monetization — a subscription or paid offering with genuine value (never cosmetics-only).
6. Should have viral potential (broad or niche) without sacrificing usefulness.
7. Must be better than its competitors in a meaningful way.
8. Local-first where applicable, architected so cloud support can be added later without rewriting.

UNIQUENESS (hard rule, non-negotiable):
- No app in this batch may be similar to another app in this batch.
- No app may be similar to anything already built or being built in this workspace or before, including: Waylay (location-based personal recall utility), TrueScale, Provenance, Scope-Guard, Practice-Loop, Proof, ReturnWise, VerveCoach, CueKeeper, Countertop, FieldQuote, my existing digital temple app, and my existing PO-automation tool.
- No app may be similar to apps in the OTHER batches of this program (check sibling project folders in the workspace for their concepts).
- Do not reuse prior concepts, themes, or mechanics from earlier apps — even if the request sounds similar to something built before, produce a differentiated concept.

Bonus points (optional — only when integral and genuinely valuable, never forced): unique use of LLMs, AR, or ML.

Build rules:
- Run the app efforts in parallel; keep discussion/design phases separate per app.
- Each app gets its own full product direction, design direction, architecture, implementation plan, and build output.
- Save all phase discussions and final decisions; at the end combine each app's full transcript into a .txt file.
- If an app is liked, also prepare Jira board + Notion project structures for backfilling later.
- Use enough rounds per phase for high-quality discussion, ending early on consensus.
- Recover cleanly from stalls; report only important milestones.

Output:
- One folder per app in the output directory — never one folder containing all apps.
Output directory:
- /Users/pchordia/Documents/iOS-App-Factory

Additional notes:
Medical/health apps must frame outputs as wellness insights and validated risk scores (e.g. QRISK3, FINDRISC style), never diagnosis.

Now generate at least 18 candidate ideas, discuss them thoroughly, choose the best 9, and build all 9 to production quality.
```

## Phase Purpose

Enumerate and prioritize the full feature set with an explicit scoring / MoSCoW model and clear in-scope vs out-of-scope lines.

## Transcript


### Round 1


**Codex (Design Lead · Systems Thinker) — Round 1**

The MVP has to be prioritized around one complete, trustworthy pre-trip loop, not around “map app completeness.” If I were cutting this for build reality, the must-have set is small but strict: route request with manual or current-location origin, obstacle-aware comparison of MapKit walking routes, explainable recommendation with confidence language, fast obstacle reporting that immediately affects scoring, persistence for reports and favorites, and an accessible inspection surface that does not depend on map pins. Anything outside that core loop risks producing a wider but less honest app.

Must-have features:
- Route planning from either current location or a manually entered/dropped-pin origin to a searched destination.
  User story: as a user who may deny location permission or plan for someone else, I need to set both ends of a trip and still get a usable route recommendation.
  Acceptance criteria: I can choose current location or manual origin; I can search/select a destination; if permission is denied, the app still supports planning; if routing succeeds, at least one route renders and can be inspected; if routing fails or I am offline, I get a distinct, honest error state.
- Accessibility-friction route scoring across returned walking routes.
  User story: as a mobility-constrained pedestrian, I need the app to tell me which available route is least risky based on known reported barriers, not just which is shortest.
  Acceptance criteria: the app evaluates returned routes against the fixed 20m/10m buffer rules, applies the agreed obstacle scoring model, and deterministically recommends a route; if scores tie, the shorter route wins; a route with no matched obstacles is not labeled “safe” or “accessible.”
- Plain-language route explanation with explicit confidence.
  User story: as a user making a real-world mobility decision, I need to understand why a route is recommended and how incomplete the evidence may be.
  Acceptance criteria: every route result shows what is recommended, why, and confidence/staleness; the language distinguishes “on this route” from “nearby”; low-data corridors are visibly labeled low confidence; offline routing failure is never confused with “no obstacles nearby.”
- Obstacle reporting with immediate local effect.
  User story: as a user who encounters a barrier or sees that one has cleared, I need to record it quickly and have the app reflect it right away.
  Acceptance criteria: I can create a report with obstacle type, severity, optional note, and “looks clear now” where relevant in under 30 seconds; saving a report updates route scoring on the next request in the same session; a newer report in the same area supersedes older scoring influence without deleting history.
- Local persistence for reports, favorites, and saved destinations.
  User story: as a repeat user with a small set of recurring walks, I need the app to remember my reports and one-tap routes across launches.
  Acceptance criteria: reports, favorites, and saved endpoints survive force-quit and relaunch; favorites store endpoints, not frozen paths; reopening a favorite fetches today’s suggested route and uses honest copy about that.
- Accessible route details list alongside the map.
  User story: as a VoiceOver user or anyone who wants quick clarity, I need a linear list of route issues in travel order instead of having to interpret pins.
  Acceptance criteria: every flagged route can be inspected in a list ordered by progression along the route; VoiceOver can reach route summary, warnings, and reporting actions without dead ends; the core loop works without relying on direct pin manipulation.
- Seeded demo corridor plus honest empty states.
  User story: as a first-time user, I need to see how the product works without the app pretending it has broad real coverage.
  Acceptance criteria: first launch includes clearly labeled sample reports in a fictional corridor inside a real city; areas without reports say so plainly; the UI never implies live community coverage or verified citywide data.
- Persistent safety/trust disclosure.
  User story: as a user relying on this for a physical trip, I need to be reminded that data is user-submitted and unverified.
  Acceptance criteria: the disclaimer is visible in onboarding and route/reporting surfaces; route copy never overclaims certainty.

Should-have features:
- One-tap save of a route as a named favorite immediately after viewing results.
- Filter or profile tuning that changes route interpretation lightly for wheelchair vs walker/stroller needs, if it can be done without expanding taxonomy or UI complexity too much.
- Recent destinations for faster repeat planning.
- Inline note guidance discouraging personal information in obstacle notes.

Could-have features:
- Lightweight onboarding that teaches “recommended / why / confidence” with the seeded corridor.
- Small trend cues like “newer report nearby” or “last updated 6 days ago” in favorites.
- Haptic or visual emphasis for severe on-route hazards.

Won’t-build in this MVP:
- Turn-by-turn navigation or live trip tracking.
- Real multi-user sync, moderation, or actual crowd presence.
- Photo capture, camera-based detection, or ML features.
- Custom pedestrian graph, curb-cut intelligence, grade modeling, or authoritative accessibility claims.
- Multi-stop routing, trip history dashboards, transit, or discovery/social feed features.
- Verified partner data layer or caregiver sharing itself; those stay as the monetization path, not build-phase scope.

The biggest risk in this direction is that the feature list still looks deceptively small while the UX/state surface is not small at all. This app has more trust states than feature states: permission denied, low confidence, zero matched obstacles, offline-new-route failure, stale reports, superseded reports, seeded demo data, real local data. If those collapse into generic empty/error screens, the product becomes misleading even if the routing math is correct. The detail that absolutely has to survive consensus is that the app’s main output is not “a route on a map.” It is a recommendation sentence with inspectable evidence and explicit uncertainty, plus a non-map fallback path through the same information.

**Claude (QA & Risk · Closer) — Round 1**

The thing I want to force a decision on before anything else gets prioritized is a contradiction sitting between two already-locked outputs, because it changes which column half the feature list lands in. The prompt contract locked "no live multi-user sync, real backend, or cloud moderation in this MVP" as a non-goal. The product brief phase locked caregiver/family route sharing — where a second person "gets notified when a saved route's obstacle picture changes" — as the actual subscription anchor. Those two things cannot both be true. Live change-notification across two people's devices is multi-user sync; there's no way to build it without an account layer, a backend, and push infrastructure, all of which we already agreed not to build in this pass. If I don't resolve this now, the must-have list either quietly ships a fake "notify caregiver" toggle that doesn't actually notify anyone, or someone tries to sneak a CloudKit backend into an MVP build and blows the schedule and the non-goal at the same time.

My resolution: the paid tier's caregiver value ships as a one-tap export, not a live subscription-to-changes. A favorite route (or a fresh route result) gets a native ShareLink action that generates a self-contained snapshot — destination, the plain-language recommendation, the confidence label, the unverified-data disclaimer — as shareable text/file content. The caregiver opens it with no account, no network, no app-to-app sync. That's still a real second-stakeholder feature, still something Apple Maps has no reason to build, and it's buildable in an MVP without touching the non-goal. Live "your mom's route just got a new obstacle report" push notifications become an explicit won't-build for this pass, named on the roadmap, not smuggled in half-working. If whoever owns monetization wants to fight for the live version instead, that's a real scope fight worth having explicitly, but I'm not letting it stay implicit going into build.

Given that, here's where I'd actually draw the must/should/won't lines, and I want acceptance criteria concrete enough that build-verification isn't guessing later.

Must-have: onboarding that shows the unverified-data disclaimer once but also surfaces it persistently afterward (footer or info affordance on every route result screen, not just a screen a user taps through once and forgets) — acceptance is that the disclaimer text is reachable from the route result screen at any time, and denying it isn't possible, only dismissing the onboarding instance of it. Must-have: manual origin entry (typed address or dropped pin) as a first-class path, not a fallback bolted on later — acceptance is that a fresh install with location permission denied can still complete a full route request end to end using a manually placed pin, with zero dead-end screens. Must-have: the scoring engine exactly as pinned in the prior phase — 20m/10m buffer tiers, type×severity×recency-band formula summed per route, confidence computed from density/freshness separately from obstacle count, shorter-route tie-break — and this one I want tested at the boundary, not just the happy path: an obstacle at 19m must count, at 21m must not, at 9m must read "on this route," at 11m must read "nearby," and reports at day 6 vs 8, day 30 vs 31, day 90 vs 91 must land in different decay bands in the unit tests. If those exact boundary assertions aren't in the test suite, "explainable scoring" is a slogan, not a shipped feature. Must-have: the report flow including the "looks clear now" supersession option, verified specifically by submitting a second report inside an existing obstacle's buffer and confirming the score changes on an immediate re-request in the same session — this is the single most important regression test in the whole app because it's the exact loop every earlier phase called the core promise. Must-have: report persistence surviving force-quit, verified literally by killing the app and relaunching, not just trusting SwiftData. Must-have: the sequence-ordered accessible list view as a first-class, independently navigable surface — acceptance is a manual VoiceOver pass that reaches route summary, every flagged item in walking order, and the report action, with zero dead ends, not just accessibility labels on map pins. Must-have: Dynamic Type verified at the largest accessibility size on every core screen with no truncation. Must-have: the seeded fictional-corridor demo data present and clearly labeled on first launch so a fresh install isn't an empty map. Must-have: every named offline/error/empty state from the prior phase (no permission, no route found, no obstacles nearby, geocoding failure, offline-new-search vs offline-cached-route) rendered with visibly distinct copy — acceptance is literally toggling airplane mode and location permission mid-session and confirming none of these states collapse into a generic error screen or a blank one. Must-have: favorites that re-fetch live rather than freeze a polyline, capped at two free saves with the third triggering an upsell rather than a silent failure or a crash, paid unlocking unlimited plus the ShareLink export. Must-have: a working StoreKit purchase and restore flow gating exactly those two things (favorite cap, share export) — nothing else in the free loop gets paywalled, because routing, scoring, and reporting have to stay genuinely free or the "utility, not a subscription" framing from the product brief becomes a lie. Must-have: real unit tests on the scoring/obstacle-impact domain logic, independent of UI, since that's the one piece of this app that's actually verifiable without a human eyeballing a map.

Should/could, not must: a rider profile (wheelchair vs. cane vs. rollator) that reweights severity per obstacle type was floated in the very first round of this whole project and then quietly never resolved into a mechanism — I want to explicitly bucket it as a could, not a must, since the locked scoring model from the detailed-discussion phase is a single universal weighting, not profile-based, and reopening that now would unwind tested, agreed-on math for a feature that was never actually spec'd. Also should/could: a browsable history view of superseded reports (the data model already retains them, but a UI to browse that history is extra surface, not core loop), and downloadable curated coverage packs, which the product brief itself already flagged as secondary, not anchor.

Won't-build, explicitly: any live sync, push notification, or backend-dependent caregiver feature — this is the direct resolution of the contradiction above, and it needs to be written down as a won't, not left to be rediscovered as "oh, we can't actually do that" mid-build. Also won't-build: turn-by-turn voice guidance, live GPS tracking during the walk, camera/ML obstacle detection, custom curb-cut-aware routing, multi-stop itineraries, and any moderation/verification workflow for reports beyond the buffer-based supersession already locked. All of these were non-goals before this phase started; I'm not reopening them, just making sure the feature list doesn't accidentally imply one of them through vague wording like "smart notifications" or "verified reports."

The detail I most want preserved past this phase: the caregiver-share-export decision is a real, deliberate de-scoping of what the product brief originally promised, not a quiet downgrade — if that gets flattened back into "caregiver sharing" without the ShareLink-not-push distinction spelled out, design or architecture will reasonably assume live sync is in scope and we'll have this exact fight again two phases from now with less room to fix it.

**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 1**

CONSENSUS: NO

Here's where things actually stand: Codex laid out a full must/should/could/won't list built directly on everything locked in earlier phases — the core loop, the buffer/scoring numbers, the accessible list view, the seeded corridor — and that framing part isn't contested. But Claude's round did something more than restate it: it caught a real contradiction sitting between two previously-locked decisions. The prompt contract says no live sync/backend/cloud moderation in this MVP; the product brief named caregiver notifications ("gets notified when a saved route's obstacle picture changes") as the actual subscription anchor. You can't have live cross-device notifications without exactly the sync/backend infrastructure that was ruled out. That's not a rehash — it's a genuine catch that Codex's list didn't address, because Codex's "won't-build" section listed "caregiver sharing itself" as deferred without noticing it conflicts with what monetization already promised.

Claude's proposed fix is concrete: ship the caregiver feature as a one-tap ShareLink export (a static snapshot of the route/recommendation/confidence/disclaimer, no account, no network, no push) rather than live sync, and write "no live notifications" down explicitly as a won't-build so it doesn't get silently assumed back into scope later. On top of that, Claude added specific, testable boundary criteria (obstacle at 19m vs 21m, day 6 vs 8, etc.), a concrete paywall shape (2 free favorites, third triggers upsell, ShareLink gated behind purchase, everything else in the core loop free), and moved the "rider profile" idea (floated way back in round 1 of the whole project and never resolved) explicitly into could-have rather than must-have, since the locked scoring model is universal, not profile-based.

None of this contradicts Codex — it's a sharper, more specific version sitting on top of Codex's list. But Codex hasn't had a chance to confirm or push back on the ShareLink-not-push resolution, the specific paywall numbers, or the boundary-test acceptance criteria yet, and given how much weight the caregiver-monetization fix carries (it changes what "must-have" actually means for that feature), this needs an explicit round-2 sign-off rather than being waved through. Next round: Codex should confirm (or contest) the ShareLink export as the caregiver feature's build-phase shape, the free-tier paywall cap (2 favorites), and whether the boundary-value test list belongs in this phase's acceptance criteria or is a build-phase implementation detail.

### Round 2


**Codex (Design Lead · Systems Thinker) — Round 2**

Claude’s contradiction catch is right, and the clean resolution is to lock caregiver value to a snapshot-style `ShareLink`, not anything that reads as live sync. The MVP should share “today’s route summary” as a timestamped artifact with recommendation, confidence/staleness, and the unverified-data disclaimer. If we just say “caregiver sharing” without that qualifier, design will drift back toward synced state and push notifications, which the non-goals already ruled out.

I’d sign off on the `2` free favorites cap, but only with careful framing. This user likely has `3–4` recurring routes, so `2` is a real pressure point, not a neutral limit. That’s fine if free still includes the full routing/reporting loop and the upsell explains the value honestly: free proves the habit on a couple of regular routes; paid unlocks unlimited saved routes and caregiver-ready share summaries. Gating core routing, scoring, or reporting would break the product promise. Gating unlimited favorites plus share export does not.

The boundary tests belong in this phase’s acceptance criteria, not deferred as “implementation detail.” `19m vs 21m`, `9m vs 11m`, `day 6 vs 8`, `30 vs 31`, `90 vs 91`, plus same-session supersession changing the next route recommendation, are the actual product contract for trust and explainability. If those are not explicitly must-pass, “accessibility-friction scoring” is still just a slogan.

**Claude (QA & Risk · Closer) — Round 2**

Codex, your must-have list holds up well and I don't want to relitigate the parts that are clean synthesis of prior phases — the seeded-corridor, the accessible-list-as-first-class-surface, the "recommendation sentence with inspectable evidence" framing, all of that I'd sign off on as-is. But I want to push back on one specific thing in your won't-build section, because I think it's actually where the coordinator's flag lands hardest: you wrote "verified partner data layer or caregiver sharing itself; those stay as the monetization path, not build-phase scope." That reads to me like you're punting the entire caregiver feature out of this phase's scope and treating "figure it out during monetization" as an acceptable answer. It isn't, and here's why it matters concretely: if caregiver sharing doesn't get a defined shape in *this* phase, the architecture phase has no idea whether to design an account/CloudKit layer or not, and we already know from three phases back that "monetization needs to pick one concretely rather than gesture at subscription abstractly" was a hard requirement. Deferring the shape of the paid feature to "the monetization path" is exactly the abstract gesture we were told not to make. So I'm not asking you to build caregiver sharing right now — I'm saying this phase has to decide its *shape* (ShareLink export, no live sync) so the must-have list can include a real, buildable line item instead of a placeholder.

On the specific fixes the coordinator asked you to confirm or contest — let me make the strongest case for each so we can actually close this round instead of bouncing to a third.

ShareLink-not-push: I don't think there's a real alternative here that doesn't violate the locked non-goal, so I'd treat this less as "my proposal vs. Codex's counter" and more as "is there any way to keep live caregiver notifications without backend infrastructure," and I don't think there is. Even a minimal version — "check for updates" polling against iCloud Drive as a poor-man's sync — is still an account-and-sync feature with real edge cases (stale caches, conflict resolution, what happens when the caregiver's copy and the primary user's copy disagree about which report superseded which). That's not an MVP corner-cut, that's a second data-sync system layered on top of a scoring engine we just finished pinning down to the boundary-meter. ShareLink avoids all of it: it's a share sheet call, a formatted string or a small JSON/PDF attachment, and it's done. If Codex has a cheaper live-sync mechanism in mind I'm open to hearing it, but "defer the whole feature's shape to later" isn't a counter-proposal, it's the exact ambiguity we're trying to kill.

The 2-favorite free cap — I'll defend the number but loosely, because the number itself is less important than the fact that it exists and is testable. Product brief said this user has "3-4 walks on repeat." If free is unlimited favorites, there's no reason to ever pay, since the entire paid feature bundle (per my proposal) is unlimited favorites + share export. If free is 1, that's stingy enough it might read as a bait-and-switch on a genuinely useful free utility, which per_app_product_brief explicitly said we don't want ("genuinely useful, but a utility, not a subscription" — that framing implies free tier shouldn't feel crippled). Two sits right at the edge of this person's real recurring-trip list, so the third save (the trip to the pharmacy, say, after clinic and transit stop are already saved) is the natural upgrade moment. I'll trade this number for a different one if Codex has a real objection, but "no cap, unlimited free favorites, paywall is share-export only" is the one alternative I'd actively argue against, because then the entire subscription rests on a single feature (sharing) with no redundancy — if ShareLink alone doesn't convert, there's no second lever.

On whether the boundary-value test list (19m/21m, day 6/8/30/31/90/91) belongs in this phase's acceptance criteria versus being a build-phase implementation detail — I want to hold my ground here and not water this down to "the engine should be tested." The distinction matters because "tested" is unfalsifiable as an acceptance criterion — anyone can write a test that passes without actually asserting the boundary. Given that literally every earlier phase called this scoring model "the differentiator" and it took two full rounds of the detailed-discussion phase to pin the exact numbers, if this phase's acceptance criteria says something vague like "scoring engine has test coverage," a rushed build pass will write a happy-path test (obstacle clearly on the route, obstacle clearly far away) and call it done, and the actual edge that makes the buffer meaningful — is 20m inclusive or exclusive, does day 7 round to the fresh band or the reduced band — never gets locked down and becomes a live bug nobody notices until QA. I'd rather this phase over-specify the acceptance criterion here than leave it to individual interpretation during build.

One more thing I want to add that neither of us has said yet and that I think is a real gap: nobody has put "at least two route alternatives requested and compared" into a testable acceptance criterion, even though Codex's own must-have list assumes route *comparison* is happening ("evaluates returned routes... deterministically recommends a route"). MKDirections can return one route or several depending on the corridor; if MapKit hands back exactly one walking route for a given origin/destination — which happens constantly in less dense areas — the entire "why this route beats the alternative" explanation has nothing to compare against. That's not a hypothetical edge case, that's probably the common case in the demo corridor itself depending on street layout. I want a must-have acceptance criterion that explicitly covers the single-route case: when MapKit returns only one walking route, the app still shows the full triad (what/why/confidence) but the "why" language adapts to something honest like "the only walking route found" rather than a comparative claim it can't support, and this needs its own test, not an assumption that multi-route responses are the norm.

So here's where I think we land to actually close this: Codex's list stands as the base, my ShareLink resolution and 2-favorite cap get written in as the concrete shape of the paid must-have rather than left as "stays as the monetization path," the boundary-value tests get written into this phase's acceptance criteria in explicit terms (not softened to generic "test coverage"), and we add one more must-have line for single-route-result honesty. If Codex agrees on those four things, I think we have consensus this round — I don't see daylight between us on values, just on whether the caregiver feature and the scoring tests get nailed down now versus deferred, and deferring either one repeats the exact mistake the prompt contract already warned us about.
