Nothing here pulls in opposite directions; it's four loose ends getting tied off.

## Final Output

**Must-have features:**

1. **Route planning from current location or manual origin to a searched destination.**
   User story: as a user who may deny location permission or plan for someone else, I need to set both ends of a trip and still get a usable route recommendation.
   Acceptance criteria: current-location or manual/dropped-pin origin both work; destination search/selection works; permission-denied does not dead-end; at least one route renders on success; offline or routing failure produces a distinct, honest error state (never confused with "no obstacles nearby").

2. **Accessibility-friction route scoring across returned walking routes.**
   User story: as a mobility-constrained pedestrian, I need the app to tell me which available route is least risky based on known reported barriers, not just which is shortest.
   Acceptance criteria: scoring uses the locked 20m/10m buffer tiers, type × severity × recency-band formula summed per route, and the shorter-route tie-break; a route with zero matched obstacles is labeled "no reported obstacles," never "safe" or "accessible." Boundary-value tests are explicit and must-pass, not left to interpretation: an obstacle at 19m counts, at 21m does not; at 9m reads "on this route," at 11m reads "nearby"; recency bands are asserted at the exact edges — day 6 vs. 8, day 30 vs. 31, day 90 vs. 91 must land in different decay bands.

3. **Plain-language route explanation with explicit confidence.**
   User story: as someone making a real mobility decision, I need to understand why a route is recommended and how complete the evidence is.
   Acceptance criteria: every result shows what's recommended, why, and confidence/staleness; confidence is derived from report density/freshness, independent of obstacle count; low-data corridors are visibly labeled low-confidence even with zero flagged obstacles.

4. **Single-route-result honesty.**
   User story: as a user in an area where MapKit only returns one walking route, I still need an honest explanation, not a comparison the app can't back up.
   Acceptance criteria: when MapKit returns exactly one walking route, the app still shows the full what/why/confidence triad, but the "why" language is adapted to something like "the only walking route found" rather than implying a comparison against alternatives that don't exist; this case has its own test, not an assumption that multi-route responses are typical.

5. **Obstacle reporting with immediate local effect, including supersession.**
   User story: as a user who hits a barrier or sees one has cleared, I need to record it fast and see the app reflect it right away.
   Acceptance criteria: report creation (type, severity, optional note, "looks clear now" where relevant) completes in under 30 seconds; a report submitted in-session changes the score of a route re-requested immediately after, no restart; a newer report inside an existing obstacle's buffer supersedes the old one in scoring (weighted toward newer), with history retained, verified by an explicit before/after test on the same buffer.

6. **Local persistence for reports, favorites, and saved destinations.**
   User story: as a repeat user with a handful of recurring walks, I need my reports and one-tap routes to survive relaunching the app.
   Acceptance criteria: reports, favorites, and saved endpoints survive an actual force-quit and relaunch (not just trusted at the framework level); favorites store origin/destination only and re-fetch live on open, with UI copy honest that it's "today's suggested route," never a frozen path.

7. **Accessible route details list alongside the map.**
   User story: as a VoiceOver user, or anyone who wants fast clarity, I need a linear list of route issues in travel order instead of interpreting map pins.
   Acceptance criteria: flagged items appear in a list ordered by progression along the route (not severity, not alphabetical); a manual VoiceOver pass reaches route summary, every flagged item, and the report action with zero dead ends; Dynamic Type at the largest accessibility size shows no truncation on any core screen.

8. **Seeded demo corridor plus honest empty states.**
   User story: as a first-time user, I need to see how the product works without the app pretending it has broad real coverage.
   Acceptance criteria: first launch includes clearly labeled sample reports in a fictional corridor (invented street names) inside a real named city; areas without reports say so plainly ("no reports yet — be the first"); the UI never implies live community coverage.

9. **Persistent safety/trust disclosure.**
   User story: as someone relying on this for a physical trip, I need a constant reminder that data is user-submitted and unverified.
   Acceptance criteria: disclaimer shown in onboarding and reachable at any time from the route result screen (footer/info affordance); it can be dismissed as an onboarding step but never fully suppressed from the app.

10. **Caregiver share export (ShareLink, not live sync).**
    User story: as a user who wants a family member or aide to see my planned route, I need to share it without either of us needing an account.
    Acceptance criteria: native `ShareLink` action generates a timestamped, self-contained snapshot (destination, recommendation, confidence/staleness, unverified-data disclaimer) as text/file content; opening it requires no account, no network call, no push infrastructure; this is explicitly not live-updating — no "notify me when this changes" mechanism ships in this MVP.

11. **Two-favorite free cap with StoreKit purchase/restore.**
    User story: as a free user, I should get real value from a couple of saved routes; as a paying user, I should get the recurring-trip and sharing value that matches how I actually use the app.
    Acceptance criteria: free tier allows up to 2 saved favorites; attempting a 3rd triggers an upsell screen, not a silent failure or crash; purchase unlocks unlimited favorites plus the ShareLink export; a working StoreKit purchase and restore flow gates exactly those two things — routing, scoring, and reporting remain free and ungated.

12. **Domain-logic unit tests independent of UI.**
    User story: as the team shipping the app's one truly verifiable subsystem, I need the scoring/obstacle-impact logic tested without relying on a human eyeballing a map.
    Acceptance criteria: the boundary-value tests from item 2, the supersession test from item 5, and the single-route case from item 4 all exist as automated tests, not just "coverage" in the abstract.

**Should-have:**
- One-tap save of a route as a named favorite immediately after viewing results.
- Recent destinations for faster repeat planning.
- Inline guidance discouraging personal/identifying info in obstacle notes.

**Could-have:**
- Rider profile (wheelchair/cane/rollator) reweighting — explicitly not must-have, since the locked scoring model is universal, not profile-based, and reopening it now would unwind tested math for a feature that was never actually spec'd.
- Browsable history view of superseded reports (data model already retains them; a UI to browse is extra surface).
- Lightweight onboarding walkthrough using the seeded corridor to teach "recommended / why / confidence."
- Small trend cues in favorites ("last updated 6 days ago").
- Downloadable curated coverage packs (already flagged in the product brief as secondary, not anchor).
- Haptic/visual emphasis for severe on-route hazards.

**Won't-build (explicit, not implied):**
- Any live sync, push notification, or backend-dependent caregiver feature — caregiver value in this MVP is the ShareLink export only; live "your mom's route just changed" notifications are out.
- Turn-by-turn voice guidance or live GPS tracking during the walk.
- Real multi-user sync, moderation, or actual crowd/community data presence.
- Photo capture, camera-based detection, or ML features.
- Custom pedestrian graph, curb-cut intelligence, or grade modeling.
- Multi-stop itineraries, trip history dashboards, transit integration, or discovery/social feed features.
- Verified partner/curated data layer (secondary paid idea for later, not this build).

**Biggest risk carried forward:** the app has more trust states than feature states — permission-denied, low-confidence, zero-matched-obstacle, offline-new-route-failure, single-route-result, stale/superseded reports, seeded-vs-real data. If these collapse into generic empty/error screens, the product misleads even when the underlying math is correct. The core output of this app is a recommendation sentence with inspectable evidence and explicit uncertainty, not "a route on a map" — that has to survive into design and build untouched.

**Quality Gate (Claude) — after round 2**

QUALITY: PASS

## Feedback

This closes the phase. All four required outputs are present and concrete: a 12-item must-have list each with a user story and testable acceptance criteria, a should-have list, a could-have list, and an explicit won't-build list. Nothing here is generic — every must-have ties directly to mechanisms pinned in `detailed_discussion` (the 20m/10m buffer, the recency bands, the shorter-route tie-break, favorites-as-live-refetch) rather than restating themes.

Strengths:
- The round-1 coordinator flag (caregiver notifications vs. the "no live sync/backend" non-goal) was a real contradiction between two previously-locked phases, and it's genuinely resolved, not papered over: caregiver value is redefined as a `ShareLink` snapshot export, and the won't-build list explicitly names "live sync, push notification, or backend-dependent caregiver feature" as out, closing the loophole Codex's own round-1 wording had left open.
- Boundary-value tests (19m/21m, 9m/11m, day 6/8, 30/31, 90/91) are written directly into the must-have acceptance criteria rather than deferred to "build-phase testing," which was the exact ambiguity Claude pushed on — the rubric requires acceptance criteria per must-have, and this is unusually rigorous compliance with that requirement given the scoring model's centrality to the product's differentiator.
- The single-route-result case (item 4) is a legitimate, non-trivial gap-fill: MapKit frequently returns one walking route, and without this the "why this route beats the alternative" framing would silently overclaim in the common case, not just an edge case.
- Monetization is concrete and testable (2-favorite cap, specific upsell trigger, StoreKit gate scoped to exactly two features), satisfying the earlier-phase requirement that monetization not stay an abstract gesture.
- The could-have bucket correctly declines to reopen the rider-profile idea, citing that reweighting would unwind already-tested universal scoring math for a feature that was never actually specified — good scope discipline consistent with the decision rules from `prompt_contract`.
- The "biggest risk carried forward" closing note (more trust states than feature states) correctly reflects the actual throughline of this entire phase's discussion and hands design/build a real warning rather than a platitude.

No contradictions with `prompt_contract`, `initial_discussion`, `per_app_product_brief`, or `detailed_discussion` were introduced. Ready to hand to the design phase.

## Coordinator Decision

See the coordinator's message above.

## Final Output

CONSENSUS: YES

This round actually closed the loop. Codex came back and explicitly signed off on all three things the coordinator flagged: the ShareLink-not-push shape for caregiver value, the 2-favorite free cap (with the caveat that it only works if free still feels genuinely useful, not crippled), and treating the boundary-value tests (19m/21m, day 6/8/30/31/90/91) as real acceptance criteria for this phase rather than something to hand-wave as "build-phase testing." Claude then pushed on one more thing — that Codex's won't-build list still had "caregiver sharing itself... stays as the monetization path" sitting there unchanged, which contradicts what Codex had just agreed to two paragraphs earlier (a defined ShareLink export shipping as MVP scope). That's not really a new disagreement, it's Codex's own position catching up with its own wording — Codex's round 2 already describes the ShareLink feature as something "the MVP should share," which only makes sense if it's a must-have, not a deferred non-goal. Claude's other addition — a must-have for the single-route case, where MapKit only returns one walking route and the "why this route beats the alternative" framing has to honestly downgrade to "the only route found" instead of a comparison it can't support — is a small, logical gap-fill on top of Codex's own scoring/comparison requirement, not a contested new mechanism. Nothing here pulls in opposite directions; it's four loose ends getting tied off.

## Final Output

**Must-have features:**

1. **Route planning from current location or manual origin to a searched destination.**
   User story: as a user who may deny location permission or plan for someone else, I need to set both ends of a trip and still get a usable route recommendation.
   Acceptance criteria: current-location or manual/dropped-pin origin both work; destination search/selection works; permission-denied does not dead-end; at least one route renders on success; offline or routing failure produces a distinct, honest error state (never confused with "no obstacles nearby").

2. **Accessibility-friction route scoring across returned walking routes.**
   User story: as a mobility-constrained pedestrian, I need the app to tell me which available route is least risky based on known reported barriers, not just which is shortest.
   Acceptance criteria: scoring uses the locked 20m/10m buffer tiers, type × severity × recency-band formula summed per route, and the shorter-route tie-break; a route with zero matched obstacles is labeled "no reported obstacles," never "safe" or "accessible." Boundary-value tests are explicit and must-pass, not left to interpretation: an obstacle at 19m counts, at 21m does not; at 9m reads "on this route," at 11m reads "nearby"; recency bands are asserted at the exact edges — day 6 vs. 8, day 30 vs. 31, day 90 vs. 91 must land in different decay bands.

3. **Plain-language route explanation with explicit confidence.**
   User story: as someone making a real mobility decision, I need to understand why a route is recommended and how complete the evidence is.
   Acceptance criteria: every result shows what's recommended, why, and confidence/staleness; confidence is derived from report density/freshness, independent of obstacle count; low-data corridors are visibly labeled low-confidence even with zero flagged obstacles.

4. **Single-route-result honesty.**
   User story: as a user in an area where MapKit only returns one walking route, I still need an honest explanation, not a comparison the app can't back up.
   Acceptance criteria: when MapKit returns exactly one walking route, the app still shows the full what/why/confidence triad, but the "why" language is adapted to something like "the only walking route found" rather than implying a comparison against alternatives that don't exist; this case has its own test, not an assumption that multi-route responses are typical.

5. **Obstacle reporting with immediate local effect, including supersession.**
   User story: as a user who hits a barrier or sees one has cleared, I need to record it fast and see the app reflect it right away.
   Acceptance criteria: report creation (type, severity, optional note, "looks clear now" where relevant) completes in under 30 seconds; a report submitted in-session changes the score of a route re-requested immediately after, no restart; a newer report inside an existing obstacle's buffer supersedes the old one in scoring (weighted toward newer), with history retained, verified by an explicit before/after test on the same buffer.

6. **Local persistence for reports, favorites, and saved destinations.**
   User story: as a repeat user with a handful of recurring walks, I need my reports and one-tap routes to survive relaunching the app.
   Acceptance criteria: reports, favorites, and saved endpoints survive an actual force-quit and relaunch (not just trusted at the framework level); favorites store origin/destination only and re-fetch live on open, with UI copy honest that it's "today's suggested route," never a frozen path.

7. **Accessible route details list alongside the map.**
   User story: as a VoiceOver user, or anyone who wants fast clarity, I need a linear list of route issues in travel order instead of interpreting map pins.
   Acceptance criteria: flagged items appear in a list ordered by progression along the route (not severity, not alphabetical); a manual VoiceOver pass reaches route summary, every flagged item, and the report action with zero dead ends; Dynamic Type at the largest accessibility size shows no truncation on any core screen.

8. **Seeded demo corridor plus honest empty states.**
   User story: as a first-time user, I need to see how the product works without the app pretending it has broad real coverage.
   Acceptance criteria: first launch includes clearly labeled sample reports in a fictional corridor (invented street names) inside a real named city; areas without reports say so plainly ("no reports yet — be the first"); the UI never implies live community coverage.

9. **Persistent safety/trust disclosure.**
   User story: as someone relying on this for a physical trip, I need a constant reminder that data is user-submitted and unverified.
   Acceptance criteria: disclaimer shown in onboarding and reachable at any time from the route result screen (footer/info affordance); it can be dismissed as an onboarding step but never fully suppressed from the app.

10. **Caregiver share export (ShareLink, not live sync).**
    User story: as a user who wants a family member or aide to see my planned route, I need to share it without either of us needing an account.
    Acceptance criteria: native `ShareLink` action generates a timestamped, self-contained snapshot (destination, recommendation, confidence/staleness, unverified-data disclaimer) as text/file content; opening it requires no account, no network call, no push infrastructure; this is explicitly not live-updating — no "notify me when this changes" mechanism ships in this MVP.

11. **Two-favorite free cap with StoreKit purchase/restore.**
    User story: as a free user, I should get real value from a couple of saved routes; as a paying user, I should get the recurring-trip and sharing value that matches how I actually use the app.
    Acceptance criteria: free tier allows up to 2 saved favorites; attempting a 3rd triggers an upsell screen, not a silent failure or crash; purchase unlocks unlimited favorites plus the ShareLink export; a working StoreKit purchase and restore flow gates exactly those two things — routing, scoring, and reporting remain free and ungated.

12. **Domain-logic unit tests independent of UI.**
    User story: as the team shipping the app's one truly verifiable subsystem, I need the scoring/obstacle-impact logic tested without relying on a human eyeballing a map.
    Acceptance criteria: the boundary-value tests from item 2, the supersession test from item 5, and the single-route case from item 4 all exist as automated tests, not just "coverage" in the abstract.

**Should-have:**
- One-tap save of a route as a named favorite immediately after viewing results.
- Recent destinations for faster repeat planning.
- Inline guidance discouraging personal/identifying info in obstacle notes.

**Could-have:**
- Rider profile (wheelchair/cane/rollator) reweighting — explicitly not must-have, since the locked scoring model is universal, not profile-based, and reopening it now would unwind tested math for a feature that was never actually spec'd.
- Browsable history view of superseded reports (data model already retains them; a UI to browse is extra surface).
- Lightweight onboarding walkthrough using the seeded corridor to teach "recommended / why / confidence."
- Small trend cues in favorites ("last updated 6 days ago").
- Downloadable curated coverage packs (already flagged in the product brief as secondary, not anchor).
- Haptic/visual emphasis for severe on-route hazards.

**Won't-build (explicit, not implied):**
- Any live sync, push notification, or backend-dependent caregiver feature — caregiver value in this MVP is the ShareLink export only; live "your mom's route just changed" notifications are out.
- Turn-by-turn voice guidance or live GPS tracking during the walk.
- Real multi-user sync, moderation, or actual crowd/community data presence.
- Photo capture, camera-based detection, or ML features.
- Custom pedestrian graph, curb-cut intelligence, or grade modeling.
- Multi-stop itineraries, trip history dashboards, transit integration, or discovery/social feed features.
- Verified partner/curated data layer (secondary paid idea for later, not this build).

**Biggest risk carried forward:** the app has more trust states than feature states — permission-denied, low-confidence, zero-matched-obstacle, offline-new-route-failure, single-route-result, stale/superseded reports, seeded-vs-real data. If these collapse into generic empty/error screens, the product misleads even when the underlying math is correct. The core output of this app is a recommendation sentence with inspectable evidence and explicit uncertainty, not "a route on a map" — that has to survive into design and build untouched.

---

CONSENSUS: YES

phase_discussions.title: Design Discussion
phase_discussions.output:
All three rounds converged, and this last one closed cleanly — Claude actually talked itself out of its own map-strip idea before Codex even had to push back hard, and landed on something better than either round-two position: no MapKit view on `Plan` at all, but the first-launch "show, don't tell" need gets met by routing straight into a fully-populated `Route Result` screen using the seeded demo data, rather than building a bespoke preview widget. Codex's response (which came just before, arguing for the same map-free `Plan` plus a demo-corridor card) and Claude's final version line up almost exactly — the only refinement is that Claude's version reuses the real result screen instead of a separate demo affordance, which is strictly less work to build and a stronger first impression. Nothing contested remains.

CONSENSUS: YES

## Final Output

**Screen inventory:**
1. **Onboarding** — three cards max (what the app does, what it doesn't guarantee, how sample/local data works), ending in a location-permission step with an equal-weight "plan without my location" path.
2. **Plan** (tab 1) — text-first: origin field (current location or manual/dropped-pin, both first-class), destination search field, a single one-row shortcut to the most relevant recent/favorite destination, a persistent trust banner, and — only on a genuinely fresh install with zero recents/favorites — one tappable "see how it works" demo card. No map surface on this screen, ever.
3. **Destination/Origin Search** — modal or pushed screen: search results, recent places, manual pin-drop entry.
4. **Route Result** — the hero screen, pushed from Plan (real search) or from the fresh-install demo card (seeded corridor data). Recommendation sentence, confidence badge (icon + word, never color-only), plain-language why, all as co-equal outputs above the fold; a segmented `Map / List` control below (not a screen push); a compact route-switcher chip row when MapKit returns multiple routes, absent entirely for single-route results; warning chips distinguishing "on this route" vs "nearby."
5. **Obstacle detail** — bottom sheet from tapping a flagged item (map pin or list row): type, severity, recency, note, on-route/nearby status, inline report action.
6. **Report Obstacle** — bottom sheet, reachable from Route Result's persistent action or any flagged item: type chips, severity, optional note, "looks clear now" toggle when applicable, save (target: under 30 seconds).
7. **Saved** (tab 2) — full list-management screen for favorites: each row live-refetches and shows current status ("no new reported issues" / "N new reports since saved"), a persistent "2 of 2 free" indicator once at cap, share action per row.
8. **Paywall** — sheet triggered at the 3rd favorite save attempt, showing the actual route just tried to be saved underneath a translucent unlock panel; sells unlimited favorites + share export specifically.
9. **Share preview** — shown before invoking the system `ShareLink`, previewing exactly what the recipient will see (destination, recommendation, confidence, timestamp, unverified-data line).
10. **Settings/info** (toolbar affordance, not a tab) — full disclaimer text, sample-data explanation, restore purchases, privacy guidance on notes, demo-data reset.

**Primary user flow:**
First-time: onboarding → Plan (manual or current-location origin) → destination search → Route Result (two-stage load: "finding walking route" → "checking reported obstacles") → recommendation/why/confidence → inspect via Map or List → optionally report an obstacle (score updates live if returned to the same result) → optionally save as favorite or share. Repeat/habitual flow (the actual daily case): open app → tap favorite shortcut on Plan or in Saved → Route Result re-fetches live → check "no new issues" → leave. Permission-denied follows the identical flow with manual origin entry substituted, never a dead end.

**State model per screen (key states):**
- *Plan:* fresh-install-empty-with-demo-card; populated-with-recents/favorites; location-permission-denied (inline banner, not modal); destination-search-empty/ambiguous.
- *Route Result:* two-stage loading (fetch → score); success-multi-route; success-single-route (language shifts to "the only walking route found," switcher chips absent); success-zero-matched-obstacles ("no reported obstacles on this route," confidence shown separately, never "safe"); low-confidence (equal visual weight to the recommendation, not caption text); offline-new-search-failure (endpoints preserved, distinct honest copy); offline-cached-route (obstacle scoring still refreshes); geocoding failure (editable in place, no backward bounce); permission lost mid-session (falls back to manual origin with explanation).
- *Saved:* empty (no favorites yet); under-cap; at-cap (paywall intercept on next save attempt); favorite reopened (live re-fetch, "today's suggested route" framing).
- *Report sheet:* default entry; reporting against an existing flagged item (adds "looks clear now"); save success (visible live update on the underlying result if returned to it).
- *Paywall:* pre-purchase; purchasing; restore.

**Visual direction:** Calm, premium, trust-first — deep slate/ink rather than default navigation blue, muted teal/petrol for primary route emphasis, amber strictly for moderate flags, rust/coral strictly for severe on-route hazards, no green-means-safe semantics anywhere. Confidence is its own badge system (icon + word). Seeded/demo content gets a distinct, consistent visual treatment (subtle dashed outline or badge) so it's never mistaken for real data at a glance. Typography carries the "premium" weight: the recommendation sentence is the visual anchor of Route Result, generous spacing, built to survive largest Dynamic Type by growing card height, not truncating — hardest on Route Result and the paywall screen.

**Accessibility notes:** The List representation on Route Result is a first-class peer to Map, not a fallback — reached via segmented control, never a buried screen push. Travel-order sequencing is the default everywhere flagged items appear. VoiceOver labels encode obstacle type, severity, recency, and on-route/nearby relationship in a single pass per item. Color never solely carries the on-route vs. nearby distinction — always paired with icon/text. Report sheet uses large tap targets, minimal custom gestures, operable one-handed and with Switch Control. The trust disclaimer is persistently reachable from Route Result but doesn't dominate reading order on every screen open.
phase_discussions.discussion_markdown:
# clearpath-access — Design Discussion

_Generated by the autonomous multi-agent orchestrator on 2026-07-07 01:53:56._

## Original Prompt

```
PORTFOLIO_CHILD_PROJECT: true
Parent portfolio: batch-cat-b1
Selected app slug: clearpath-access

Build this app as a working local-first SwiftUI MVP.

## Selected App Spec

# Clearpath Access

Build mode: **MVP build**.

## App Name

Clearpath Access

## Category

Navigation

## One-Sentence Promise

Accessibility-aware pedestrian routing with community-sourced obstacle reporting

## Claude Design Handoff Prompt

(not supplied yet)

## Parent Portfolio Prompt

Build 9 completely separate production-ready iOS apps at the same time: exactly 3 Health & Fitness apps, 3 Medical apps, and 3 Navigation apps.

This is a multi-app PORTFOLIO request: multiple apps in one program. Each selected app becomes its own separate project — one folder per app.


Requirements for every app:
1. Must go from 0 to production-ready, not just a few features.
2. Must be unique, useful, and commercially viable.
3. Must be beautifully designed and feel premium.
4. Must provide real end-user value.
5. Must have a realistic path to monetization — a subscription or paid offering with genuine value (never cosmetics-only).
6. Should have viral potential (broad or niche) without sacrificing usefulness.
7. Must be better than its competitors in a meaningful way.
8. Local-first where applicable, architected so cloud support can be added later without rewriting.

UNIQUENESS (hard rule, non-negotiable):
- No app in this batch may be similar to another app in this batch.
- No app may be similar to anything already built or being built in this workspace or before, including: Waylay (location-based personal recall utility), TrueScale, Provenance, Scope-Guard, Practice-Loop, Proof, ReturnWise, VerveCoach, CueKeeper, Countertop, FieldQuote, my existing digital temple app, and my existing PO-automation tool.
- No app may be similar to apps in the OTHER batches of this program (check sibling project folders in the workspace for their concepts).
- Do not reuse prior concepts, themes, or mechanics from earlier apps — even if the request sounds similar to something built before, produce a differentiated concept.

Bonus points (optional — only when integral and genuinely valuable, never forced): unique use of LLMs, AR, or ML.

Build rules:
- Run the app efforts in parallel; keep discussion/design phases separate per app.
- Each app gets its own full product direction, design direction, architecture, implementation plan, and build output.
- Save all phase discussions and final decisions; at the end combine each app's full transcript into a .txt file.
- If an app is liked, also prepare Jira board + Notion project structures for backfilling later.
- Use enough rounds per phase for high-quality discussion, ending early on consensus.
- Recover cleanly from stalls; report only important milestones.

Output:
- One folder per app in the output directory — never one folder containing all apps.
Output directory:
- /Users/pchordia/Documents/iOS-App-Factory

Additional notes:
Medical/health apps must frame outputs as wellness insights and validated risk scores (e.g. QRISK3, FINDRISC style), never diagnosis.

Now generate at least 18 candidate ideas, discuss them thoroughly, choose the best 9, and build all 9 to production quality.
```

## Phase Purpose

Decide UX flows, information architecture, key screens/states, and the design principles that govern them.

## Transcript


### Round 1


**Codex (Product Strategist · Closer) — Round 1**

The design should center on one question the user is asking at the door: “Can I make this walk today, and why do you think that?” If the app does anything that weakens that answer, it is noise. So I would keep the structure tight: onboarding and trust setup first, then a single planning surface, then a route result that is really a decision screen, not just a map, then lightweight reporting, favorites, and paywall/share moments branching off that core.

The screen inventory I’d lock is this. First-launch onboarding is three cards max: what the app does, what it does not guarantee, and how local sample data works. The final onboarding step is location permission, but with a clear “plan without my location” path so denial never feels like failure. The home/planner screen is the primary shell: origin field, destination field, swap/edit controls, recent destinations, favorite routes, a map preview, and a visible trust banner saying reports are user-submitted and unverified. Search should be its own modal or pushed screen with Apple-style search results, recent places, and a “drop pin manually” entry path for origin. Route results should be the real centerpiece: recommended route card at the top, plain-English why/confidence line, warning chips for on-route versus nearby obstacles, map with route overlays, and a segmented toggle or stacked switch between map view and route details list. Obstacle details should not be a separate deep screen unless needed; tapping a flagged item can open a bottom sheet with type, severity, recency, note, and whether it is on-route or nearby. Report obstacle should be a fast sheet, not a full-screen detour: type, severity, optional note, “looks clear now,” save. Favorites should likely live as a compact library screen reached from home, with named routes, saved endpoints, last checked time, and share action. The share/export screen can just be a system share sheet triggered from route results or favorites, but the pre-share preview matters because the user needs to know what the caregiver will actually see. The upsell screen is simple: unlimited favorites plus caregiver-ready route export, nothing broader. Settings is small but necessary: disclaimer, sample-data explanation, restore purchases, note privacy guidance, and maybe a demo-data reset.

The primary user flow should be brutally short. Open app, choose or confirm origin, choose destination, fetch route, see one recommendation with a reason and confidence, inspect issues in travel order, optionally save as favorite or share, optionally submit a report, then re-run and watch the recommendation update. If location is denied, the flow is identical except origin starts as manual entry. If the user is a repeat user, the real daily flow is even shorter: open, tap favorite, see today’s suggested route to that destination, check whether anything new affects it, leave.

The route result screen needs the most disciplined state model because that is where trust is won or lost. Before search, it is an instructional empty state with favorites and recent destinations. While routing, it shows a loading state that distinguishes “fetching route” from “scoring obstacles” if those happen sequentially. On success with multiple routes, it shows a recommendation plus alternatives summary. On success with one route, it must switch language to “only walking route found” and stop implying comparison. On success with zero matched obstacles, it should say “no reported obstacles on this route” and still show confidence separately. On low-confidence corridors, the confidence warning needs equal visual weight to the recommendation, not tiny caption text. On offline-new-search failure, the screen should preserve entered endpoints and say clearly that new routing is unavailable right now. On cached-route-offline, it can still refresh obstacle scoring and say so. On geocoding failure, origin/destination entry should stay editable in place without bouncing the user backward. On permission loss mid-session, it should fall back to manual origin with a clear explanation.

The planner screen also needs explicit states. Fresh install with no favorites should show the sample corridor and explain it is demo data. Repeat use with favorites should bias toward one-tap routes, not a blank map. Permission denied should replace current-location affordances with a manual-origin card rather than keeping disabled controls around. No nearby reports in the visible map area should be an honest informational state, not an error state. Purchase-locked third favorite should trigger an upsell intercept at the moment of save, with the route still visible behind it so the app does not feel punitive.

Visually, I would avoid civic-app grayness and avoid generic navigation blue overload. This should feel calm, precise, and trustworthy, more like a premium health-adjacent utility than a transit app. Use a warm light background, deep slate text, muted teal or petrol for primary route emphasis, amber for caution, and rust or coral only for severe on-route hazards. The recommended route card should read like a briefing card, not a map legend. Confidence should have its own badge system, but not green/red safety semantics because “green means safe” is exactly the wrong implication here. Map styling should be subdued so the overlays and list do the interpretive work. Typography should be large, plain, and assertive, with the recommendation sentence as the visual anchor of the screen.

Accessibility has to shape structure, not decorate it later. The route details list is a peer to the map, not a fallback buried three taps down. Travel order is the default ordering everywhere. VoiceOver labels need to encode obstacle type, severity, recency, and route relationship in one pass. Dynamic Type should push cards taller rather than truncating explanation text. Color can never carry the difference between “on this route” and “nearby”; that needs iconography and text. The report sheet should be operable one-handed and with Switch Control, which means large tap targets and minimal custom gestures. The trust disclaimer must be persistently reachable from route results, but it cannot dominate reading order every time a screen opens or it will become accessibility friction itself.

The biggest design risk is building a beautiful route map that emotionally reads as certainty while the text quietly says uncertainty. If the screen hierarchy makes the map feel primary and the explanation/confidence feel secondary, the product will overclaim even with correct copy. The detail the final output must preserve is that the route result is a decision screen with three co-equal outputs every time: recommendation, reason, and confidence. Everything else, including the map, is there to support those three things.

**Claude (Design Lead · Visionary) — Round 1**

Coming into this fresh as design lead, the thing I want to protect is that this app's hero moment is a sentence, not a map — every other screen decision should serve getting the user from "I need to go somewhere" to reading that sentence and trusting it in under ten seconds. So I'd resist the instinct to build this like a mapping app with a features bar bolted on. I'd structure it as a genuinely small IA: two tabs, Plan and Saved, plus a settings/info affordance in the toolbar (not a third tab, since it's low-frequency) that's where the persistent disclaimer lives in full. Everything else — reporting, sharing, the paywall — happens as sheets pushed from context, not new top-level destinations, because this user is trying to make one decision quickly, not explore an app.

Plan is the landing screen and it needs to feel almost embarrassingly simple: an origin field defaulting to current location with a manual/dropped-pin alternative one tap away (never buried, since permission-denied and caregiver-planning both depend on it being a first-class equal option, not a fallback link), a destination search field with recents and favorites surfaced right there so a repeat user never has to type anything, and a single primary action. No map on this screen at all, actually — I'd save the map for the result, and let Plan be almost text-only, fast, and calm. States here: empty (fresh install, no recents, no favorites — this is where I'd want a small "try the demo corridor" affordance so a brand-new user isn't staring at two blank fields with no idea what the app even does), populated-with-recents, location-permission-denied banner state (not a modal, not a dead end — just an inline note that current location isn't available and manual entry is ready), and a validation state when destination search comes back empty or ambiguous.

Route Result is where I want to be the most opinionated. I don't think the recommendation triad should read like a map app's route card with a badge slapped on — I want it to read like a receipt: a single dominant headline sentence ("Recommended: Elm Street corridor — fewer reported hazards"), directly under it a confidence chip that is never color-only (icon plus word, since we can't lean on red/yellow/green alone), and under that a short "why" — two or three plain clauses, not a bullet dump. Below the fold, a segmented control, not a second screen push: Map / List. I feel strongly this should be a segment toggle rather than a separate navigation destination, because the list view is not a secondary accessibility accommodation, it's a first-class parallel representation of the same data, and forcing a screen-stack push to reach it is exactly the kind of thing that reads fine sighted and creates a dead-end-feeling detour for VoiceOver. The list itself is walking-order sequential items, each one a full sentence a screen reader can land on and understand alone ("Second: reported broken pavement, moderate, 4 days ago, on this route"), each with an inline "report" affordance so a user encountering a flagged spot doesn't have to go hunt for a global report button. If MapKit hands back more than one walkable route, I want a lightweight route-switcher — not a full comparison table, just a compact horizontal set of alternative summary chips ("This route" / "2 min longer, fewer hazards") that swap the same card and map underneath, so comparison never becomes its own separate screen either. When there's only one route, that switcher simply doesn't render, and the why-text downgrades to "the only walking route found between these points" — that distinction has to be visually obvious, not a quiet copy change, or a user will assume they're seeing a chosen winner over alternatives that never existed.

Reporting is a bottom sheet, reachable from the persistent action on Route Result and from any individual list item, and it should stay to the six-item vocabulary plus severity plus optional note plus the "looks clear now" option when reporting against something already flagged — this has to feel like it takes 15 seconds, not fill-out-a-form, so I'd use large tappable type chips and a two-level severity control rather than pickers or steppers. Submitting it should visibly animate the recommendation card updating if the user is returned to Route Result — that live-update moment is honestly the best marketing this app has, and I don't want it to be invisible or require a manual refresh tap.

Saved is the favorites tab: a plain list of named routes, each row live-fetching its current status in the background and showing something like "no new reported issues" or "1 new report since you saved this" — never a frozen snapshot presented as current. A clear "2 of 2 free" indicator sits at the top once the user has two, and attempting a third opens the paywall sheet, which needs to sell the actual unlock (unlimited saved routes plus the share export) rather than a generic "go premium" wall — I want that screen to show, concretely, the third route the user was just trying to save, still visible under a translucent unlock panel, so the value being paid for is the exact thing in front of them. Tapping a favorite opens the same Route Result screen, re-fetched live, with copy that says "today's suggested route to Dr. Patel's office" — never implying it's the same physical path as last time by default.

Share is just the native share sheet off a generated snapshot — I don't think it needs its own designed screen beyond a preview of what's being shared (destination, the recommendation sentence, confidence, timestamp, the unverified-data line), which matters because a user should be able to see exactly what a caregiver is about to receive before it goes out, not trust that it's fine.

The visual direction I'd fight for: this should not look like a generic turn-by-turn maps app, and it shouldn't look clinical either — it's neither Apple Maps nor a hospital intake form. I'd go with a calm, confident palette anchored in deep slate/ink rather than the expected blue, reserving warm amber strictly for moderate flags and red strictly for severe ones so color carries real meaning instead of being decorative, and using a soft, muted "sample data" visual treatment (a subtle dashed outline or distinct badge) anywhere seeded demo content appears so it's never mistaken for real reports at a glance, not just in copy. Typography should do a lot of the "premium" work here — a strong single headline weight for the recommendation sentence, generous spacing, and everything built to survive Dynamic Type's largest size without truncating, which given how sentence-heavy this app is will actually be one of the harder layout problems, especially on the Route Result card and the paywall screen.

The biggest risk in my own direction is the segmented Map/List toggle — it's the right call for accessibility and for treating the list as first-class, but it means the map itself has to be genuinely optional and the recommendation card has to work as a complete, satisfying screen with the map segment never opened at all, which is a stricter design constraint than most navigation apps ever accept. The detail I most want preserved past this phase: the recommendation card is the product, and every screen radiating out from it — planning, reporting, saving, sharing — exists to feed it better information or get the user to it faster, not to compete with it for attention.

**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 1**

Here's where the room actually stands after round one: there's a lot of real agreement, but the information architecture itself is still unsettled, so this isn't a close-out yet.

**What's already locked in, no daylight between them:**
- The route result screen is the actual product — a decision screen built around three co-equal things every time: what's recommended, why, and how confident the app is. Neither of them wants the map to visually dominate that triad.
- The accessible list view is a first-class parallel to the map, not a fallback — both want travel-order sequencing and both are wary of burying it behind extra navigation.
- Single-route vs multi-route needs visibly different language, not a quiet copy change — both called this out independently.
- Reporting is a fast bottom sheet (type/severity/note/"looks clear now"), and submitting it should visibly animate the recommendation updating live — that's the best "aha" moment in the app and neither wants it hidden behind a manual refresh.
- Favorites show live status ("no new reported issues" / "1 new report since you saved this"), never a frozen snapshot, and the 3rd-favorite paywall moment should show the actual thing being unlocked rather than a generic upsell wall.
- Share is just the native share sheet, but with a preview so the user knows exactly what a caregiver will see before it goes out.
- Visual direction agrees on avoiding generic maps-blue and civic-app grayness, using color only where it carries real meaning (never green-means-safe), and treating Dynamic Type as a real layout constraint especially on the recommendation card.

**What's still genuinely open:**
- **The home/planning screen's shape is different in each proposal.** Codex describes one shell screen — origin/destination fields, recents, favorites, *and a map preview* — all together. Claude explicitly wants Plan to be map-free and nearly text-only, with Saved pulled out as its own top-level tab reached via a two-tab structure (Plan / Saved) rather than a favorites section living inside the home screen. That's a real structural fork, not a wording difference — it changes the screen inventory and the tab bar itself.
- Nobody's addressed whether Route Result's loading state should distinguish "fetching the route" from "scoring obstacles against it" as two visible steps (Codex raised this; Claude's loading-state notes didn't weigh in).
- Gemini is still unavailable, so there's no third read on the IA fork.

CONSENSUS: NO

Next round needs Codex and Claude to actually resolve the IA question directly: is this a two-tab app (Plan / Saved) with Plan being deliberately map-free, or a single home shell that blends planning, recents, favorites, and a map preview together? That decision determines the screen inventory everything else in this phase hangs off of.

### Round 2


**Codex (Product Strategist · Closer) — Round 2**

Claude’s right that the only real open issue now is the IA fork, and I’m going to push for the two-tab structure with a deliberately map-light `Plan` screen rather than the all-in-one home shell I sketched in round one. After sitting with it, the blended home screen is trying to satisfy first-time discovery, repeat-use shortcuts, manual-origin fallback, and map reassurance all in one place, and that is exactly how you end up rebuilding a generic navigation app even while saying you don’t want one. The user’s first job here is not “inspect the area,” it’s “set the trip quickly and get the decision sentence.” A map preview on the planner screen mostly creates visual weight without adding decision value, and worse, it competes with the trust framing before the app has even produced a recommendation. So I’d collapse that. `Plan` should be fast, calm, and mostly textual: origin, destination, recents, favorites shortcuts, demo-corridor affordance on fresh install, and a visible trust banner. Then `Route Result` earns the map because that’s where spatial context actually matters. `Saved` deserves its own tab because repeat users are a core audience, not an edge case, and hiding favorites in a home section keeps the app biased toward one-off search instead of the recurring-trip habit we already said is the retention engine.

I also think Claude’s instinct to keep the list and map as a segmented peer view on the result screen is the correct structural call, and it actually resolves the accessibility problem more cleanly than my earlier “toggle or stacked switch” language did. If the recommendation card is the hero and `Map / List` is the representation switch underneath it, then the product stays honest: the sentence is primary, the representations are secondary tools. That’s the right hierarchy. The one thing I want to keep from my round-one proposal is the explicit two-step loading language on `Route Result`: first “finding walking route,” then “checking reported obstacles.” That is not implementation trivia. It tells the user what the app is waiting on and prevents a stalled spinner from feeling like vague failure. So the concrete structure I’d lock is: onboarding, then a two-tab app with `Plan` and `Saved`; `Plan` is map-free and handles origin/destination plus recents/favorites/demo entry; `Route Result` is pushed from either tab and is the real decision screen with recommendation/why/confidence on top and a segmented `Map / List` below; reporting, sharing, paywall, and obstacle detail all stay as sheets from context. That gives us a view hierarchy a SwiftUI developer can actually build, keeps every required workflow on a clear screen path, and most importantly stops the planner from bloating into the kind of faux-complete map home screen this product does not need.

**Claude (Design Lead · Visionary) — Round 2**

Codex's shell-screen pitch is stronger than I gave it credit for on one specific point: a map preview on the planning screen isn't the same claim as "the map should dominate the route result," and I conflated those two things in round one. The route result screen absolutely should not let the map outshine the recommendation sentence — that's the risk Codex is naming too when they say "a beautiful route map that emotionally reads as certainty while the text quietly says uncertainty," and we're in complete agreement there. But on the planning screen, before any recommendation exists at all, a small map isn't competing with anything — there's no sentence yet to undercut. So I want to partially concede: Plan doesn't need to be map-free, it needs a map that knows its place.

Here's the concrete version I'd actually build: a single Plan tab, origin/destination fields up top exactly as I described, then a compact, collapsed map strip — maybe 25% of screen height, not full-bleed — showing either the user's current area or, on a fresh install with nothing saved yet, the demo corridor with a couple of sample pins visible. That solves a real problem Codex is right about and I underweighted: a brand-new user staring at two empty text fields has no idea what this app even does, and "try the demo corridor" as a text link (what I proposed in round one) is a much weaker invitation than actually seeing pins on a small map and going "oh, that's what a flagged report looks like." So the map preview earns its place as an orientation device, not a decision device — it's showing "here's the kind of thing this app tracks," not "here's your answer," and I think that distinction is exactly what keeps it from violating the principle we both already agree on.

Where I'm not conceding is favorites living inside that same home shell as a permanent fixture rather than getting its own tab. Codex's version has origin, destination, recents, favorites, and a map preview all coexisting on one screen, and I think that's one too many jobs for a screen whose entire purpose is "get me to a route in ten seconds." Think about what happens once someone has hit the free cap and is a paying customer with, say, six saved routes — "favorite routes" stops being a two-item widget and becomes a real list that needs scrolling, renaming, deleting, maybe reordering. If that list lives permanently on the home screen, either it eats vertical space that used to belong to the map/search area, or it gets truncated to "see all," which just relocates the same navigation-depth problem Codex is trying to avoid, except now it's hidden inside a busier screen instead of being an honest second tab. A tab switch and a "see all" tap cost the same one interaction — the difference is that a tab makes Saved a real, stable place with its own identity (which matters for the paywall moment too: "2 of 2 free" as a persistent header on a dedicated screen reads very differently than a badge crammed into a home-screen widget). So I'd keep two tabs, Plan and Saved, but make Plan's top section show maybe the single most recent destination or top favorite as a one-tap shortcut row — not the whole list, just the fast path for the habitual daily case Codex correctly points out is the real everyday flow ("open, tap favorite, see today's suggested route, leave"). That gets the speed benefit of Codex's blended screen without the long-term clutter problem.

On the loading-state question the coordinator flagged — Codex's two-stage loading state (fetching the route, then scoring it against obstacles) is a genuinely good catch and I didn't address it in round one because I was focused on the result screen's success states, not its transition into them. I want to fully back this, and actually push it a little further: I think that two-stage loader is a legitimate branding opportunity, not just a spinner-politeness thing. If it just says "Loading..." we've wasted a moment that could be teaching the user what makes this app different from Apple Maps in the first five seconds they ever use it — "Finding your route" then "Checking for reported obstacles" makes the obstacle-intelligence layer visible as a distinct, deliberate step even before any result renders, which reinforces the whole value proposition before the user has read a single recommendation sentence. I'd lock that in as a real requirement, not a nice-to-have animation detail.

So here's where I think we actually land, cleanly: two tabs, Plan and Saved, with Plan carrying an orientation-only collapsed map (real content on first launch, showing the labeled demo corridor; showing current-location context after that) plus a single top shortcut to the most relevant recent/favorite destination, and Saved as its own full screen for real list management, the free-cap indicator, and the paywall trigger. Route Result stays exactly as both of us already described it — recommendation, why, confidence as three co-equal outputs, segmented Map/List rather than a screen push, and now a two-stage loading state that telegraphs both halves of what the app actually does before the answer even appears.

### Round 3


**Codex (Product Strategist · Closer) — Round 3**

I’m with Claude on the two-tab structure and with round-two Codex on keeping `Saved` as a real place, but I do not think the collapsed map strip belongs on `Plan`. That’s the last loop to close, and I want to close it cleanly instead of splitting the difference out of politeness.

The reason is simple: `Plan` is not an orientation screen, it’s an input screen. The user opens this app to answer “can I make this walk today?” not “what does this product category look like?” Claude’s argument for the mini-map is strongest on first launch, where two blank fields can feel abstract. Fair. But solving a first-launch teaching problem by permanently putting a MapKit surface on the planning screen is the wrong trade. It adds visual noise, introduces another stateful component before the core action even starts, and quietly nudges the app back toward “map app with accessibility extras,” which is exactly what we’ve spent this whole phase trying not to become. Worse, once permission is denied, or once the user is in a corridor with no visible reports, that little map strip becomes ambiguous dead weight: is it telling me something useful, or just occupying space while I’m trying to enter an address? The answer, most days, is the latter.

The cleaner move is to solve the onboarding/orientation problem directly instead of smearing it across the planner forever. Put the teaching into onboarding and into a compact first-launch demo card on `Plan`: something explicit like “See how route warnings work” with a clear CTA into the seeded corridor. After that, `Plan` stays fast and mostly textual: origin, destination, recents, one-tap shortcut row for the most relevant recent or favorite, trust banner, go. Then `Route Result` is where the map earns its place, because that’s the first moment spatial context actually helps the decision. That keeps the screen roles honest. `Plan` asks the question. `Route Result` answers it. `Saved` is the repeat-use library. Reporting/share/paywall are contextual sheets. And yes, keep Codex’s two-stage loading language exactly as stated: “finding walking route” then “checking reported obstacles.” That’s product-defining, not garnish.

So my vote is: two tabs, `Plan` and `Saved`; no persistent map strip on `Plan`; use onboarding plus a first-launch demo-corridor card to teach the model instead; keep a one-row shortcut to the most relevant saved/recent route for the habitual daily case; and let `Route Result` remain the first screen that shows a map at all. That gives the SwiftUI developer a cleaner hierarchy, keeps the app out of faux-navigation-app territory, and protects the one thing this product cannot afford to dilute: the recommendation sentence is the hero, and nothing upstream should compete with getting the user to it fast.

**Claude (Design Lead · Visionary) — Round 3**

Sitting with this overnight (well, for one round), I think Codex's round-two instinct to keep `Plan` textual was actually more right than my collapsed-map-strip counter, and I want to talk myself out of my own idea rather than wait for Codex to do it for me — because the more I picture building that mini-map, the more I think it's a worse answer to the exact problem I was trying to solve.

Here's the issue: a 25%-height MapKit view that's just sitting there for "orientation" is an awkward object. Is it interactive? If yes, now `Plan` needs pan/zoom/pin-tap handling and suddenly has real map logic living on a screen whose entire job, as Codex put it, is to "get the trip set quickly and get to the decision sentence" — that's scope bleeding into the one screen we both agreed should stay fast and calm. If it's not interactive, it's a static-feeling widget users will still try to touch, and a map that doesn't respond to touch reads as broken, not calm. Either way I've reintroduced exactly the kind of "faux-complete map home screen" Codex warned about in their very last message, just at 25% height instead of full-bleed. I don't think shrinking the map fixes the actual objection — it just makes the same mistake smaller.

But I don't want to fully retreat to "text-only demo-corridor affordance" either, because I still think a plain text link undersells the moment. A first-time user tapping a link that says "see a demo" and then reading a paragraph about sample data is a worse first impression than actually seeing what a flagged obstacle looks like. So here's the version I'd actually propose, and I think it's better than both of our round-two positions: instead of building a bespoke map surface just for `Plan`, reuse the screen we're already building anyway. On a genuinely fresh install with zero recents and zero favorites, `Plan` shows one tappable card — "See how it works: sample route in [City]" — and tapping it pushes straight into the real `Route Result` screen, fully populated with the demo corridor's seeded data, using the exact same recommendation/why/confidence card and the same segmented Map/List control a real search would produce. No separate widget, no half-interactive strip, no new component — just the onboarding "show, don't tell" instinct satisfied by pointing at the screen that already needs to exist and work perfectly anyway. That's a stronger demo too, honestly, because it's not a preview of a preview — it's the literal real experience with fake-but-labeled data standing in for real reports, which is exactly the "starter data, never live" framing we locked two phases ago.

That gives Codex the map-free `Plan` they argued for — no MapKit view, no visual weight competing with origin/destination, nothing for a repeat user to even notice once they have favorites and the demo card disappears — while still giving me the "show, don't just describe" moment I was chasing, just relocated to a screen that already has to support it. It also means `Plan` genuinely only needs to render: two text fields, a recents/favorite shortcut row, a trust banner, and conditionally one demo-entry card. That's a real, small, buildable screen, and I think it's a cleaner answer than either of us had going in. I'd call this the resolution: two tabs, `Plan` fully text-first with no map surface at all, first-run-only demo card that routes into a real populated `Route Result` rather than a custom preview widget, and everything else — the two-stage loader, the segmented Map/List on results, Saved as its own tab — locked exactly as we already agreed.

## Final Output

**Screen inventory:**
1. **Onboarding** — three cards max (what the app does, what it doesn't guarantee, how sample/local data works), ending in a location-permission step with an equal-weight "plan without my location" path.
2. **Plan** (tab 1) — text-first: origin field (current location or manual/dropped-pin, both first-class), destination search field, a single one-row shortcut to the most relevant recent/favorite destination, a persistent trust banner, and — only on a genuinely fresh install with zero recents/favorites — one tappable "see how it works" demo card. No map surface on this screen, ever.
3. **Destination/Origin Search** — modal or pushed screen: search results, recent places, manual pin-drop entry.
4. **Route Result** — the hero screen, pushed from Plan (real search) or from the fresh-install demo card (seeded corridor data). Recommendation sentence, confidence badge (icon + word, never color-only), plain-language why, all as co-equal outputs above the fold; a segmented `Map / List` control below (not a screen push); a compact route-switcher chip row when MapKit returns multiple routes, absent entirely for single-route results; warning chips distinguishing "on this route" vs "nearby."
5. **Obstacle detail** — bottom sheet from tapping a flagged item (map pin or list row): type, severity, recency, note, on-route/nearby status, inline report action.
6. **Report Obstacle** — bottom sheet, reachable from Route Result's persistent action or any flagged item: type chips, severity, optional note, "looks clear now" toggle when applicable, save (target: under 30 seconds).
7. **Saved** (tab 2) — full list-management screen for favorites: each row live-refetches and shows current status ("no new reported issues" / "N new reports since saved"), a persistent "2 of 2 free" indicator once at cap, share action per row.
8. **Paywall** — sheet triggered at the 3rd favorite save attempt, showing the actual route just tried to be saved underneath a translucent unlock panel; sells unlimited favorites + share export specifically.
9. **Share preview** — shown before invoking the system `ShareLink`, previewing exactly what the recipient will see (destination, recommendation, confidence, timestamp, unverified-data line).
10. **Settings/info** (toolbar affordance, not a tab) — full disclaimer text, sample-data explanation, restore purchases, privacy guidance on notes, demo-data reset.

**Primary user flow:**
First-time: onboarding → Plan (manual or current-location origin) → destination search → Route Result (two-stage load: "finding walking route" → "checking reported obstacles") → recommendation/why/confidence → inspect via Map or List → optionally report an obstacle (score updates live if returned to the same result) → optionally save as favorite or share. Repeat/habitual flow (the actual daily case): open app → tap favorite shortcut on Plan or in Saved → Route Result re-fetches live → check "no new issues" → leave. Permission-denied follows the identical flow with manual origin entry substituted, never a dead end.

**State model per screen (key states):**
- *Plan:* fresh-install-empty-with-demo-card; populated-with-recents/favorites; location-permission-denied (inline banner, not modal); destination-search-empty/ambiguous.
- *Route Result:* two-stage loading (fetch → score); success-multi-route; success-single-route (language shifts to "the only walking route found," switcher chips absent); success-zero-matched-obstacles ("no reported obstacles on this route," confidence shown separately, never "safe"); low-confidence (equal visual weight to the recommendation, not caption text); offline-new-search-failure (endpoints preserved, distinct honest copy); offline-cached-route (obstacle scoring still refreshes); geocoding failure (editable in place, no backward bounce); permission lost mid-session (falls back to manual origin with explanation).
- *Saved:* empty (no favorites yet); under-cap; at-cap (paywall intercept on next save attempt); favorite reopened (live re-fetch, "today's suggested route" framing).
- *Report sheet:* default entry; reporting against an existing flagged item (adds "looks clear now"); save success (visible live update on the underlying result if returned to it).
- *Paywall:* pre-purchase; purchasing; restore.

**Visual direction:** Calm, premium, trust-first — deep slate/ink rather than default navigation blue, muted teal/petrol for primary route emphasis, amber strictly for moderate flags, rust/coral strictly for severe on-route hazards, no green-means-safe semantics anywhere. Confidence is its own badge system (icon + word). Seeded/demo content gets a distinct, consistent visual treatment (subtle dashed outline or badge) so it's never mistaken for real data at a glance. Typography carries the "premium" weight: the recommendation sentence is the visual anchor of Route Result, generous spacing, built to survive largest Dynamic Type by growing card height, not truncating — hardest on Route Result and the paywall screen.

**Accessibility notes:** The List representation on Route Result is a first-class peer to Map, not a fallback — reached via segmented control, never a buried screen push. Travel-order sequencing is the default everywhere flagged items appear. VoiceOver labels encode obstacle type, severity, recency, and on-route/nearby relationship in a single pass per item. Color never solely carries the on-route vs. nearby distinction — always paired with icon/text. Report sheet uses large tap targets, minimal custom gestures, operable one-handed and with Switch Control. The trust disclaimer is persistently reachable from Route Result but doesn't dominate reading order on every screen open.

**Quality Gate (Claude) — after round 3**

QUALITY: PASS

## Feedback

This closes the phase cleanly. All five required outputs are present and concrete: a 10-item screen inventory (with each screen's contents and entry points specified, not just names), a primary flow covering both first-time and repeat/habitual paths plus the permission-denied variant, a state model per screen enumerating the specific named states from prior phases (single-route vs multi-route, zero-matched-obstacle, offline-new-search vs offline-cached-route, at-cap paywall intercept), a visual direction with concrete color semantics, and accessibility notes that specify mechanism (segmented control as peer, not fallback; travel-order sequencing; color never solo-carrying meaning).

Strengths:
- The IA fork flagged by the round-1/round-2 coordinator is genuinely resolved, not split down the middle: Codex moved off its blended-home-screen position, Claude talked itself out of its own map-strip counter-proposal, and the landing point (no MapKit view on `Plan` at all; first-launch teaching need met by routing into a fully-populated real `Route Result` with seeded data) is better than either round-2 position, not a compromise average.
- Every mechanism locked in `detailed_discussion` and `app_features` survives into concrete screen/state form: the 20m/10m buffer's "on this route"/"nearby" distinction appears as warning chips (item 4), the single-route honesty requirement gets its own named state, the two-favorite cap and paywall-shows-the-actual-item-being-unlocked requirement is a named screen behavior, ShareLink-not-push is reflected as a share-preview screen with no live-update implication, and the ShareLink/StoreKit split from `app_features` isn't quietly reopened.
- The two-stage loading state ("finding walking route" → "checking reported obstacles") — raised by Codex, extended by Claude into a branding/teaching moment — made it into the state model as a named state, not lost as a stray good idea.
- Accessibility notes are specific enough to build against: segmented control (not push) for List/Map parity, travel-order default, color-never-alone rule, Switch Control/one-handed operability for the report sheet — this satisfies the phase's own "structure, not decorate later" framing rather than restating generic a11y language.
- No contradictions introduced against `prompt_contract`, `per_app_product_brief`, `detailed_discussion`, or `app_features` — favorites-as-live-refetch, the fixed six-item obstacle vocabulary, the disclaimer's persistent-but-dismissible-only-at-onboarding rule, and the won't-build list (no live sync) are all consistent with what's rendered here.

Both acceptance checks pass: a SwiftUI developer has enough to build the view hierarchy (tab structure, sheet vs. push relationships, segmented control placement all specified), and every must-have workflow from `app_features` has an explicit screen/state path (manual origin entry, report-with-supersession, favorite-cap-to-paywall, single-route case, offline distinctions, VoiceOver list). Ready to hand to the design/architecture phase.
