4. **Route Result** — the hero screen, pushed from Plan (real search) or from the fresh-install demo card (seeded corridor data). Recommendation sentence, confidence badge (icon + word, never color-only), plain-language why, all as co-equal outputs above the fold; a segmented `Map / List` control below (not a screen push); a compact route-switcher chip row when MapKit returns multiple routes, absent entirely for single-route results; warning chips distinguishing "on this route" vs "nearby."
5. **Obstacle detail** — bottom sheet from tapping a flagged item (map pin or list row): type, severity, recency, note, on-route/nearby status, inline report action.
6. **Report Obstacle** — bottom sheet, reachable from Route Result's persistent action or any flagged item: type chips, severity, optional note, "looks clear now" toggle when applicable, save (target: under 30 seconds).
7. **Saved** (tab 2) — full list-management screen for favorites: each row live-refetches and shows current status ("no new reported issues" / "N new reports since saved"), a persistent "2 of 2 free" indicator once at cap, share action per row.
8. **Paywall** — sheet triggered at the 3rd favorite save attempt, showing the actual route just tried to be saved underneath a translucent unlock panel; sells unlimited favorites + share export specifically.
9. **Share preview** — shown before invoking the system `ShareLink`, previewing exactly what the recipient will see (destination, recommendation, confidence, timestamp, unverified-data line).
10. **Settings/info** (toolbar affordance, not a tab) — full disclaimer text, sample-data explanation, restore purchases, privacy guidance on notes, demo-data reset.

**Primary user flow:**
First-time: onboarding → Plan (manual or current-location origin) → destination search → Route Result (two-stage load: "finding walking route" → "checking reported obstacles") → recommendation/why/confidence → inspect via Map or List → optionally report an obstacle (score updates live if returned to the same result) → optionally save as favorite or share. Repeat/habitual flow (the actual daily case): open app → tap favorite shortcut on Plan or in Saved → Route Result re-fetches live → check "no new issues" → leave. Permission-denied follows the identical flow with manual origin entry substituted, never a dead end.

**State model per screen (key states):**
- *Plan:* fresh-install-empty-with-demo-card; populated-with-recents/favorites; location-permission-denied (inline banner, not modal); destination-search-empty/ambiguous.
- *Route Result:* two-stage loading (fetch → score); success-multi-route; success-single-route (language shifts to "the only walking route found," switcher chips absent); success-zero-matched-obstacles ("no reported obstacles on this route," confidence shown separately, never "safe"); low-confidence (equal visual weight to the recommendation, not caption text); offline-new-search-failure (endpoints preserved, distinct honest copy); offline-cached-route (obstacle scoring still refreshes); geocoding failure (editable in place, no backward bounce); permission lost mid-session (falls back to manual origin with explanation).
- *Saved:* empty (no favorites yet); under-cap; at-cap (paywall intercept on next save attempt); favorite reopened (live re-fetch, "today's suggested route" framing).
- *Report sheet:* default entry; reporting against an existing flagged item (adds "looks clear now"); save success (visible live update on the underlying result if returned to it).
- *Paywall:* pre-purchase; purchasing; restore.

**Visual direction:** Calm, premium, trust-first — deep slate/ink rather than default navigation blue, muted teal/petrol for primary route emphasis, amber strictly for moderate flags, rust/coral strictly for severe on-route hazards, no green-means-safe semantics anywhere. Confidence is its own badge system (icon + word). Seeded/demo content gets a distinct, consistent visual treatment (subtle dashed outline or badge) so it's never mistaken for real data at a glance. Typography carries the "premium" weight: the recommendation sentence is the visual anchor of Route Result, generous spacing, built to survive largest Dynamic Type by growing card height, not truncating — hardest on Route Result and the paywall screen.

**Accessibility notes:** The List representation on Route Result is a first-class peer to Map, not a fallback — reached via segmented control, never a buried screen push. Travel-order sequencing is the default everywhere flagged items appear. VoiceOver labels encode obstacle type, severity, recency, and on-route/nearby relationship in a single pass per item. Color never solely carries the on-route vs. nearby distinction — always paired with icon/text. Report sheet uses large tap targets, minimal custom gestures, operable one-handed and with Switch Control. The trust disclaimer is persistently reachable from Route Result but doesn't dominate reading order on every screen open.

---

CONSENSUS: YES

phase_discussions.title: Design Handoff
phase_discussions.output:
CONSENSUS: YES

Round two closed this out cleanly. Codex stopped hedging on the external-design question and came down firmly on Claude's side: no external Claude Design pass, this handoff itself is the build spec, full stop. Codex explicitly adopted essentially all of Claude's engineering catches from round one — the deliberate minimum-hold timing on the two-stage loader, lazy per-row refetching in Saved instead of a tab-open stampede, the hard separation between "fetch route geometry" (network, once) and "rescore against obstacles" (local, replayable), the persisted one-time flag for the demo card instead of a derived-from-current-data check, and the single-scrolling-container fix for Route Result at max Dynamic Type. Claude's round two reinforced the no-external-design position with a sharp argument (Codex's own draft prompt already proves the point — it can't carry the specific hex values, spacing scale, or behavioral rules that actually matter, so "send a prompt out" really means "reconstruct this whole document elsewhere and reconcile it back") and added three more concrete, uncontested technical locks: build the map with SwiftUI's native `Map`/`MapContentBuilder` API rather than a UIKit-bridged `MKMapView` (solves the lifecycle question outright), give any pulse/morph animation a Reduce-Motion-respecting static fallback given this app's audience, and keep the two-stage loading dwell time explicit (400–600ms) so it can't get "optimized away" as dead code. Nothing here pulls against Codex's position — it's the same direction, just more specific — and there's no remaining fork in the room.

## Final Output

**Decision on external design:** No external Claude Design pass. This handoff (Codex's screen-by-screen spec + Claude's architecture/token/state refinements, both below) is the build source of truth for direct SwiftUI implementation. If outside visual taste is wanted later, it's scoped narrowly as a post-build polish review of finished screens against the token system — never a reopened concept phase.

**Screen-by-screen specification:**

1. **Onboarding** — 3 cards, stop there: (1) what it does, framed around avoiding reported barriers, not fastest route; (2) what it doesn't guarantee — the unverified-data warning stated plainly; (3) how local/sample data works, including that some areas will have little or no data. Final action row: "Use my location" / "Plan without my location" as visually equal-weight choices (not primary/secondary styling) — manual origin is a core mode, not a fallback.

2. **Plan (tab 1)** — text-first, no map surface, ever. Origin field (current location or manual/pin), destination field, single primary action, one compact recent/favorite shortcut row, persistent quiet trust banner. On a genuinely fresh install (governed by a persisted `hasCompletedFirstRouteSearch` flag, never a live "zero recents/favorites" check — this stops the demo card from reappearing after a user deletes their favorites), one demo card: "See how route warnings work," which pushes straight into a fully populated real Route Result using seeded corridor data — no separate tutorial illustration.

3. **Search (origin/destination)** — pushed/modal screen: results, recent places, manual pin-drop path, explicit empty/ambiguous states. Permission-denied biases smoothly to manual entry, never framed as "degraded mode."

4. **Route Result (hero screen)** — built as **one scrolling container**, not a fixed header over a scrollable body (required for max Dynamic Type — otherwise the segmented control and content can get pushed off-screen). Order: recommendation sentence → confidence badge (icon+word) → why text, all one visual block; then on-route/nearby warning chips; then route-switcher chips (only when MapKit returns multiple routes — absent entirely, with honest "only walking route found" wording, when it returns one); then segmented Map/List control; then the selected representation's content. Map mode uses SwiftUI's native `Map`/`MapContentBuilder` (`MapPolyline`, `Annotation`, `MapCircle`) — not a `UIViewRepresentable`-wrapped `MKMapView` — so the Map segment's view is created/torn down normally by the view hierarchy when List is selected, no manual lifecycle management needed. List mode is travel-order sequential, full-sentence rows, a genuine first-class peer, not a fallback.
   - **Two-stage loading is a locked spec requirement, not an implementation footnote:** stage 1 "finding walking route" (real network wait), stage 2 "checking reported obstacles" holds for a minimum 400–600ms even when local scoring finishes in milliseconds — a deliberate, intentional delay so the app's differentiator is legible on first use, must not be "optimized away" as dead code.

5. **Obstacle detail** — bottom sheet: type, severity, age, note, route relationship, inline report action. Staleness must be legible here — trust comes from showing evidence age, not hiding it.

6. **Report Obstacle** — bottom sheet: fixed six type chips, severity as segmented control/button group (never a slider — hard to operate with Switch Control), optional note, "looks clear now" toggle when applicable. Save triggers **local rescoring of the currently-held route polyline** — never a second MKDirections call. This keeps offline reporting working and is what makes the "watch the recommendation update live" moment real; dismissing the sheet should produce a visible (but Reduce-Motion-respecting) update on the recommendation card.

7. **Saved (tab 2)** — full list-management screen. Rows refetch **lazily and staggered** (visible-first, per-row "checking today's route…" loading copy) — never all-at-once on tab appear, which would otherwise fire a pile of concurrent MapKit calls for a paid user with several saved routes. Persistent "2 of 2 free" indicator at cap; share action per row.

8. **Paywall** — sheet on 3rd favorite save attempt, shown over the actual route just being saved (translucent unlock panel, blocked action still visible behind it). Sells unlimited favorites + share export only — never implies routing/scoring/reporting are premium.

9. **Share preview** — shown before the system `ShareLink`: destination, recommendation sentence, confidence, timestamp, unverified-data line. Reads as a route snapshot, not a social card.

10. **Settings/info** (toolbar affordance) — full disclaimer, sample-data explanation, restore purchases, note-privacy guidance, demo-data reset.

**Design tokens:**
- Colors: ink `#12161C` (primary text/chrome), warm off-white `#F7F5F1` (background), petrol `#1F5C56` (single primary/interactive accent), amber `#B8860B` (moderate severity only), coral/rust `#B4432E` (severe on-route hazard only), slate-gray `#6B7280` (secondary/meta text). No color is reused for a second meaning anywhere — severity colors never double as confidence colors; confidence is always icon+word, never color-alone. No green-means-safe semantics anywhere.
- Spacing: 4/8/12/16/24/32 base scale. Corner radius: 16pt primary cards, 12pt chips/pills. Minimum tap target: 44×44pt everywhere.
- Type: system font throughout (correct Dynamic Type metrics, no custom font risk); recommendation sentence set at a distinct larger/semibold style so it visually anchors the screen even before color does anything.

**Component inventory:** RecommendationCard, ConfidenceBadge (3 fixed states: low/moderate/high, icon+word), WarningChip/ObstacleChip (icon-based on-route/nearby distinction, never color-alone), SeverityIndicator (amber/coral only, separate channel from confidence), RouteSwitcherChip, TrustBanner, ObstacleRow, ObstacleDetailSheet, ReportSheet, FavoriteRow, DemoCard (dashed-outline treatment, applied consistently everywhere seeded data renders, including inside List mode), SharePreviewCard, PaywallUnlockPanel, standard search cells. Deliberately kept small — no decorative component variety for its own sake.

**State & interaction notes:**
- Plan: fresh-install-with-demo-card (flag-driven, one-time only), populated, permission-denied (inline banner), search-empty/ambiguous.
- Route Result: two-stage loading (with the minimum-hold rule above), multi-route success, single-route success (honest wording, no switcher chips), zero-matched-obstacle ("no reported obstacles," never "safe," confidence shown separately), low-confidence (equal visual weight to the recommendation), offline-new-search-failure (endpoints preserved, distinct copy), offline-cached-route-review (rescoring still works locally), geocoding failure (editable in place), permission-lost-mid-session (falls back to manual origin).
- Saved: empty, under-cap, at-cap (paywall intercept), reopened favorite (live re-fetch of route geometry, "today's suggested route" framing), lazy staggered per-row refresh.
- Report sheet: default, reporting-against-existing-obstacle (adds "looks clear now"), save triggers local rescore + visible card update.
- Architecture notes carried into build: per-tab `NavigationStack`s (not one global stack); Route Result driven by a single `@Observable` view model holding fetched route geometry + independently recomputed score/confidence + segment selection; `RouteScorer`/`ObstacleMatcher` operate on plain Swift structs (not SwiftData `@Model` types directly) so boundary-value tests stay fast and dependency-free; `LocationService` wraps `CLLocationManager`, `@MainActor`-isolated, authorization exposed as observable state.

**Accessibility requirements:** Dynamic Type at largest accessibility sizes with no truncation on recommendation/why/confidence/paywall copy (enabled by the single-scroll-container fix above). VoiceOver reading order on Route Result: recommendation → confidence → why → route selector (if present) → representation toggle → content. List rows are fully composed sentences, not fragmented sublabels. On-route vs. nearby never depends on color alone. Every custom control (chips, badges, segmented control) gets an explicit `accessibilityLabel`/`accessibilityValue` written out, not left to default inference. Report sheet fully usable one-handed and with Switch Control. Disclaimer reachable from Route Result at any time, without hijacking reading order on every open.

**Motion notes:** Restrained, purposeful, never playful — no spring-bounce anywhere on Route Result. Two-stage loading transitions with the locked minimum-hold timing. Map/List switch crossfades content (not a slide, since no spatial navigation is actually happening) while the recommendation block stays fixed at top. Report-save triggers a subtle recommendation-card update animation (opacity/background flash, <300ms). Severe-hazard emphasis may use a small pulse on first appearance. **All of the above must respect Reduce Motion** (`accessibilityReduceMotion`) — falling back to a static, non-animated color/icon change — given this app's audience skews toward users more likely to have motion-sensitivity settings enabled.
phase_discussions.discussion_markdown:
# clearpath-access — Design Handoff

_Generated by the autonomous multi-agent orchestrator on 2026-07-07 01:58:32._

## Original Prompt

```
PORTFOLIO_CHILD_PROJECT: true
Parent portfolio: batch-cat-b1
Selected app slug: clearpath-access

Build this app as a working local-first SwiftUI MVP.

## Selected App Spec

# Clearpath Access

Build mode: **MVP build**.

## App Name

Clearpath Access

## Category

Navigation

## One-Sentence Promise

Accessibility-aware pedestrian routing with community-sourced obstacle reporting

## Claude Design Handoff Prompt

(not supplied yet)

## Parent Portfolio Prompt

Build 9 completely separate production-ready iOS apps at the same time: exactly 3 Health & Fitness apps, 3 Medical apps, and 3 Navigation apps.

This is a multi-app PORTFOLIO request: multiple apps in one program. Each selected app becomes its own separate project — one folder per app.


Requirements for every app:
1. Must go from 0 to production-ready, not just a few features.
2. Must be unique, useful, and commercially viable.
3. Must be beautifully designed and feel premium.
4. Must provide real end-user value.
5. Must have a realistic path to monetization — a subscription or paid offering with genuine value (never cosmetics-only).
6. Should have viral potential (broad or niche) without sacrificing usefulness.
7. Must be better than its competitors in a meaningful way.
8. Local-first where applicable, architected so cloud support can be added later without rewriting.

UNIQUENESS (hard rule, non-negotiable):
- No app in this batch may be similar to another app in this batch.
- No app may be similar to anything already built or being built in this workspace or before, including: Waylay (location-based personal recall utility), TrueScale, Provenance, Scope-Guard, Practice-Loop, Proof, ReturnWise, VerveCoach, CueKeeper, Countertop, FieldQuote, my existing digital temple app, and my existing PO-automation tool.
- No app may be similar to apps in the OTHER batches of this program (check sibling project folders in the workspace for their concepts).
- Do not reuse prior concepts, themes, or mechanics from earlier apps — even if the request sounds similar to something built before, produce a differentiated concept.

Bonus points (optional — only when integral and genuinely valuable, never forced): unique use of LLMs, AR, or ML.

Build rules:
- Run the app efforts in parallel; keep discussion/design phases separate per app.
- Each app gets its own full product direction, design direction, architecture, implementation plan, and build output.
- Save all phase discussions and final decisions; at the end combine each app's full transcript into a .txt file.
- If an app is liked, also prepare Jira board + Notion project structures for backfilling later.
- Use enough rounds per phase for high-quality discussion, ending early on consensus.
- Recover cleanly from stalls; report only important milestones.

Output:
- One folder per app in the output directory — never one folder containing all apps.
Output directory:
- /Users/pchordia/Documents/iOS-App-Factory

Additional notes:
Medical/health apps must frame outputs as wellness insights and validated risk scores (e.g. QRISK3, FINDRISC style), never diagnosis.

Now generate at least 18 candidate ideas, discuss them thoroughly, choose the best 9, and build all 9 to production quality.
```

## Phase Purpose

Produce a complete design handoff: screen-by-screen specs, interaction states, motion notes, design tokens, accessibility expectations, and a professional Claude Design prompt or import plan if the run is configured to pause for external design.

## Transcript


### Round 1


**Codex (Design Lead · Visionary) — Round 1**

I’d design this as a trust-first navigation product, not a map product. The interface should feel like it is helping someone make one high-stakes micro-decision before leaving the house: “What’s the best walk today, what could go wrong, and how sure are you?” That means the visual hierarchy cannot be map-first. The first thing the user should read on the result screen is a recommendation sentence, the second is confidence, the third is why. The map is evidence. The list is evidence. Neither is the product.

The app structure should stay very small: onboarding, a two-tab shell with Plan and Saved, contextual sheets for reporting/share/paywall/obstacle detail, and a lightweight settings/info surface. That is enough. Anything beyond that starts to make this feel like a generic mobility map utility instead of a premium route-confidence tool.

Onboarding should be three screens and stop. First card: what it does, phrased around avoiding reported barriers rather than finding the fastest route. Second card: what it does not guarantee, with the unverified-data warning stated plainly and without hedging. Third card: how local data and sample data work, including the fact that some areas will have little or no data. The final action row should present “Use my location” and “Plan without my location” as equal-weight choices, not primary/secondary styling. That parity matters because manual origin entry is not an error fallback here; it is a core mode.

Plan should be text-first and almost severe in its restraint. No map surface. Top area: origin field with current-location or manual entry state, destination field, and a single main action. Under that: one compact shortcut row showing the most relevant recent or saved destination. Under that: the persistent trust banner, always visible but quiet. Only on a truly fresh install with no recents and no favorites should a demo card appear, and it should not explain the product abstractly. It should say something like “See how route warnings work” and drop the user directly into a fully populated sample Route Result using the seeded corridor. That is better than any tutorial illustration because it reuses the actual screen that has to sell the product anyway.

The search surface for origin and destination should be a focused modal or pushed screen, not a cluttered inline expander. It should contain search results, recent places, a manual pin-drop entry path, and explicit empty/error states. The pin-drop mode should feel deliberate and calm, not like an obscure developer utility. If location permission is denied, the app should smoothly bias toward manual origin without ever making the user feel they are in a degraded “limited mode.”

Route Result is the hero and should be built like a briefing card with spatial proof beneath it. Above the fold: a dominant recommendation sentence, then a confidence badge with icon plus label, then a one- to two-line why statement. All three should read as one block. Beneath that: small warning chips for “On this route” and “Nearby.” Beneath that: a segmented control for Map and List, with the List treated as a first-class mode, not an accessibility afterthought. If multiple routes exist, a compact chip row sits between the summary block and the segment control so alternative routes can be swapped in place without leaving the screen. If only one route exists, that chip row disappears completely and the wording in the summary changes honestly to reflect that this is the only walking route found. The screen must never imply comparative intelligence when no comparison exists.

The Map mode should be visually quiet. The route overlays and obstacle states should do the work, not the basemap. The recommended route gets the strongest teal/petrol treatment, alternates are muted, and severe on-route hazards get rust/coral emphasis. Nearby hazards should be visibly lower emphasis than on-route hazards and should never share identical shape, icon, or label semantics. Tapping a flag opens the obstacle detail sheet.

The List mode should be excellent enough that a user could ignore the map forever and still feel they used the full app. Items should be ordered strictly by travel progression, not severity. Each row should speak in natural language: type, severity, freshness, and relationship to route. Each row should include an inline affordance to inspect details or report an update. If the product is serious about accessibility, this list cannot feel like the “fallback version”; it has to feel arguably better than the map for understanding risk quickly.

Obstacle detail should be a bottom sheet, not a full screen. It needs type, severity, age, note, and route relationship immediately visible, plus a direct action to report an update. This sheet should make staleness legible, because that is where the app earns trust: not by pretending certainty, but by showing how old or sparse the evidence is.

The report flow should be a fast bottom sheet with six fixed type chips, a severity selector, optional note, and the “looks clear now” option when invoked from an existing obstacle context. This sheet should be optimized for one-hand use and fast completion. Save should trigger a visible update on the underlying Route Result if the user returns there. That live score change is the app’s magic trick; design should spotlight it. A subtle pulse or content morph in the recommendation card is enough. Do not hide it behind a silent recompute.

Saved should be its own tab because recurring trips are not edge behavior here; they are the habit loop. Each saved row should feel alive, not archived: route name, destination, current status summary, last refresh context, and share action. Once the free cap is reached, the screen should carry a persistent but non-obnoxious “2 of 2 free saved routes used” indicator. If a user tries to save a third, the paywall should appear over the exact route they were saving, with the blocked action still visible in the background. The value proposition should be concrete: unlimited saved routes and share export. Nothing else.

The paywall should look like part of the product, not a monetization template. It should use the blocked route as context and explain why someone with recurring accessibility-sensitive trips would want more than two saved routes. The copy should never imply that core routing or reporting are premium. Those remain free, because gating the trust loop would poison the product.

Share preview should exist before the system share sheet. It should show exactly what is being shared: destination, recommendation sentence, confidence, timestamp, and the unverified-data warning. It should feel like a route snapshot, not a social share card. The moment should signal care and responsibility, not virality.

Settings/info can stay compact, but it has to contain the full disclaimer, sample-data explanation, restore purchases, note privacy guidance, and demo reset. This is a utility screen, not a brand playground.

The state model needs to be aggressively explicit because this app’s biggest UX risk is conflating meaning. On Plan: fresh install with demo card, populated state, permission denied, search ambiguity, search empty. On Route Result: first-stage loading for route fetch, second-stage loading for obstacle scoring, multi-route success, single-route success, zero-matched-obstacle success, low-confidence success, offline new search failure, offline cached-route review, geocoding failure, and permission lost mid-session. These must all look and read differently. “No reported obstacles” cannot look like “offline.” “Low confidence” cannot look like “moderate friction.” “Single route found” cannot look like “recommended over alternatives.” If these collapse visually, the app becomes misleading even with correct logic underneath.

The visual system should avoid default map-app cues. I’d use a warm off-white or soft mineral background, deep slate/ink for text, petrol/teal for primary route emphasis, amber for moderate caution, and rust/coral for severe on-route hazards. No green for “safe,” ever. Confidence should have its own neutral semantic system with icon plus word rather than traffic-light coding. Demo/sample content should use a distinct badge or dashed treatment everywhere it appears so nobody mistakes it for real local coverage. The typography should carry a lot of the premium feeling: a large, high-contrast recommendation line, roomy spacing, and card layouts that grow vertically at large Dynamic Type sizes instead of truncating.

The component set is small and should stay disciplined: route recommendation card, confidence badge, why block, trust banner, warning chips, route-switcher chips, segmented Map/List control, obstacle row, obstacle detail sheet, report sheet, saved route row, demo card, paywall overlay, share snapshot preview, and standard search cells. That’s enough to build a coherent language across the app. I would not introduce decorative component variety just to make screens feel “finished.”

Motion should be restrained but meaningful. Onboarding cards can slide with slight depth but nothing playful. The Route Result two-stage loading should transition in a way that teaches the app’s logic: first route line appears, then obstacle annotations and summary language resolve. Switching Map/List should preserve the recommendation block fixed at top while the lower representation crossfades or slides horizontally. Saving a route should have a crisp confirmation. Reporting a new obstacle should trigger a subtle re-evaluation animation in the recommendation card so the user sees cause and effect. Severe warnings can use a small pulse on first appearance, but nothing that feels alarmist or game-like.

Accessibility constraints need to be treated as structural requirements. Dynamic Type up to the largest accessibility sizes with no truncation on recommendation, why, confidence, or paywall copy. VoiceOver reading order on Route Result must be recommendation, confidence, why, route selector if present, representation toggle, then content. List rows need fully composed labels, not fragmented sublabels. On-route versus nearby must never depend on color alone. Hit targets must stay large across chips and sheet controls. The report flow should be fully usable with Switch Control and one-handed reach. The disclaimer must be reachable from Route Result at any time, but it should not hijack the reading order every time the screen appears.

The biggest risk in this direction is over-indexing on calm restraint and ending up slightly too austere, especially on Plan. If that screen becomes so stripped down that it feels empty rather than intentional, the app will read as unfinished. The fix is not adding a map back. The fix is making the demo card, trust banner, and recommendation typography on Route Result feel unmistakably designed and premium.

The detail that absolutely has to survive the phase is this: the recommendation block on Route Result is the product. It must always present recommendation, confidence, and why as co-equal outputs, with the map and list serving as evidence beneath it. If that hierarchy breaks, the app turns into a misleading accessibility-themed maps UI, which is exactly what it cannot afford to be.

If external Claude Design is desired, I would hand it this prompt directly:

Design a premium iPhone app called Clearpath Access, a trust-first pedestrian routing app for wheelchair users, cane users, rollator users, stroller-pushers, and caregivers. The app is local-first and uses Apple walking routes plus locally stored obstacle reports to recommend the least risky route, not the shortest route. The core UX principle: the hero is a recommendation sentence with explicit confidence and plain-language why, not the map.

Design for SwiftUI on iPhone, iOS 17+, with these screens:
Onboarding with 3 cards max: what it does, what it does not guarantee, how sample/local data works, ending with equal-weight actions for Use My Location and Plan Without My Location.
Plan tab: no map, text-first, origin field, destination field, one-row recent/favorite shortcut, persistent trust banner, and only on fresh install a demo card that opens a fully populated sample route result using seeded fictional corridor data.
Search screen for origin/destination: results, recent places, manual pin-drop path, clear empty and error states.
Route Result: recommendation sentence, confidence badge, why text above the fold; warning chips for on-route vs nearby; segmented Map/List control; alternative route chips only when multiple routes exist; honest single-route wording when only one route exists.
Obstacle detail bottom sheet.
Report obstacle bottom sheet with fixed type chips, severity, optional note, and looks clear now option.
Saved tab with live status rows, share action, and free cap indicator.
Paywall sheet shown on 3rd favorite save attempt, selling unlimited favorites plus share export.
Share preview before native ShareLink.
Settings/info screen with disclaimer, sample-data explanation, privacy guidance, restore purchases, demo reset.

Visual direction: calm, premium, trust-first, not a generic maps app, not clinical. Use deep slate/ink text, soft mineral or warm light backgrounds, petrol/teal for primary route emphasis, amber for moderate caution, rust/coral for severe on-route hazards. Never use green to imply safety. Confidence must use icon plus word, not color alone. Demo/sample data needs a distinct visual treatment so it cannot be mistaken for real local coverage.

Interaction and state requirements: distinct UI for route fetch loading and obstacle scoring loading; distinct UI for no route found, no reported obstacles, low confidence, offline new search failure, offline cached route review, geocoding failure, permission denied, permission lost mid-session, multi-route vs single-route results. Route Result should support a fully first-class List mode that can stand on its own without the map.

Accessibility requirements: large Dynamic Type with no truncation, VoiceOver-friendly reading order, list rows that read as complete sentences, non-color distinction for on-route vs nearby, large hit targets, one-handed report flow, Switch Control-friendly sheets.

Generate a screen-by-screen visual handoff with layout hierarchy, spacing intent, component definitions, color and typography tokens, and motion notes suitable for direct SwiftUI implementation.

**Claude (Frontend Engineer · Skeptic) — Round 1**

I want to start by pressure-testing three things from the locked design phase before I add anything new, because if these don't hold up architecturally, the rest of the handoff inherits false confidence.

First, the two-stage loading state ("finding walking route" then "checking reported obstacles") got treated as a branding win in the design phase, and I think that's dangerously optimistic without a build-side caveat. MKDirections.calculate() is a real network round-trip, but obstacle scoring against a cached SwiftData store within a 20m buffer is a local computation that will often finish in under 50ms. If we render that second stage honestly — show it only as long as it actually takes — it'll flash and read as a glitch, not a deliberate step. The fix has to be explicit in the handoff: the "checking reported obstacles" stage gets an artificial minimum hold (something like 400–600ms) so it's perceivable, and that's a product decision, not an implementation footnote, because it means we're deliberately delaying a result the app already has. If nobody writes that down now, whoever builds this will either cut the stage silently or ship a flicker and call it a bug later.

Second, "favorites re-fetch live, never a frozen polyline" sounds clean in a product discussion and becomes a real problem the moment someone has more than two or three saved routes, which is the entire point of the paid tier. If the Saved tab eagerly triggers a live MKDirections call plus obstacle rescoring for every row the instant the tab appears, a user with eight saved routes just fired eight concurrent network requests and eight polyline-distance computations on tab switch. That's a battery and rate-limit risk, and it also means "no new reported issues" badges will pop in staggered over a second or two, which needs its own loading-per-row state, not a blocking spinner over the whole list. My position: refetch is lazy and staggered — visible rows only, one row's status computed as it scrolls into view or on a short-lived visibility trigger, not all-at-once on tab appear. This needs to be named as a state in the Saved screen spec, not left as an implementation detail, because it changes what "the list" looks like in the first 500ms after opening it.

Third, and this is the one I feel strongest about: the design phase talks about a report "changing the score of a route re-requested immediately after," and I want to kill any reading of "re-request" that implies calling MapKit again. Resubmitting to MKDirections after a report is wasteful, adds a second network dependency into a flow that's supposed to work offline, and actually breaks the offline-cached-route state that was already named as a requirement — you can't refetch a route you have no network for, but you can absolutely rescore an already-fetched polyline against updated local obstacle data. So the architecture has to cleanly separate "fetch route geometry" (network, MapKit, happens once per search or favorite-open) from "score route against obstacles" (pure local computation, replayable any number of times against the same cached polyline). The report sheet's dismissal should trigger a rescore of whatever route is currently held in view state, never a new directions call. If this separation isn't explicit in the handoff, someone will wire "submit report" to just call the whole search pipeline again, which will silently break offline reporting and probably introduce a network dependency into what was supposed to be the local-first core loop.

On state management shape: I'd anchor this on a per-tab NavigationStack (Plan's stack and Saved's stack are independent path arrays), not one global stack, because tapping a favorite from Saved and tapping a destination from Plan both push a Route Result screen, and if there's only one shared stack, backgrounding mid-flow or switching tabs mid-push gets ambiguous about which tab "owns" that pushed screen. Route Result itself should be driven by a single `@Observable` view model holding both the fetched route (or nil), the obstacle score/confidence result (recomputed independently), and the current segment selection (map/list) — and MKMapView should not be instantiated at all while the List segment is selected. Keeping a live MapKit view resident just because the user is looking at the list is unnecessary memory and rendering overhead in an app that already has exactly one screen where a map appears at all; lazy-load it only when the Map segment is chosen, and let it deallocate (not just hide) if the user backs out.

I also want to flag the demo-card visibility rule as under-specified in a way that will produce a confusing bug. The design phase said the "see how it works" card on Plan appears only "on a genuinely fresh install with zero recents and zero favorites." If that condition is derived live from current data state rather than from an explicit persisted flag, then a user who saves and later deletes both of their favorites will see the onboarding demo card reappear, which reads as broken, not helpful. This needs one boolean in UserDefaults or a lightweight settings record — `hasCompletedFirstRouteSearch` — set once and never unset, so "fresh install" is a真 one-time state, not a recomputed one.

One more architectural point worth locking now because it affects testability, which this app's whole product promise depends on: the scoring/obstacle-matching engine (buffer math, severity×recency formula, confidence derivation) must operate on plain Swift structs, not directly on SwiftData `@Model` types. Point-to-polyline distance calculation (there's no built-in MapKit API for "distance from point to route" — it has to be computed by projecting the obstacle coordinate onto each segment of the route polyline and taking the minimum) is exactly the kind of logic that's easy to get subtly wrong at the 19m/21m boundary, and it needs to run in unit tests with zero SwiftData/ModelContainer setup, zero MapKit dependency, fast and deterministic. So: `RouteScorer` and `ObstacleMatcher` take `RoutePolyline` and `ObstacleReport` value types as plain input/output, and the SwiftData layer maps into and out of those at the edges. If this boundary gets blurred — if scoring logic ends up reaching into `@Query` results directly — the boundary-value tests this whole project has been promising since the detailed-discussion phase become integration tests in disguise, slower and more brittle than they need to be.

For location, I'd name a concrete component: a `LocationService` wrapping `CLLocationManager`, `@MainActor`-isolated, exposing authorization status as an `@Observable` property so permission-revoked-mid-session is just a state change the Plan and Route Result views react to, not a manual polling check. Authorization change delivery from CoreLocation is delegate-based, so this needs an explicit bridge into the actor-isolated observable state per the standard main-actor-hop pattern, not a raw delegate touching UI state directly.

Screen-by-screen, layered onto the already-locked inventory: Route Result at maximum Dynamic Type is the single hardest layout problem in the app, and I don't think a fixed VStack survives it. The recommendation sentence, confidence badge, and why-text can each grow substantially, and if the segmented Map/List control and the content below it are laid out in a rigid stack, accessibility-size text will push the control and the map/list content off-screen on an SE-class device with no way to reach it. This screen needs to be a ScrollView with the recommendation/why/confidence block, the segmented control, and the map-or-list content all scrolling together — not a fixed header with a scrollable region underneath — otherwise "grows the card, doesn't truncate" quietly becomes "grows the card off the bottom of the screen."

Design tokens, concretely, so this isn't just adjectives: base palette — ink `#12161C` for primary text and dominant UI chrome, a warm off-white `#F7F5F1` background (not pure white, keeps the "premium utility" not "clinical" framing already agreed), petrol `#1F5C56` as the single primary/interactive accent (buttons, links, selected segment), amber `#B8860B`-range strictly for moderate-severity chips, coral/rust `#B4432E` strictly for severe on-route hazards, and a neutral slate-gray `#6B7280` for secondary/meta text (timestamps, "nearby" labels). No color in this list is reused for a second meaning anywhere in the app — severity colors never double as confidence colors, which matters because confidence already has to be icon+word, never color-alone, per the locked accessibility rule. Spacing scale: 4/8/12/16/24/32 base unit system, card corner radius 16pt for primary cards (recommendation card, favorite rows, paywall panel), 12pt for chips/pills, minimum tap target 44×44pt everywhere including report-sheet type chips. Type: system font (San Francisco) throughout for correct Dynamic Type behavior — no custom font risking a poor accessibility-size metric — with the recommendation sentence set at a distinct, larger text style (`.title3`/`.headline` weight, semibold) so it visually anchors the screen even before color does anything.

Component inventory worth naming explicitly rather than leaving implicit: RecommendationCard (headline sentence + confidence badge + why-text, used only on Route Result), ConfidenceBadge (icon+word, three fixed states: low/moderate/high, reused nowhere else so it never collides with severity color), ObstacleChip (type icon + on-route/nearby distinction via icon, not color alone), SeverityIndicator (separate from ConfidenceBadge, amber/coral only), FavoriteRow (name, live status text, share affordance, tap target for reopen), DemoBadge (dashed-outline treatment, applied consistently anywhere seeded data renders, including inside the list view — not just on map pins), TrustBanner (persistent, low-visual-weight but always tappable to expand full disclaimer text), and PaywallUnlockPanel (translucent overlay over the real content being gated, not a generic modal).

Motion should be restrained and purposeful, not decorative, given the trust-first framing: the recommendation card's live-update after a report submission should use a brief highlight/pulse (opacity or background flash on the changed value, under 300ms) so the "aha, it just updated" moment is visible without being flashy; segment control switches between Map/List should crossfade content rather than slide, since sliding implies spatial navigation that isn't actually happening; the two-stage loading transition should be a simple text crossfade, not a skeleton-loader animation, keeping with the "plain, honest" visual tone rather than borrowing consumer-app polish that would undercut the trust framing. No spring-bounce anywhere on Route Result — overshoot animation reads as playful, which fights the "considered, calm" tone the visual direction agreed on.

Accessibility beyond what's already locked: every custom control (ObstacleChip, ConfidenceBadge, segmented Map/List control) needs an explicit `accessibilityLabel`/`accessibilityValue` pair written out in the handoff rather than left to SwiftUI's default inference, since default inference on icon-plus-text composite views is exactly where VoiceOver dead-ends happen in practice. The report sheet's severity control should be implemented as a segmented control or large button group, not a slider — sliders are genuinely hard to operate precisely with Switch Control and VoiceOver, and severity is a small fixed set of values anyway.

On the Claude Design prompt question: I don't think this app should go out to an external design pass at all. Three full phases have already produced a specific, opinionated, internally consistent visual and IA direction — palette, spacing, screen states, component list — and the biggest risk of route this through external Claude Design now isn't quality, it's drift: an external pass optimizing for visual appeal could easily reintroduce the exact things this project spent three rounds ruling out (color-as-confidence, a map-heavy planning screen, generic navigation-blue). My vote is explicit no-external-design: hand this spec plus the token values directly to SwiftUI implementation, treating this document itself as the design source of truth. If the group wants a second opinion on visual polish specifically, I'd rather scope that as a narrow "review these six SwiftUI screens against the token system" pass late in build, not a from-scratch design handoff that has to be reconciled back against everything already locked.

**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 1**

CONSENSUS: NO

Here's where the room stands after round one: there's a huge amount of agreement on substance, but one real fork just opened up, plus a batch of technical specifics that haven't been cross-checked yet.

**What's solid:** Both agree completely on the core design philosophy — recommendation sentence first, confidence second, why third, map and list are just evidence beneath that. Both keep the same screen inventory, the same two-tab shell, the same "no MapKit on Plan" call, the same distinct-states-must-look-different principle (single-route vs multi-route, zero-matched vs offline, etc.), the same palette direction (ink/petrol/amber/coral, never green-for-safe, confidence as icon+word not color). Codex wrote this up as a full screen-by-screen spec with tokens, components, motion, and accessibility notes — genuinely thorough and consistent with everything locked in prior phases.

**The one real disagreement:** Codex drafted a ready-to-use Claude Design handoff prompt "if external design is desired" — leaving the door open without picking a side. Claude took a firm position against that: three phases already produced a specific, internally consistent design direction, and sending it out for an external design pass risks quietly reintroducing exactly what got ruled out (color-as-confidence, a map-heavy Plan screen, generic maps-blue). Claude's vote is to skip external design entirely and treat this handoff itself as the source of truth for direct SwiftUI build, with maybe a narrow late-stage "check these six screens against the token system" pass instead. Codex hasn't weighed in on that specific argument yet since it came after.

**Also unresolved — Codex hasn't had a chance to react:** Claude raised several concrete engineering catches that change details of the spec rather than the vision: the two-stage loading state needs an artificial minimum hold time on the second stage or it'll flicker instead of read as deliberate; favorites in the Saved tab need lazy/staggered refetching, not all-at-once on tab appear, or a paid user with several saved routes fires a pile of concurrent requests; "report changes the score on re-request" has to mean local rescoring against the already-fetched route, never a second MapKit call, to keep offline reporting working; the demo card's visibility needs to be a persisted one-time flag, not derived live from current favorite/recent counts, or deleting your last favorite would make onboarding reappear; and Route Result needs to be a single scrolling container (not a fixed header + scrollable body) so max Dynamic Type doesn't push content off-screen. Claude also proposed specific hex tokens, a component list, and motion notes as refinements on top of Codex's version.

None of this contradicts Codex's spec — it's mostly sharpening it — but it needs Codex's sign-off (or pushback) before it's locked, and the external-design question needs an actual decision one way or the other.

## Final Output
Not yet — next round needs Codex to respond to two things: (1) take a real position on the external-design question (agree to skip it and treat this as the build spec, or make a case for still sending a prompt out), and (2) confirm or contest Claude's engineering-level additions (loading-state minimum hold, lazy staggered favorites refetch, rescore-not-refetch separation, persisted demo-card flag, the scrolling-container fix for Route Result, and the proposed token/component specifics).

### Round 2


**Codex (Design Lead · Visionary) — Round 2**

Claude’s pushback is mostly right, and I think the useful move here is to stop pretending there’s still a “design vs engineering” split on these details. They are design decisions now because they change what the user perceives. I’m with Claude on skipping an external Claude Design pass. Three phases already locked the thing that matters most: this app is a recommendation product with map evidence, not a map product with accessibility garnish. That is an unusually fragile design premise. A fresh external pass will be tempted to “improve” it back into a more familiar navigation app, because familiar map UX patterns are seductive and visually legible. That would be drift, not help. So I’d make the no-external-design call explicitly and treat this handoff as the build source of truth. If we want outside taste later, it should be a narrow polish review against the built SwiftUI screens, not a reopened concept phase.

On Claude’s implementation catches: I’d adopt almost all of them, because they sharpen the handoff instead of changing its soul. The two-stage loader absolutely needs a deliberate minimum dwell on the second phase or it becomes a flicker and teaches nothing. I would write that straight into the motion spec: “finding walking route” resolves first, then “checking reported obstacles” holds long enough to read, even if local scoring is faster. That is a small, intentional delay in service of clarity, and I think it is worth it because it makes the app’s differentiator legible in the first-use moment. Same with Saved: Claude is right that “every row live-refetches” cannot mean “slam MKDirections for the whole list on tab open.” That is not just a performance issue; it produces visually noisy status churn in the exact screen that is supposed to feel dependable. So the Saved spec should say rows resolve lazily, visible-first, with per-row loading copy like “checking today’s route…” rather than a screen-blocking spinner. That keeps the list calm and honest.

The biggest thing I want to lock from Claude’s note is the route-fetch versus route-score separation. That should be explicit in the handoff because it directly affects the feel of the report loop. Reporting an obstacle should not feel like “start over and wait for directions again.” It should feel like “the advice just updated because the evidence changed.” That means the current Route Result owns a fetched route geometry and can be rescored locally, repeatedly, without another network call. Design-wise, that gives us a much stronger interaction too: the report sheet dismisses, the recommendation card subtly morphs, the confidence/why text updates, maybe the warning chips change, and the map/list evidence refreshes in place. That is a better product moment than a full reload spinner anyway. I’d also take Claude’s point on the demo card condition: it should be governed by a one-time persisted “has completed first planning flow” flag, not by whether the user currently has zero favorites/recents, because resurfacing a first-run teaching card later would feel amateur.

I also buy Claude’s warning about Route Result needing to scroll as one vertical experience at large Dynamic Type. That’s not a layout implementation quirk; it changes the screen model. The recommendation block, route-switcher chips, Map/List control, and the selected representation all need to live in one scroll container so accessibility sizes don’t turn the segmented control into unreachable chrome below a giant fixed header. That keeps the hierarchy intact: recommendation first, evidence beneath, but all reachable. Where I’d push back slightly is only on over-formalizing every low-level component. We should name the important ones, yes, but not turn the handoff into a design system for its own sake. The reusable set is already enough: RecommendationCard, ConfidenceBadge, TrustBanner, WarningChip, RouteSwitcherChip, ObstacleRow, ObstacleDetailSheet, ReportSheet, FavoriteRow, DemoCard, SharePreviewCard, PaywallUnlockPanel. That’s the language of the app.

So my closing position is pretty simple. No external design pass. Lock this handoff as the build spec. Incorporate Claude’s refinements as first-class design decisions: minimum-hold two-stage loading, lazy per-row Saved refresh, local rescore-not-refetch after reporting, persisted first-run demo-card flag, and a single scrolling Route Result for accessibility. Keep the visual system we already converged on: warm off-white background, ink text, petrol primary route emphasis, amber moderate caution, coral severe on-route hazard, no green-safe semantics, confidence always icon-plus-word. If we write those decisions down clearly, a SwiftUI builder can implement this without hidden judgment calls, and more importantly the product will still feel like the thing we’ve been fighting to protect: a calm, premium route-confidence tool whose hero is a recommendation sentence you can trust, not a pretty map that overpromises.

**Claude (Frontend Engineer · Skeptic) — Round 2**

Codex's round one spec is genuinely good and I don't think there's daylight between us on the vision — the "briefing card with spatial proof beneath it" framing for Route Result is exactly right, and honestly better phrased than how I said the same thing. But I want to push back on one thing Codex did that reads like a dodge: writing out a full, polished Claude Design prompt "if external design is desired" without actually taking a side on whether it's desired. That's the coordinator's whole flag, and I don't think we should let it sit as an option on the table — I want to make the strongest possible case that we should not use it, and I think Codex's own prompt is actually the best evidence for my position, not against it.

Look at what's in that prompt versus what's in this room. It says "petrol/teal for primary route emphasis, amber for moderate caution, rust/coral for severe hazards" — but it doesn't carry my exact hex values (`#1F5C56`, `#B8860B`, `#B4432E`), the 4/8/12/16/24/32 spacing scale, the 44×44pt minimum tap target, or the specific rule that severity colors and confidence badges must never share a semantic channel. It doesn't mention the persisted `hasCompletedFirstRouteSearch` flag instead of a derived-empty-state check. It doesn't mention that favorites refetch has to be lazy and staggered, not fired all at once on tab appear. It doesn't mention that "re-score after report" must never re-call MKDirections. None of that stuff is decorative — it's the substance of three phases of hard-won decisions, and a general-purpose design tool has no way to know any of it unless we write it into the prompt, at which point the "prompt" has become this entire document anyway. So the real choice isn't "internal spec vs. external design," it's "internal spec vs. internal spec plus a lossy compression of itself sent externally and reconciled back in." That second path is strictly more work for a worse guarantee of consistency, and I think we should just say no-external-design, full stop, and treat this write-up — tokens, states, components, motion, a11y — as the actual build spec. If Codex still wants a second opinion on pure visual polish later, fine, but that's a "review six finished SwiftUI screens" ask, not a from-scratch design commission.

On the engineering points from my last round — since Codex hasn't reacted to them yet, let me sharpen the ones I think matter most so there's something concrete to agree or fight with rather than a vague "confirm or contest." Codex's motion note for the two-stage loading state says it should "transition in a way that teaches the app's logic: first route line appears, then obstacle annotations and summary language resolve" — I like that framing a lot, but it still doesn't resolve my actual worry, which is timing, not sequencing. If obstacle scoring against local SwiftData genuinely takes 40ms, "first the route line appears, then obstacle annotations resolve" happens so fast it reads as one paint, not two steps, and the "teaching" value evaporates. I want us to lock, explicitly, that the second stage holds for a minimum perceptible duration (400–600ms) even when the underlying computation is instant — that's a deliberate, artificial delay, and it needs to be written down as a spec requirement so nobody "optimizes" it away as dead code during build.

I also want to add a concrete resolution to my own map-lifecycle concern from last round, because I realized I left it as an assertion without naming the actual mechanism: this should be built with the SwiftUI `Map` view and `MapContentBuilder` (`MapPolyline`, `Annotation`, `MapCircle`), not a `UIViewRepresentable`-wrapped `MKMapView`. iOS 17's native SwiftUI Map API is entirely sufficient for route overlays, obstacle pins, and tap handling here, and it sidesteps the exact memory/lifecycle ambiguity I raised — a plain SwiftUI view backing the Map segment gets created and torn down by the view hierarchy the normal way when the List segment is selected, instead of us having to manually manage a UIKit-bridged map instance's lifecycle ourselves. This is worth locking now because it changes what "lazy-load the map only when the Map segment is active" actually looks like in code — it's just conditional view construction, not a manual dispose/recreate dance.

One more thing I'll add that's new, not a repeat: Codex's motion notes mention "a small pulse on first appearance" for severe warnings and a "subtle re-evaluation animation" on the recommendation card after a report. Both are good ideas, but neither mentions Reduce Motion, and given this app's whole audience skews toward users who are more likely than average to have accessibility settings dialed in — vestibular sensitivity included — any pulse/bounce/morph animation needs a static fallback keyed off `UIAccessibility.isReduceMotionEnabled` (or the SwiftUI `accessibilityReduceMotion` environment value). A severe-hazard pulse that can't be turned off is a real accessibility miss in an app whose entire premise is serving mobility-constrained users, and I'd rather write "respects Reduce Motion, falls back to a static color/icon change with no animation" into the motion section now than have it get treated as launch-day polish that ships without it.

So: I'd call the substance done — Codex's screen-by-screen spec plus my architecture/token/state additions together form a complete handoff — and the only real decision left in the room is the external-design question, where I'm arguing hard for skipping it entirely and shipping this document as the build spec, plus locking the four or five engineering specifics above (minimum hold time, native SwiftUI Map not UIViewRepresentable, lazy staggered favorites refetch, rescore-not-refetch, persisted first-run flag, Reduce Motion fallback) as non-negotiable build notes rather than nice-to-haves.
