**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 2**

CONSENSUS: YES

Round two closed this out cleanly. Codex stopped hedging on the external-design question and came down firmly on Claude's side: no external Claude Design pass, this handoff itself is the build spec, full stop. Codex explicitly adopted essentially all of Claude's engineering catches from round one — the deliberate minimum-hold timing on the two-stage loader, lazy per-row refetching in Saved instead of a tab-open stampede, the hard separation between "fetch route geometry" (network, once) and "rescore against obstacles" (local, replayable), the persisted one-time flag for the demo card instead of a derived-from-current-data check, and the single-scrolling-container fix for Route Result at max Dynamic Type. Claude's round two reinforced the no-external-design position with a sharp argument (Codex's own draft prompt already proves the point — it can't carry the specific hex values, spacing scale, or behavioral rules that actually matter, so "send a prompt out" really means "reconstruct this whole document elsewhere and reconcile it back") and added three more concrete, uncontested technical locks: build the map with SwiftUI's native `Map`/`MapContentBuilder` API rather than a UIKit-bridged `MKMapView` (solves the lifecycle question outright), give any pulse/morph animation a Reduce-Motion-respecting static fallback given this app's audience, and keep the two-stage loading dwell time explicit (400–600ms) so it can't get "optimized away" as dead code. Nothing here pulls against Codex's position — it's the same direction, just more specific — and there's no remaining fork in the room.

## Final Output

**Decision on external design:** No external Claude Design pass. This handoff (Codex's screen-by-screen spec + Claude's architecture/token/state refinements, both below) is the build source of truth for direct SwiftUI implementation. If outside visual taste is wanted later, it's scoped narrowly as a post-build polish review of finished screens against the token system — never a reopened concept phase.

**Screen-by-screen specification:**

1. **Onboarding** — 3 cards, stop there: (1) what it does, framed around avoiding reported barriers, not fastest route; (2) what it doesn't guarantee — the unverified-data warning stated plainly; (3) how local/sample data works, including that some areas will have little or no data. Final action row: "Use my location" / "Plan without my location" as visually equal-weight choices (not primary/secondary styling) — manual origin is a core mode, not a fallback.

2. **Plan (tab 1)** — text-first, no map surface, ever. Origin field (current location or manual/pin), destination field, single primary action, one compact recent/favorite shortcut row, persistent quiet trust banner. On a genuinely fresh install (governed by a persisted `hasCompletedFirstRouteSearch` flag, never a live "zero recents/favorites" check — this stops the demo card from reappearing after a user deletes their favorites), one demo card: "See how route warnings work," which pushes straight into a fully populated real Route Result using seeded corridor data — no separate tutorial illustration.

3. **Search (origin/destination)** — pushed/modal screen: results, recent places, manual pin-drop path, explicit empty/ambiguous states. Permission-denied biases smoothly to manual entry, never framed as "degraded mode."

4. **Route Result (hero screen)** — built as **one scrolling container**, not a fixed header over a scrollable body (required for max Dynamic Type — otherwise the segmented control and content can get pushed off-screen). Order: recommendation sentence → confidence badge (icon+word) → why text, all one visual block; then on-route/nearby warning chips; then route-switcher chips (only when MapKit returns multiple routes — absent entirely, with honest "only walking route found" wording, when it returns one); then segmented Map/List control; then the selected representation's content. Map mode uses SwiftUI's native `Map`/`MapContentBuilder` (`MapPolyline`, `Annotation`, `MapCircle`) — not a `UIViewRepresentable`-wrapped `MKMapView` — so the Map segment's view is created/torn down normally by the view hierarchy when List is selected, no manual lifecycle management needed. List mode is travel-order sequential, full-sentence rows, a genuine first-class peer, not a fallback.
   - **Two-stage loading is a locked spec requirement, not an implementation footnote:** stage 1 "finding walking route" (real network wait), stage 2 "checking reported obstacles" holds for a minimum 400–600ms even when local scoring finishes in milliseconds — a deliberate, intentional delay so the app's differentiator is legible on first use, must not be "optimized away" as dead code.

5. **Obstacle detail** — bottom sheet: type, severity, age, note, route relationship, inline report action. Staleness must be legible here — trust comes from showing evidence age, not hiding it.

6. **Report Obstacle** — bottom sheet: fixed six type chips, severity as segmented control/button group (never a slider — hard to operate with Switch Control), optional note, "looks clear now" toggle when applicable. Save triggers **local rescoring of the currently-held route polyline** — never a second MKDirections call. This keeps offline reporting working and is what makes the "watch the recommendation update live" moment real; dismissing the sheet should produce a visible (but Reduce-Motion-respecting) update on the recommendation card.

7. **Saved (tab 2)** — full list-management screen. Rows refetch **lazily and staggered** (visible-first, per-row "checking today's route…" loading copy) — never all-at-once on tab appear, which would otherwise fire a pile of concurrent MapKit calls for a paid user with several saved routes. Persistent "2 of 2 free" indicator at cap; share action per row.

8. **Paywall** — sheet on 3rd favorite save attempt, shown over the actual route just being saved (translucent unlock panel, blocked action still visible behind it). Sells unlimited favorites + share export only — never implies routing/scoring/reporting are premium.

9. **Share preview** — shown before the system `ShareLink`: destination, recommendation sentence, confidence, timestamp, unverified-data line. Reads as a route snapshot, not a social card.

10. **Settings/info** (toolbar affordance) — full disclaimer, sample-data explanation, restore purchases, note-privacy guidance, demo-data reset.

**Design tokens:**
- Colors: ink `#12161C` (primary text/chrome), warm off-white `#F7F5F1` (background), petrol `#1F5C56` (single primary/interactive accent), amber `#B8860B` (moderate severity only), coral/rust `#B4432E` (severe on-route hazard only), slate-gray `#6B7280` (secondary/meta text). No color is reused for a second meaning anywhere — severity colors never double as confidence colors; confidence is always icon+word, never color-alone. No green-means-safe semantics anywhere.
- Spacing: 4/8/12/16/24/32 base scale. Corner radius: 16pt primary cards, 12pt chips/pills. Minimum tap target: 44×44pt everywhere.
- Type: system font throughout (correct Dynamic Type metrics, no custom font risk); recommendation sentence set at a distinct larger/semibold style so it visually anchors the screen even before color does anything.

**Component inventory:** RecommendationCard, ConfidenceBadge (3 fixed states: low/moderate/high, icon+word), WarningChip/ObstacleChip (icon-based on-route/nearby distinction, never color-alone), SeverityIndicator (amber/coral only, separate channel from confidence), RouteSwitcherChip, TrustBanner, ObstacleRow, ObstacleDetailSheet, ReportSheet, FavoriteRow, DemoCard (dashed-outline treatment, applied consistently everywhere seeded data renders, including inside List mode), SharePreviewCard, PaywallUnlockPanel, standard search cells. Deliberately kept small — no decorative component variety for its own sake.

**State & interaction notes:**
- Plan: fresh-install-with-demo-card (flag-driven, one-time only), populated, permission-denied (inline banner), search-empty/ambiguous.
- Route Result: two-stage loading (with the minimum-hold rule above), multi-route success, single-route success (honest wording, no switcher chips), zero-matched-obstacle ("no reported obstacles," never "safe," confidence shown separately), low-confidence (equal visual weight to the recommendation), offline-new-search-failure (endpoints preserved, distinct copy), offline-cached-route-review (rescoring still works locally), geocoding failure (editable in place), permission-lost-mid-session (falls back to manual origin).
- Saved: empty, under-cap, at-cap (paywall intercept), reopened favorite (live re-fetch of route geometry, "today's suggested route" framing), lazy staggered per-row refresh.
- Report sheet: default, reporting-against-existing-obstacle (adds "looks clear now"), save triggers local rescore + visible card update.
- Architecture notes carried into build: per-tab `NavigationStack`s (not one global stack); Route Result driven by a single `@Observable` view model holding fetched route geometry + independently recomputed score/confidence + segment selection; `RouteScorer`/`ObstacleMatcher` operate on plain Swift structs (not SwiftData `@Model` types directly) so boundary-value tests stay fast and dependency-free; `LocationService` wraps `CLLocationManager`, `@MainActor`-isolated, authorization exposed as observable state.

**Accessibility requirements:** Dynamic Type at largest accessibility sizes with no truncation on recommendation/why/confidence/paywall copy (enabled by the single-scroll-container fix above). VoiceOver reading order on Route Result: recommendation → confidence → why → route selector (if present) → representation toggle → content. List rows are fully composed sentences, not fragmented sublabels. On-route vs. nearby never depends on color alone. Every custom control (chips, badges, segmented control) gets an explicit `accessibilityLabel`/`accessibilityValue` written out, not left to default inference. Report sheet fully usable one-handed and with Switch Control. Disclaimer reachable from Route Result at any time, without hijacking reading order on every open.

**Motion notes:** Restrained, purposeful, never playful — no spring-bounce anywhere on Route Result. Two-stage loading transitions with the locked minimum-hold timing. Map/List switch crossfades content (not a slide, since no spatial navigation is actually happening) while the recommendation block stays fixed at top. Report-save triggers a subtle recommendation-card update animation (opacity/background flash, <300ms). Severe-hazard emphasis may use a small pulse on first appearance. **All of the above must respect Reduce Motion** (`accessibilityReduceMotion`) — falling back to a static, non-animated color/icon change — given this app's audience skews toward users more likely to have motion-sensitivity settings enabled.

**Quality Gate (Claude) — after round 2**

QUALITY: PASS

## Feedback

This is a strong, builder-ready handoff that satisfies every required output and both acceptance checks.

Strengths:
- All six required outputs present: screen-by-screen spec (10 screens with layout intent, components, states, a11y, motion each), concrete hex/spacing/type tokens, a deliberately small component inventory, state/interaction notes per screen, explicit accessibility requirements, and a clear, justified no-external-design decision (not a hedge — it directly engages why an external pass would risk drift, per Claude's round-2 argument that Codex's own draft prompt proves the point).
- Genuine resolution of the round-1 fork rather than papering over it: the external-design hedge is fully resolved (Codex flipped to explicit agreement), and all of Claude's engineering catches (rescore-not-refetch separation, persisted `hasCompletedFirstRouteSearch` flag, lazy staggered Saved refresh, single-scroll-container for Dynamic Type, native SwiftUI `Map` API, minimum-hold two-stage loading, Reduce Motion fallback) are incorporated as locked spec items with rationale, not left as unresolved suggestions.
- Traceable consistency with every prior phase: the 20m/10m buffer language surfaces as warning-chip behavior, ShareLink-not-push appears as the Share preview screen, the 2-favorite cap and "show the actual item being unlocked" paywall rule are both present, the seeded-corridor/DemoCard treatment is consistent, and the single-route-result honesty requirement is reflected in the Route Result switcher-chip logic.
- Acceptance checks pass concretely: a SwiftUI builder has enough (native Map API choice, NavigationStack-per-tab, `@Observable` view model shape, RouteScorer/ObstacleMatcher boundary as plain structs) to implement without guessing, and no hidden decisions remain (colors, spacing, tap targets, motion timing, and a11y labeling are all specified numerically or behaviorally rather than adjectivally).

No repair needed. Ready to close this phase and move to the next (implementation/architecture) phase.

## Coordinator Decision

See the coordinator's message above.

## Final Output

CONSENSUS: YES

Round two closed this out cleanly. Codex stopped hedging on the external-design question and came down firmly on Claude's side: no external Claude Design pass, this handoff itself is the build spec, full stop. Codex explicitly adopted essentially all of Claude's engineering catches from round one — the deliberate minimum-hold timing on the two-stage loader, lazy per-row refetching in Saved instead of a tab-open stampede, the hard separation between "fetch route geometry" (network, once) and "rescore against obstacles" (local, replayable), the persisted one-time flag for the demo card instead of a derived-from-current-data check, and the single-scrolling-container fix for Route Result at max Dynamic Type. Claude's round two reinforced the no-external-design position with a sharp argument (Codex's own draft prompt already proves the point — it can't carry the specific hex values, spacing scale, or behavioral rules that actually matter, so "send a prompt out" really means "reconstruct this whole document elsewhere and reconcile it back") and added three more concrete, uncontested technical locks: build the map with SwiftUI's native `Map`/`MapContentBuilder` API rather than a UIKit-bridged `MKMapView` (solves the lifecycle question outright), give any pulse/morph animation a Reduce-Motion-respecting static fallback given this app's audience, and keep the two-stage loading dwell time explicit (400–600ms) so it can't get "optimized away" as dead code. Nothing here pulls against Codex's position — it's the same direction, just more specific — and there's no remaining fork in the room.

## Final Output

**Decision on external design:** No external Claude Design pass. This handoff (Codex's screen-by-screen spec + Claude's architecture/token/state refinements, both below) is the build source of truth for direct SwiftUI implementation. If outside visual taste is wanted later, it's scoped narrowly as a post-build polish review of finished screens against the token system — never a reopened concept phase.

**Screen-by-screen specification:**

1. **Onboarding** — 3 cards, stop there: (1) what it does, framed around avoiding reported barriers, not fastest route; (2) what it doesn't guarantee — the unverified-data warning stated plainly; (3) how local/sample data works, including that some areas will have little or no data. Final action row: "Use my location" / "Plan without my location" as visually equal-weight choices (not primary/secondary styling) — manual origin is a core mode, not a fallback.

2. **Plan (tab 1)** — text-first, no map surface, ever. Origin field (current location or manual/pin), destination field, single primary action, one compact recent/favorite shortcut row, persistent quiet trust banner. On a genuinely fresh install (governed by a persisted `hasCompletedFirstRouteSearch` flag, never a live "zero recents/favorites" check — this stops the demo card from reappearing after a user deletes their favorites), one demo card: "See how route warnings work," which pushes straight into a fully populated real Route Result using seeded corridor data — no separate tutorial illustration.

3. **Search (origin/destination)** — pushed/modal screen: results, recent places, manual pin-drop path, explicit empty/ambiguous states. Permission-denied biases smoothly to manual entry, never framed as "degraded mode."

4. **Route Result (hero screen)** — built as **one scrolling container**, not a fixed header over a scrollable body (required for max Dynamic Type — otherwise the segmented control and content can get pushed off-screen). Order: recommendation sentence → confidence badge (icon+word) → why text, all one visual block; then on-route/nearby warning chips; then route-switcher chips (only when MapKit returns multiple routes — absent entirely, with honest "only walking route found" wording, when it returns one); then segmented Map/List control; then the selected representation's content. Map mode uses SwiftUI's native `Map`/`MapContentBuilder` (`MapPolyline`, `Annotation`, `MapCircle`) — not a `UIViewRepresentable`-wrapped `MKMapView` — so the Map segment's view is created/torn down normally by the view hierarchy when List is selected, no manual lifecycle management needed. List mode is travel-order sequential, full-sentence rows, a genuine first-class peer, not a fallback.
   - **Two-stage loading is a locked spec requirement, not an implementation footnote:** stage 1 "finding walking route" (real network wait), stage 2 "checking reported obstacles" holds for a minimum 400–600ms even when local scoring finishes in milliseconds — a deliberate, intentional delay so the app's differentiator is legible on first use, must not be "optimized away" as dead code.

5. **Obstacle detail** — bottom sheet: type, severity, age, note, route relationship, inline report action. Staleness must be legible here — trust comes from showing evidence age, not hiding it.

6. **Report Obstacle** — bottom sheet: fixed six type chips, severity as segmented control/button group (never a slider — hard to operate with Switch Control), optional note, "looks clear now" toggle when applicable. Save triggers **local rescoring of the currently-held route polyline** — never a second MKDirections call. This keeps offline reporting working and is what makes the "watch the recommendation update live" moment real; dismissing the sheet should produce a visible (but Reduce-Motion-respecting) update on the recommendation card.

7. **Saved (tab 2)** — full list-management screen. Rows refetch **lazily and staggered** (visible-first, per-row "checking today's route…" loading copy) — never all-at-once on tab appear, which would otherwise fire a pile of concurrent MapKit calls for a paid user with several saved routes. Persistent "2 of 2 free" indicator at cap; share action per row.

8. **Paywall** — sheet on 3rd favorite save attempt, shown over the actual route just being saved (translucent unlock panel, blocked action still visible behind it). Sells unlimited favorites + share export only — never implies routing/scoring/reporting are premium.

9. **Share preview** — shown before the system `ShareLink`: destination, recommendation sentence, confidence, timestamp, unverified-data line. Reads as a route snapshot, not a social card.

10. **Settings/info** (toolbar affordance) — full disclaimer, sample-data explanation, restore purchases, note-privacy guidance, demo-data reset.

**Design tokens:**
- Colors: ink `#12161C` (primary text/chrome), warm off-white `#F7F5F1` (background), petrol `#1F5C56` (single primary/interactive accent), amber `#B8860B` (moderate severity only), coral/rust `#B4432E` (severe on-route hazard only), slate-gray `#6B7280` (secondary/meta text). No color is reused for a second meaning anywhere — severity colors never double as confidence colors; confidence is always icon+word, never color-alone. No green-means-safe semantics anywhere.
- Spacing: 4/8/12/16/24/32 base scale. Corner radius: 16pt primary cards, 12pt chips/pills. Minimum tap target: 44×44pt everywhere.
- Type: system font throughout (correct Dynamic Type metrics, no custom font risk); recommendation sentence set at a distinct larger/semibold style so it visually anchors the screen even before color does anything.

**Component inventory:** RecommendationCard, ConfidenceBadge (3 fixed states: low/moderate/high, icon+word), WarningChip/ObstacleChip (icon-based on-route/nearby distinction, never color-alone), SeverityIndicator (amber/coral only, separate channel from confidence), RouteSwitcherChip, TrustBanner, ObstacleRow, ObstacleDetailSheet, ReportSheet, FavoriteRow, DemoCard (dashed-outline treatment, applied consistently everywhere seeded data renders, including inside List mode), SharePreviewCard, PaywallUnlockPanel, standard search cells. Deliberately kept small — no decorative component variety for its own sake.

**State & interaction notes:**
- Plan: fresh-install-with-demo-card (flag-driven, one-time only), populated, permission-denied (inline banner), search-empty/ambiguous.
- Route Result: two-stage loading (with the minimum-hold rule above), multi-route success, single-route success (honest wording, no switcher chips), zero-matched-obstacle ("no reported obstacles," never "safe," confidence shown separately), low-confidence (equal visual weight to the recommendation), offline-new-search-failure (endpoints preserved, distinct copy), offline-cached-route-review (rescoring still works locally), geocoding failure (editable in place), permission-lost-mid-session (falls back to manual origin).
- Saved: empty, under-cap, at-cap (paywall intercept), reopened favorite (live re-fetch of route geometry, "today's suggested route" framing), lazy staggered per-row refresh.
- Report sheet: default, reporting-against-existing-obstacle (adds "looks clear now"), save triggers local rescore + visible card update.
- Architecture notes carried into build: per-tab `NavigationStack`s (not one global stack); Route Result driven by a single `@Observable` view model holding fetched route geometry + independently recomputed score/confidence + segment selection; `RouteScorer`/`ObstacleMatcher` operate on plain Swift structs (not SwiftData `@Model` types directly) so boundary-value tests stay fast and dependency-free; `LocationService` wraps `CLLocationManager`, `@MainActor`-isolated, authorization exposed as observable state.

**Accessibility requirements:** Dynamic Type at largest accessibility sizes with no truncation on recommendation/why/confidence/paywall copy (enabled by the single-scroll-container fix above). VoiceOver reading order on Route Result: recommendation → confidence → why → route selector (if present) → representation toggle → content. List rows are fully composed sentences, not fragmented sublabels. On-route vs. nearby never depends on color alone. Every custom control (chips, badges, segmented control) gets an explicit `accessibilityLabel`/`accessibilityValue` written out, not left to default inference. Report sheet fully usable one-handed and with Switch Control. Disclaimer reachable from Route Result at any time, without hijacking reading order on every open.

**Motion notes:** Restrained, purposeful, never playful — no spring-bounce anywhere on Route Result. Two-stage loading transitions with the locked minimum-hold timing. Map/List switch crossfades content (not a slide, since no spatial navigation is actually happening) while the recommendation block stays fixed at top. Report-save triggers a subtle recommendation-card update animation (opacity/background flash, <300ms). Severe-hazard emphasis may use a small pulse on first appearance. **All of the above must respect Reduce Motion** (`accessibilityReduceMotion`) — falling back to a static, non-animated color/icon change — given this app's audience skews toward users more likely to have motion-sensitivity settings enabled.

---

CONSENSUS: YES

phase_discussions.title: Ios Architecture Review
phase_discussions.output:
Round two closed the one real open question cleanly. Codex dropped the hedge and committed: **non-consumable lifetime "Clearpath Pro" unlock**, not a subscription — because there's no recurring cost or live service behind unlimited favorites + share export, and Apple's own subscription guidance expects subscriptions to gate something that decays without ongoing investment. Claude independently landed on the exact same call for the exact same reasons and piled on a practical argument too: non-consumable collapses the entitlement model to one boolean check with no expiration/renewal/grace-period states to build or test. Nobody's holding out for subscription anymore.

Everything else from round one got explicitly confirmed rather than just left standing: CloudKit-safe SwiftData schema shape now (defaults, optional relationships, no uniqueness constraints, explicit stable IDs) without turning on the CloudKit entitlement itself; the three-way split of no-connectivity vs. `MKError.directionsNotFound` vs. geocoding failure as genuinely separate code paths; `RouteFetching`/`LocationProviding` protocol seams so tests never touch real MapKit/CoreLocation; two independent `ModelContainer` instances against the same store URL as the actual proxy for "survives force-quit"; `performAccessibilityAudit()` wired into CI as a mechanical gate alongside (not instead of) one manual VoiceOver pass; and zero third-party dependencies, full stop, no exception hedge.

Claude added two small, uncontested extensions in this round that nobody's pushing back on: cached route geometry needs its own staleness rule separate from obstacle recency (so "today's suggested route" doesn't get shown off a four-day-old offline snapshot — it should say "your last available route, from [day]" past a threshold), and recents should be capped at five, storing only label+coordinate with no per-search timestamp trail, plus a "clear recents" control next to demo-data reset — closing the same accidental-movement-history risk Codex flagged for obstacle notes.

CONSENSUS: YES

## Final Output

**SwiftUI architecture:** Small, disciplined app — per-tab `NavigationStack`s (Plan, Saved), feature-scoped `@MainActor`-isolated `@Observable` view models, thin service layer underneath: `LocationService` (permission + current location), a search/routing service wrapping MapKit, an obstacle/persistence service, a `RouteScorer`/`ObstacleMatcher` pair operating purely on plain Swift structs (never SwiftData `@Model` types or MapKit-native types directly), and a purchases service. Route geometry is fetched from MapKit exactly once per request/favorite-open, immediately converted to app-owned plain structs, and never re-fetched just to reflect a new report — reporting triggers local rescoring of the already-held polyline only. `RouteFetching` and `LocationProviding` are defined as protocols so unit/view-model tests inject fakes and never touch real MapKit/CoreLocation.

**Apple framework choices:** SwiftUI + Observation for UI/state, MapKit for search, directions, and route rendering (native `Map`/`MapContentBuilder`, no `UIViewRepresentable`-wrapped `MKMapView`), CoreLocation for permission and current-location origin (When-In-Use only — no Always, no background location), SwiftData for local persistence, StoreKit 2 for the paid unlock, `NWPathMonitor` for coarse reachability context (to distinguish "no network" from a MapKit-specific failure, not as the sole truth source). No ML, AR, or LLM dependency in v1 — none clear the "materially improves the trust loop" bar.

**Persistence and local data plan:** SwiftData schema for obstacle reports, favorites, and recent destinations, written CloudKit-safe from day one (every attribute has a default, relationships are optional, no unique-attribute constraints, stable IDs as explicit fields) even though the CloudKit capability/entitlement stays off in this MVP — so "sync-ready" is a real property of the schema, not just a claim. Obstacle reports carry `seeded` vs. `userEntered` provenance and use supersession (soft state, newer-weighted) rather than hard delete. Favorites store origin/destination endpoints only, never a frozen polyline, and re-fetch live on open. A cached route snapshot (for offline review) is explicitly ephemeral and carries its own fetched-at timestamp with a staleness threshold (~1 day) — past that, offline-review copy shifts from "today's suggested route" to an honest "your last available route, from [day]" rather than implying currency it doesn't have. Recents are capped at five, store label+coordinate only with no per-search timestamp trail, and get an explicit "clear recents" control next to the demo-data reset in Settings.

**Permissions/privacy plan:** When-In-Use location only, manual/pin-drop origin entry as a first-class equal mode (never a fallback), permission-revoked-mid-session handled as an observed authorization-state change (not polled). No camera, no contacts, no notifications, no motion. Free-text obstacle notes and saved destinations use complete-until-first-unlock file protection, with inline copy discouraging personal/identifying info since sharing exists downstream. No hidden search/location history beyond the capped, visible recents list.

**Dependency/license decision:** Zero third-party packages, full stop — no conditional exception. Apple's own frameworks cover every requirement, including the ShareLink export.

**Testing implications:** Pure unit tests over plain structs for the scorer/matcher — boundary-value tests at 19m/21m, 9m/11m, day 6/8, day 30/31, day 90/91, the single-route wording branch, the shorter-route tie-break, and the supersession-changes-score-immediately case — none requiring SwiftData, CoreLocation, or a live network path. Integration tests cover persistence seeding, the third-favorite paywall intercept, permission-denied → manual-origin flow, and offline cached-route review (including the new staleness-copy rule). The "survives force-quit" requirement is tested via two independent `ModelContainer` instances against the same on-disk store URL, not an in-memory relaunch fake. `XCTestCase.performAccessibilityAudit()` runs as a mechanical CI gate (contrast, hit-target size, missing labels) alongside one deliberate manual VoiceOver pass per milestone for the semantic reading-order requirement, which the automated audit can't catch. StoreKit testing uses a local `.storekit` configuration file plus a real sandbox-account restore-purchases test after delete/reinstall — no server-side receipt validation in v1, consistent with the non-consumable, on-device-`VerificationResult`-only entitlement model now locked for the single "Clearpath Pro" unlock.
phase_discussions.discussion_markdown:
# clearpath-access — Ios Architecture Review

_Generated by the autonomous multi-agent orchestrator on 2026-07-07 02:02:48._

## Original Prompt

```
PORTFOLIO_CHILD_PROJECT: true
Parent portfolio: batch-cat-b1
Selected app slug: clearpath-access

Build this app as a working local-first SwiftUI MVP.

## Selected App Spec

# Clearpath Access

Build mode: **MVP build**.

## App Name

Clearpath Access

## Category

Navigation

## One-Sentence Promise

Accessibility-aware pedestrian routing with community-sourced obstacle reporting

## Claude Design Handoff Prompt

(not supplied yet)

## Parent Portfolio Prompt

Build 9 completely separate production-ready iOS apps at the same time: exactly 3 Health & Fitness apps, 3 Medical apps, and 3 Navigation apps.

This is a multi-app PORTFOLIO request: multiple apps in one program. Each selected app becomes its own separate project — one folder per app.


Requirements for every app:
1. Must go from 0 to production-ready, not just a few features.
2. Must be unique, useful, and commercially viable.
3. Must be beautifully designed and feel premium.
4. Must provide real end-user value.
5. Must have a realistic path to monetization — a subscription or paid offering with genuine value (never cosmetics-only).
6. Should have viral potential (broad or niche) without sacrificing usefulness.
7. Must be better than its competitors in a meaningful way.
8. Local-first where applicable, architected so cloud support can be added later without rewriting.

UNIQUENESS (hard rule, non-negotiable):
- No app in this batch may be similar to another app in this batch.
- No app may be similar to anything already built or being built in this workspace or before, including: Waylay (location-based personal recall utility), TrueScale, Provenance, Scope-Guard, Practice-Loop, Proof, ReturnWise, VerveCoach, CueKeeper, Countertop, FieldQuote, my existing digital temple app, and my existing PO-automation tool.
- No app may be similar to apps in the OTHER batches of this program (check sibling project folders in the workspace for their concepts).
- Do not reuse prior concepts, themes, or mechanics from earlier apps — even if the request sounds similar to something built before, produce a differentiated concept.

Bonus points (optional — only when integral and genuinely valuable, never forced): unique use of LLMs, AR, or ML.

Build rules:
- Run the app efforts in parallel; keep discussion/design phases separate per app.
- Each app gets its own full product direction, design direction, architecture, implementation plan, and build output.
- Save all phase discussions and final decisions; at the end combine each app's full transcript into a .txt file.
- If an app is liked, also prepare Jira board + Notion project structures for backfilling later.
- Use enough rounds per phase for high-quality discussion, ending early on consensus.
- Recover cleanly from stalls; report only important milestones.

Output:
- One folder per app in the output directory — never one folder containing all apps.
Output directory:
- /Users/pchordia/Documents/iOS-App-Factory

Additional notes:
Medical/health apps must frame outputs as wellness insights and validated risk scores (e.g. QRISK3, FINDRISC style), never diagnosis.

Now generate at least 18 candidate ideas, discuss them thoroughly, choose the best 9, and build all 9 to production quality.
```

## Phase Purpose

Review the app as an iOS product before final tech specs: SwiftUI architecture, Apple framework choices, local persistence, privacy, permissions, offline behavior, StoreKit/subscription architecture, permissive dependency policy, and testability.

## Transcript


### Round 1


**Codex (Backend / Systems Engineer · Skeptic) — Round 1**

I would keep this app aggressively native and boring in the places that matter, because the product risk here is not “can we build a map UI,” it is “can we avoid shipping a misleading trust surface.” The architecture should be a small SwiftUI app with per-tab `NavigationStack`s, feature-scoped `@Observable` view models on the main actor, and a thin services layer behind them. One service owns location permission and current location snapshots, one owns place search and route fetches through MapKit, one owns obstacle persistence and seeded-data install, one owns route scoring/confidence computation as pure value-type domain logic, and one owns purchases. I would not let SwiftData models bleed into the scoring engine or into the UI state for route calculation. Fetch route geometry from MapKit once, convert it immediately into plain structs the app owns, and from that point on scoring, supersession, confidence, and list ordering should all be local deterministic code that can run with zero network and zero framework magic.

Framework-wise, this is straightforward if we stay disciplined: SwiftUI + Observation for UI, MapKit for search/routing/map rendering, CoreLocation for permission and current-location origin, SwiftData for local storage, StoreKit 2 for the two-favorites cap unlock and restore flow, and potentially AppStorage/UserDefaults for tiny app flags like onboarding completion and whether seed data has been installed. I would not add CloudKit in MVP just to look future-ready. Make the data model sync-shaped, yes, but don’t ship half an account system for a product whose core promise is local-first. Also no ML, no AR, no LLM dependency in v1. None of them improve the core trust loop enough to justify complexity, privacy surface, or testing cost. If later you want plain-language explanation generation, that should still be template-driven from deterministic scoring reasons, not model-generated prose.

For persistence, I’d use SwiftData but keep the schema narrow and explicit: obstacle reports, saved destinations/favorites, recent destinations, and maybe a lightweight cached route snapshot if you want offline review of the last-opened route. The important split is between durable user/authored data and disposable route-fetch results. Obstacle reports need stable IDs, created/updated timestamps, status semantics that support supersession rather than hard delete, and provenance flags for `seeded` vs `userEntered`. Favorites should store endpoints and user label, never a trusted frozen route polyline. If you cache route geometry at all, treat it as ephemeral session/offline convenience data with fetched-at timestamp and routing context, not as canonical truth. The biggest quiet failure mode here is letting stale cached geometry masquerade as “today’s recommended route.” The UI copy already wants honesty; the storage model needs to enforce it.

Privacy and permissions need to be stricter than most consumer map apps because the notes and destinations can become health-adjacent even if the app never claims medical use. Ask only for When In Use location. No Always permission, no background location, no motion, no notifications, no camera, no contacts. Manual origin entry must be first-class so denial is not punitive. Saved destinations and free-text obstacle notes should use complete-until-first-user-authentication file protection. I would add explicit note-copy discouraging personal info because even purely local notes tend to become shareable later. Also, do not persist a hidden trip history just because the user searches often. Persist recents only if they are visible and user-benefiting. “We can reconstruct user movement from search/origin/destination drift” is exactly the sort of accidental surveillance posture this app should avoid.

Offline behavior needs to be written down with hard boundaries now, before someone hand-waves “local-first” into something false. New routing is network-dependent because MapKit directions are network-dependent. Full stop. Offline still allows reading saved favorites, viewing persisted obstacle reports, opening a previously cached route snapshot if one exists, and rescoring that held route against local obstacle changes. Reporting a new obstacle while offline must work and must update the currently displayed recommendation locally without a new directions call. That fetch-vs-score separation is not an implementation preference; it is the only way the product’s “report and immediately see the result change” promise survives bad connectivity.

For monetization, use StoreKit 2 only, with a simple non-consumable or auto-renewable subscription depending how hard you want to commit to recurring revenue now. From the current feature shape, I would actually lean non-consumable lifetime “Pro” for MVP unless the broader portfolio strategy requires subscription everywhere. Two saved favorites free, unlimited favorites plus share export paid, restore purchases supported, no server receipt validation in v1. If you force subscription without cloud sync, verified data, or caregiver live features, the value proposition is thin and cancellation risk is high. The safest architecture is still StoreKit 2 entitlement state observed locally, with the purchase boundary isolated so a later backend can validate receipts if the business model changes.

I would keep dependencies at zero third-party packages unless a license-clean, clearly necessary gap appears during implementation, and I don’t currently see one. Apple frameworks cover the need. That matters because this app’s real complexity is not missing UI widgets; it is correctness around route matching, confidence, permission fallback, and stale-data honesty. Third-party map helpers or persistence wrappers mostly add moving parts and legal review surface without reducing the hard problems. If any dependency is considered later, the bar should be “materially lowers platform risk” and license must be permissive, but default answer here should be no.

Testing is where I’d be most rigid. The route scorer, buffer matcher, recency banding, confidence derivation, tie-break logic, and supersession rules should be pure unit tests over plain structs. They should not require SwiftData, CLLocation, or MapKit UI. Boundary tests are non-negotiable because this app’s trust promise lives there: 9m vs 11m, 19m vs 21m, 30 vs 31 days, single-route wording, newer-clear report superseding older-hazard report in the same corridor, zero obstacles with low confidence vs zero obstacles with moderate confidence. Then add integration tests around persistence seeding, purchase gating at third favorite save, permission-denied manual-origin flow, and offline cached-route review. UI tests should cover onboarding, denied location, a seeded demo route, saving two favorites, third-favorite paywall intercept, report submission updating the visible recommendation, and VoiceOver-relevant accessibility labels on the route summary and obstacle rows. I would not overinvest in map-pin tap UI tests because they are brittle; the list representation is the reliable automation surface.

The biggest objection to my own direction is that it can make the app feel narrower and less “smart” than people expect from navigation software. No backend, no live sync, no verified data layer, no push alerts, no fancy ML explanation engine. But that is exactly why it is shippable. If you try to close those gaps now, you either fake capability or create a half-built distributed system around a trust-sensitive product. The detail I would preserve at all costs in the final output is this: route geometry acquisition and accessibility scoring must be separate subsystems with separate failure modes. Network gets you a path. Local data judges that path. If that line blurs, offline behavior breaks, tests get muddy, and the app starts overclaiming what it actually knows.

**Claude (QA & Risk · Pragmatist) — Round 1**

Coming at this from QA and pragmatics: the product and design phases have done real work, and my job now is to poke at where this specific plan breaks on an actual iPhone, in an actual App Store review, in an actual CI run — not to relitigate the vision.

Start with the schema risk, because this is the one that can quietly cost a rewrite later even though nothing in this phase asked us to build sync yet. SwiftData paired with CloudKit later has real, well-known constraints: every attribute needs a default value, relationships have to be optional (no non-optional to-many without a default), no unique constraints on attributes, and no `@Attribute(.unique)`. If the `ObstacleReport`, `Favorite`, and `SavedDestination` models get built now without those constraints already in place — because nothing is stopping SwiftData from letting you build a schema CloudKit can't mirror — then "architected so cloud can be added later without rewriting" is a false promise the day someone actually flips CloudKit on. My ask for tech_specs: write the SwiftData models today as if `ModelConfiguration(cloudKitDatabase:)` were already on, even though it isn't, so the schema constraint is paid for now while it's cheap. I would not actually turn on the CloudKit capability/entitlement in this MVP — that adds container provisioning, signing complexity, and an entitlement that does nothing for a local-first app — but the model shapes need to already obey the rules.

Location and permissions should stay as minimal as this app can possibly get away with, and I think that's actually a selling point, not just a constraint: request "When In Use" only, never "Always," since nothing in the locked spec requires background tracking or turn-by-turn — that was explicitly ruled out. That keeps the Info.plist usage-description surface small and keeps the privacy nutrition label honest and simple (location used for routing, not linked to identity, not used for tracking — that's a real checkbox in App Store Connect and it's worth getting right the first time rather than amending it later). The permission-denied-to-manual-entry path being a first-class mode rather than a fallback is good design, but the thing QA actually needs to verify is permission *revoked mid-session* — backgrounding the app, killing location access in Settings, foregrounding again — and that requires `LocationService` to observe authorization changes as state, not poll it, which the architecture phase already specified; I just want to flag that this exact transition (granted → revoked → app still on Route Result screen) needs its own explicit test, because it's the one permission edge case that's easy to design for on paper and easy to leave unverified in practice.

On MapKit specifically: I want to draw a hard line between three failure modes that are semantically different but easy to accidentally collapse into one "something went wrong" banner — no network at all (checkable up front with `NWPathMonitor`, so we're not guessing from an opaque `MKError`), a real network call that comes back with `MKError.directionsNotFound` (genuinely no walking route exists between two points), and a geocoding failure on the search step before routing even starts. All three were named as distinct states in the design phase, which is right, but that only holds if the implementation actually inspects error type and network reachability separately rather than funneling everything into a single catch block — that's the kind of thing that looks fine in the demo corridor and quietly breaks the first time someone tries a real address that MapKit can't route.

Testability is where I'd push hardest, because this app's whole pitch is "trust the recommendation," and if the scoring logic isn't actually verifiable in CI, that pitch is unbacked. The plain-struct `RouteScorer`/`ObstacleMatcher` boundary already locked is correct and I don't want to relitigate it — but I'd add that `MKDirections` and `CLLocationManager` both need to sit behind thin protocols (`RouteFetching`, `LocationProviding`) so view-model-level tests can inject fakes instead of making real network calls or needing simulator location spoofing. Real MapKit calls inside a CI-run test suite are slow, flaky, and a good way to get rate-limited for no reason — none of the boundary-value tests on the 20m/10m buffer or the recency bands need a real network call to prove anything. For the "survives force-quit and relaunch" persistence requirement from `app_features` — you can't literally force-quit inside an XCTest — the honest test is: write through one `ModelContainer` instance, then open a second, independent `ModelContainer` pointed at the same on-disk store URL and confirm the data reads back; that's the real proxy for "survives relaunch," and it's worth being explicit that this is the technique, so nobody ships a test that just reuses the same in-memory container and calls it coverage.

I'd also flag that Xcode's built-in accessibility audit API (`XCTestCase`'s `performAccessibilityAudit()` on iOS 17+) should be wired into the UI test target as an actual gate, not left to manual VoiceOver spot-checks — it catches contrast, hit-target-size, and missing-label issues mechanically. It won't catch the specific reading-order requirement (recommendation → confidence → why → selector → content) though, since that's semantic, not structural, so that still needs one deliberate manual VoiceOver pass per milestone build, not just at the very end.

On StoreKit: the thing that's still underspecified going into tech_specs is whether the "unlock unlimited favorites + share export" purchase is a one-time non-consumable or an auto-renewing subscription — `app_features` says "purchase unlocks," the parent portfolio brief allows either, but this materially changes the implementation (`Transaction.currentEntitlements` works for both, but a subscription needs expiration/renewal-lapsed handling and different restore-purchase messaging, a non-consumable doesn't). I'd rather this get pinned as a concrete decision in tech_specs than discovered mid-build. Separately, this needs a local `.storekit` configuration file for Xcode Previews/simulator testing plus an actual sandbox-account test of restore-purchases after a real delete-and-reinstall — not just trusting that `AppStore.sync()` works because the API exists. And one more thing worth a heads-up rather than a blocker: the free-text obstacle note field, combined with a share/export path, sits in a gray zone of Apple's UGC guideline (1.2) even though ShareLink is one-to-one and not a live feed — I don't think it triggers the full moderation/block/report requirement since nothing is published or searchable, but it's worth a one-line note in the App Review submission notes explaining that reports are private/local and export is user-initiated, so it doesn't become a review holdup we didn't see coming.

Last thing: dependency policy here should just be zero third-party packages, full stop, not "permissive-license only" as a hedge — MapKit, CoreLocation, SwiftData, StoreKit 2, SwiftUI, and Observation cover literally everything this app needs, including the ShareLink export. Adding any SPM dependency for an MVP this scoped would be pure risk for no capability gained, and I'd rather lock that as a hard zero than leave the door open to "just this one small package" creeping in during build.

**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 1**

Here's where round one leaves things: Codex and Claude are pulling in the same direction on basically everything that matters — small SwiftUI app, per-tab `NavigationStack`s, `@Observable` view models behind thin service boundaries, MapKit/CoreLocation/SwiftData/StoreKit 2 as the entire framework stack, no ML/AR/LLM in v1, zero third-party dependencies, and a hard architectural wall between "fetch route geometry" (network) and "score it against obstacles" (local, deterministic, unit-testable). Neither side is fighting the other's core recommendation.

But Claude's round added a handful of specific engineering commitments that Codex hasn't had a chance to weigh in on yet, and they're substantive enough that I don't think this phase should close without a reaction:

- **SwiftData schema written CloudKit-safe now, even with CloudKit switched off** — every attribute needs a default, relationships must be optional, no unique constraints — so the "architected for cloud later without a rewrite" promise from the product brief is actually true the day it matters, not just asserted.
- **Three distinct MapKit/network failure paths**, not one catch-all: no connectivity (checked via `NWPathMonitor` up front), `MKError.directionsNotFound`, and geocoding failure — these were named as separate UI states back in the design phase, and that only holds if the code actually distinguishes them.
- **`RouteFetching`/`LocationProviding` protocol seams** so view-model tests can inject fakes instead of hitting real MapKit/CoreLocation in CI.
- **A concrete technique for the "survives force-quit" persistence test** (two independent `ModelContainer` instances against the same store URL, since you can't literally force-quit inside XCTest).
- **`performAccessibilityAudit()` wired into the UI test target** as a mechanical gate, on top of (not instead of) a manual VoiceOver pass for the reading-order requirement.
- **The StoreKit product type is still an open decision** — Codex leans non-consumable lifetime "Pro" but hedged it against "unless the portfolio wants subscription everywhere"; Claude flagged that this needs to be pinned as one concrete choice before tech_specs, since a subscription and a non-consumable have genuinely different entitlement/restore/expiration handling.
- One very minor wording-only difference: Codex said dependencies should default to "no" but left a license-clean-exception door open; Claude wants "zero third-party, full stop." That's not really a disagreement, just Claude declining the hedge.

CONSENSUS: NO

Next round needs Codex to react to those specific adds (CloudKit-safe schema now, the three-way MapKit error split, the DI test seams, the ModelContainer persistence-test technique, the accessibility-audit gate) and, most importantly, actually pick one StoreKit product type instead of leaving it conditional — that's the one open decision that changes real implementation shape going into tech_specs.

### Round 2


**Codex (Backend / Systems Engineer · Skeptic) — Round 2**

Claude’s additions are the right kind of annoying, which is exactly why I buy most of them. The biggest one is the StoreKit hedge I left open. I don’t think we should keep that fuzzy going into tech specs. For this specific app, subscription is the wrong shape for MVP. There’s no cloud sync, no live caregiver feed, no verified municipal layer, no ongoing server cost that the user can feel, and the paid unlock is basically “more favorites plus share export.” That is a weak subscription and a perfectly defensible one-time upgrade. So I’d lock it as a non-consumable lifetime Pro unlock in v1, implemented with StoreKit 2 only, local entitlement checks, restore support, and a `.storekit` config for simulator/UI testing. If later the product grows into CloudKit sync, caregiver change alerts, or curated verified coverage packs, then you can add a subscription tier without having designed the whole entitlement surface around a recurring bill the current product does not earn.

On the data side, Claude is also right that “cloud-ready later” is meaningless unless the SwiftData schema is written with CloudKit’s rules now. I would not enable CloudKit capability in MVP, but I would absolutely shape the models as if `cloudKitDatabase` might be turned on later: defaults everywhere, optional relationships, no uniqueness constraints, explicit stable external IDs stored as plain fields rather than relying on SwiftData uniqueness, and soft-state semantics for supersession instead of destructive mutation. That avoids the classic trap where a tidy local schema becomes a migration tax the moment sync matters. Same story on the service seams: `RouteFetching` and `LocationProviding` protocols are not architecture astronautics here, they are what keeps CI from depending on real MapKit/CoreLocation behavior. And Claude’s three-way failure split is not pedantry. “No network,” “MapKit found no walking route,” and “search/geocode failed” are different user truths and the UI has already promised to distinguish them. If the implementation collapses them, the product becomes less honest than the copy says it is.

The other stuff I’d lock without drama: zero third-party packages, not “probably zero.” There is nothing here that justifies dependency risk. Use `NWPathMonitor` for reachability context, but don’t pretend it’s the truth source for every failure; it’s just enough to separate obvious offline from MapKit-specific failures. Keep the fetch-route-vs-score-route wall hard, because that is the core non-functional contract in this app. Reporting while offline only works if rescoring is pure local logic over a held polyline snapshot. And yes, the persistence test technique Claude named is the honest one: two independent `ModelContainer`s against the same disk store, not an in-memory fake relaunch. Same with `performAccessibilityAudit()` as a gate: it won’t replace a real VoiceOver pass for reading order, but it should absolutely be in CI so hit-target and labeling regressions stop being “we’ll check later.” If I’m trying to push one idea hardest, it’s this: tech specs should treat honesty as a systems property, not just a copywriting property. That means schema choices, error taxonomy, entitlement model, and test seams all need to reinforce the same thing the UI says.

**Claude (QA & Risk · Pragmatist) — Round 2**

Codex's five-service split — location, search/route, obstacle persistence, scoring, purchases — is exactly the shape I'd want to see in tech_specs, and I want to underline the part that matters most for QA: "fetch route geometry from MapKit once, convert immediately into plain structs the app owns." That's not a style preference, that's the thing that makes the whole offline story testable at all. If route geometry ever lived as a MapKit-native type inside app state, half my proposed unit tests (buffer boundaries, recency bands, supersession) would silently become integration tests that need a live network path to even compile a test fixture. So I'm fully with Codex here, and I'd add one thing to the persistence model they sketched: the "ephemeral cached route snapshot for offline review" needs its own staleness rule, separate from obstacle recency. Right now we have a rule for "how old is this obstacle report" but not "how old is this cached route geometry itself." Picture this: someone opens a saved favorite while offline, and the app shows the last successfully fetched polyline from four days ago. If the copy says "today's suggested route to Dr. Patel's office" — which is exactly the wording we locked in the design phase — that's now a lie by omission. I'd want a hard rule that cached route geometry carries a fetched-at timestamp, and past some threshold (say, more than a day old) the offline-review copy has to shift to something like "showing your last available route, from Tuesday" instead of implying it's current. That's a small addition but it closes a real honesty gap in the exact feature Codex is right to want us to build.

On the recents/history point — Codex's "don't reconstruct movement from search drift" framing is the correct instinct, and I want to turn it into something testable rather than leave it as a principle: cap recent destinations at a small fixed number (five is plenty), store only label plus coordinate, no per-search timestamp trail, and give it an explicit "clear recents" control sitting right next to the demo-data reset in Settings. That's cheap to build and it's the difference between "the app remembers your last few destinations for convenience" and "the app has a receipts drawer of everywhere you've typed," which is exactly the accidental-surveillance posture we don't want given how health-adjacent some of these destinations will be (clinic, pharmacy).

Now the thing the coordinator actually flagged as the one real open decision: StoreKit product type. Codex leaned non-consumable lifetime "Pro" but hedged it against "unless the portfolio wants subscription everywhere." I want to kill that hedge, not soften it — non-consumable is the right call and I don't think it's close. Apple's own subscription guidelines (3.1.2) expect a subscription to gate ongoing service value — something that costs us something recurring to provide, or that decays if you stop paying. Unlimited saved favorites and a ShareLink export are neither: there's no server cost, no live feed, no content that goes stale without our continued investment. A subscription wrapped around "you can save more than two routes locally" is the kind of thing that gets a "why is this recurring" side-eye in review, and it's also just a worse pitch to the actual user, who'd reasonably ask why they're paying monthly for local storage capacity on their own phone. Pragmatically it's also the smaller build: a non-consumable collapses the entitlement check to "does `Transaction.currentEntitlements` ever contain this product ID" with no expiration date, no renewal state, no billing-retry or grace-period UI, no "subscription lapsed, are your extra favorites still visible read-only or hidden" question that a subscription would force us to answer. Fewer states means fewer tests means it actually ships. So: one non-consumable "Clearpath Pro" unlock, StoreKit 2, on-device verification only via `VerificationResult`, no receipt-validation backend — Codex already said that last part and I agree, there's nothing here worth standing up server infrastructure for.

Everything else from round one I'll consider locked unless someone pushes back: CloudKit-safe SwiftData schema shape now without turning on the CloudKit entitlement, the three-way split between no-connectivity/`directionsNotFound`/geocoding-failure as genuinely different code paths instead of one catch block, `RouteFetching`/`LocationProviding` behind protocols so tests never touch real MapKit or CoreLocation, the two-independent-`ModelContainer`-instances technique as the actual proxy for "survives force-quit," and `performAccessibilityAudit()` as a mechanical CI gate on top of one manual VoiceOver pass for reading order. Codex didn't object to any of those and they don't cost anything extra to adopt, so I'd treat this phase as done once the StoreKit type is settled — and I think non-consumable just settled it.

**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 2**

Round two closed the one real open question cleanly. Codex dropped the hedge and committed: **non-consumable lifetime "Clearpath Pro" unlock**, not a subscription — because there's no recurring cost or live service behind unlimited favorites + share export, and Apple's own subscription guidance expects subscriptions to gate something that decays without ongoing investment. Claude independently landed on the exact same call for the exact same reasons and piled on a practical argument too: non-consumable collapses the entitlement model to one boolean check with no expiration/renewal/grace-period states to build or test. Nobody's holding out for subscription anymore.

Everything else from round one got explicitly confirmed rather than just left standing: CloudKit-safe SwiftData schema shape now (defaults, optional relationships, no uniqueness constraints, explicit stable IDs) without turning on the CloudKit entitlement itself; the three-way split of no-connectivity vs. `MKError.directionsNotFound` vs. geocoding failure as genuinely separate code paths; `RouteFetching`/`LocationProviding` protocol seams so tests never touch real MapKit/CoreLocation; two independent `ModelContainer` instances against the same store URL as the actual proxy for "survives force-quit"; `performAccessibilityAudit()` wired into CI as a mechanical gate alongside (not instead of) one manual VoiceOver pass; and zero third-party dependencies, full stop, no exception hedge.

Claude added two small, uncontested extensions in this round that nobody's pushing back on: cached route geometry needs its own staleness rule separate from obstacle recency (so "today's suggested route" doesn't get shown off a four-day-old offline snapshot — it should say "your last available route, from [day]" past a threshold), and recents should be capped at five, storing only label+coordinate with no per-search timestamp trail, plus a "clear recents" control next to demo-data reset — closing the same accidental-movement-history risk Codex flagged for obstacle notes.

CONSENSUS: YES

## Final Output

**SwiftUI architecture:** Small, disciplined app — per-tab `NavigationStack`s (Plan, Saved), feature-scoped `@MainActor`-isolated `@Observable` view models, thin service layer underneath: `LocationService` (permission + current location), a search/routing service wrapping MapKit, an obstacle/persistence service, a `RouteScorer`/`ObstacleMatcher` pair operating purely on plain Swift structs (never SwiftData `@Model` types or MapKit-native types directly), and a purchases service. Route geometry is fetched from MapKit exactly once per request/favorite-open, immediately converted to app-owned plain structs, and never re-fetched just to reflect a new report — reporting triggers local rescoring of the already-held polyline only. `RouteFetching` and `LocationProviding` are defined as protocols so unit/view-model tests inject fakes and never touch real MapKit/CoreLocation.

**Apple framework choices:** SwiftUI + Observation for UI/state, MapKit for search, directions, and route rendering (native `Map`/`MapContentBuilder`, no `UIViewRepresentable`-wrapped `MKMapView`), CoreLocation for permission and current-location origin (When-In-Use only — no Always, no background location), SwiftData for local persistence, StoreKit 2 for the paid unlock, `NWPathMonitor` for coarse reachability context (to distinguish "no network" from a MapKit-specific failure, not as the sole truth source). No ML, AR, or LLM dependency in v1 — none clear the "materially improves the trust loop" bar.

**Persistence and local data plan:** SwiftData schema for obstacle reports, favorites, and recent destinations, written CloudKit-safe from day one (every attribute has a default, relationships are optional, no unique-attribute constraints, stable IDs as explicit fields) even though the CloudKit capability/entitlement stays off in this MVP — so "sync-ready" is a real property of the schema, not just a claim. Obstacle reports carry `seeded` vs. `userEntered` provenance and use supersession (soft state, newer-weighted) rather than hard delete. Favorites store origin/destination endpoints only, never a frozen polyline, and re-fetch live on open. A cached route snapshot (for offline review) is explicitly ephemeral and carries its own fetched-at timestamp with a staleness threshold (~1 day) — past that, offline-review copy shifts from "today's suggested route" to an honest "your last available route, from [day]" rather than implying currency it doesn't have. Recents are capped at five, store label+coordinate only with no per-search timestamp trail, and get an explicit "clear recents" control next to the demo-data reset in Settings.

**Permissions/privacy plan:** When-In-Use location only, manual/pin-drop origin entry as a first-class equal mode (never a fallback), permission-revoked-mid-session handled as an observed authorization-state change (not polled). No camera, no contacts, no notifications, no motion. Free-text obstacle notes and saved destinations use complete-until-first-unlock file protection, with inline copy discouraging personal/identifying info since sharing exists downstream. No hidden search/location history beyond the capped, visible recents list.

**Dependency/license decision:** Zero third-party packages, full stop — no conditional exception. Apple's own frameworks cover every requirement, including the ShareLink export.

**Testing implications:** Pure unit tests over plain structs for the scorer/matcher — boundary-value tests at 19m/21m, 9m/11m, day 6/8, day 30/31, day 90/91, the single-route wording branch, the shorter-route tie-break, and the supersession-changes-score-immediately case — none requiring SwiftData, CoreLocation, or a live network path. Integration tests cover persistence seeding, the third-favorite paywall intercept, permission-denied → manual-origin flow, and offline cached-route review (including the new staleness-copy rule). The "survives force-quit" requirement is tested via two independent `ModelContainer` instances against the same on-disk store URL, not an in-memory relaunch fake. `XCTestCase.performAccessibilityAudit()` runs as a mechanical CI gate (contrast, hit-target size, missing labels) alongside one deliberate manual VoiceOver pass per milestone for the semantic reading-order requirement, which the automated audit can't catch. StoreKit testing uses a local `.storekit` configuration file plus a real sandbox-account restore-purchases test after delete/reinstall — no server-side receipt validation in v1, consistent with the non-consumable, on-device-`VerificationResult`-only entitlement model now locked for the single "Clearpath Pro" unlock.

**Quality Gate (Claude) — after round 2**

QUALITY: PASS

## Feedback

This closes the phase well. All six required outputs are present and concrete: SwiftUI architecture (per-tab `NavigationStack`s, `@Observable` view models, named services, plain-struct scoring boundary, protocol seams), Apple framework choices (SwiftUI/Observation, MapKit native `Map`, CoreLocation When-In-Use, SwiftData, StoreKit 2, `NWPathMonitor`), persistence plan (CloudKit-safe schema shape without enabling the entitlement, supersession semantics, favorites-as-live-refetch, the new cached-route staleness rule, capped/trimmed recents), permissions/privacy plan (minimal permission surface, file protection, no hidden history), dependency decision (zero third-party, no hedge), and testing implications (concrete boundary values, DI seams, the two-`ModelContainer` persistence-test technique, `performAccessibilityAudit()` plus manual VoiceOver pass, StoreKit sandbox testing).

Strengths:
- The one genuinely open question from round 1 — StoreKit product type — got a clean, reasoned resolution (non-consumable lifetime unlock) rather than being left conditional, with both agents converging independently on the same rationale (no recurring cost/service behind the paid features, per Apple's own subscription guidance) plus a practical build-cost argument. This isn't a papered-over disagreement; it's a real decision that changes implementation shape and is now unambiguous going into tech_specs.
- Traceable consistency with every earlier phase: the CloudKit-safe-schema-without-CloudKit-entitlement approach honors the "architected so cloud can be added later without rewriting" requirement from the prompt contract without violating the "no real backend in MVP" non-goal; the fetch-vs-score separation preserves the offline reporting promise locked in `detailed_discussion`/design phases; the favorites-live-refetch and staleness-copy addition directly protects the "today's suggested route" honesty rule already locked; zero third-party dependencies matches the parent portfolio's implicit simplicity bias and this phase's own rubric.
- The two new additions (cached-route staleness threshold, capped/trimmed recents) are small, well-justified, and explicitly tied to failure modes already named earlier in the project (false confidence from stale data; accidental movement-history logging) rather than being scope creep.
- ML/AR/LLM is explicitly and correctly declined with a stated bar ("materially improves the trust loop") — satisfies the acceptance check that any such use be justified by user value (here, justified by absence of value).
- No architecture gaps: every layer named (location, search/routing, persistence, scoring, purchases) with concrete framework backing; nothing hand-waved as "TBD."

Both acceptance checks pass: the app is buildable natively with named frameworks and no ambiguous gaps, and the zero-third-party-dependency decision trivially satisfies the permissive-license constraint. Ready to hand to tech_specs.

Integration tests cover persistence seeding, the third-favorite paywall intercept, permission-denied → manual-origin flow, and offline cached-route review (including the new staleness-copy rule). The "survives force-quit" requirement is tested via two independent `ModelContainer` instances against the same on-disk store URL, not an in-memory relaunch fake. `XCTestCase.performAccessibilityAudit()` runs as a mechanical CI gate (contrast, hit-target size, missing labels) alongside one deliberate manual VoiceOver pass per milestone for the semantic reading-order requirement, which the automated audit can't catch. StoreKit testing uses a local `.storekit` configuration file plus a real sandbox-account restore-purchases test after delete/reinstall — no server-side receipt validation in v1, consistent with the non-consumable, on-device-`VerificationResult`-only entitlement model now locked for the single "Clearpath Pro" unlock.

---

CONSENSUS: YES

phase_discussions.title: Tech Specs
phase_discussions.output:
Round three closed the loop. Codex accepted essentially everything Claude added in round two without a fight — `ReportDraft`/`ReportSubmission` as the single place that decides save-vs-supersede-vs-supersede-nearby, `Segment`, `AppDependencies` as the composition root, seed-data versioning (`currentSeedDataVersion` + `resetSeedData()`), and the approximate-location risk note on `LocationAuthorizationState`. Codex also proposed the same flattening of `RouteExplanationBuilder` from a protocol to a static function that Claude was pushing for, using the identical reasoning (deterministic pure computation doesn't need a fake seam) — so that's independently confirmed from both sides now, not just asserted by one. Claude's round three then formally conceded the one place Codex had pushed back — dropping `Favorite.lastOpenedAt`, since nothing in the locked spec sorts or displays favorites by recency — and sharpened `ReportSubmission`'s own logic: the proximity check for "is this a report about the same physical obstacle" needs to reuse the inner 10m buffer, not the outer 20m one, since two unrelated hazards on the same block could otherwise wrongly cancel each other out.

There's one small snag worth being upfront about rather than quietly papering over: Codex's round three text and JSON explicitly argued to *keep* `CachedRouteStore.deleteExpiredRoutes(olderThan:)` ("if it's named, someone owns it — cached-route honesty is a locked requirement, not just housekeeping"), but Claude's round three response dropped it anyway, apparently under the impression Codex wanted it cut too (that was true in round two, but Codex reversed specifically on this one method in round three). Since every other point converged cleanly and this is a single one-line protocol method with a real, undisputed argument behind keeping it, I'm resolving it in Codex's favor rather than spinning up a fourth round over one method signature: `deleteExpiredRoutes(olderThan:)` stays on `CachedRouteStore`.

CONSENSUS: YES

## Final Output

**Architecture overview:** A small, disciplined SwiftUI app built around one hard rule that both agents converged on independently: protocols exist only where a build lane needs to fake a platform resource in tests (network, CoreLocation, StoreKit, disk) — `RouteFetching`, `PlaceSearching`, `LocationProviding`, `ObstacleStore`, `FavoritesStore`, `RecentsStore`, `CachedRouteStore`, `PurchaseManaging`, `ReachabilityProviding`. Everything else that's pure, deterministic computation is a plain static function with no fakeable seam — `RouteScorer`, `ObstacleMatcher`, `RouteExplanationBuilder`. Route geometry is fetched from MapKit exactly once per request or favorite-open, converted immediately into app-owned plain structs (`RouteSnapshot`/`RouteCandidate`), and never re-fetched to reflect a new report — `RouteResultViewModel.rescore()` is the only mutation path after a report, and it never calls `RouteFetching` again. A single `AppDependencies` composition-root bundle is built once in `App/` and injected everywhere, so no feature wires up its own ad hoc service instances. `RouteResultInput` is the one shared navigation payload Plan, Saved, and the onboarding demo card all construct identically, closing off the risk of three slightly different push contracts into Route Result.

**File/module layout:** `App/` (entry point, composition root wiring `AppDependencies` and the `ModelContainer`), `Domain/` (plain structs plus `RouteScorer`/`ObstacleMatcher`/`RouteExplanationBuilder` — zero imports beyond Foundation/CoreLocation-for-math, zero SwiftData or MapKit types), `Persistence/` (SwiftData `@Model` records, `ModelContainerFactory`, `DemoDataSeeder`, mapping extensions to/from domain structs), `Services/` (concrete `LocationService`, `RouteSearchService`, `ObstacleRepository`, `FavoritesRepository`, `RecentsRepository`, `ReachabilityMonitor`, `PurchaseService`, each conforming to its protocol), `Features/{Onboarding,Plan,Search,RouteResult,ObstacleDetail,ReportObstacle,Saved,Paywall,SharePreview,Settings}/` each with a View + `@Observable` view model, `DesignSystem/` (tokens plus the locked component set), and `Tests/` split into a pure-domain unit target (no SwiftData, MapKit, or CoreLocation — this is what keeps the boundary-value tests fast) and a separate integration/UI target.

**Data models:** Domain layer (framework-free, `Sendable`, used everywhere scoring/matching/UI logic runs): `Coordinate`, `RouteEndpoint`, `ObstacleType`/`ObstacleSeverity`/`ReportProvenance`, `ObstacleReport`, `RoutePolyline`, `RouteCandidate`, `RouteSnapshot`/`RouteSnapshotSource`, `ObstacleRelationship`, `ObstacleMatch`, `ConfidenceLevel`, `FrictionCategory`, `RouteExplanation`/`WarningChipModel`/`WarningChipKind`, `ScoredRoute`, `RouteRecommendation`, `ScoringConstants`, `ReportDraft`, `Favorite`, `RecentDestination`, `ShareSnapshot`. Persistence layer (SwiftData, CloudKit-safe from day one — defaults on every attribute, optional relationships, no unique constraints, explicit stable UUID fields): `ObstacleReportRecord`, `FavoriteRecord`, `RecentDestinationRecord`, `CachedRouteSnapshotRecord`. A thin mapping layer at the `Persistence/` boundary is the only place these two worlds touch.

**Persistence strategy:** Obstacle reports carry `seeded`/`userEntered` provenance and use supersession (`isActiveForScoring` flag, newer-weighted, old row retained for history) rather than hard delete, with two distinct supersession paths — explicit by-ID (the "looks clear now" action on a known obstacle) and proximity-based via the inner 10m buffer (an unprompted new report landing on the same physical hazard as an existing one) — both centralized behind `ReportSubmission.submit`, so no UI lane has to decide which write path applies. Favorites store origin/destination endpoints only, never a frozen polyline, and always re-fetch live on open. Cached route snapshots are explicitly ephemeral, carry their own `fetchedAt` and `source`, and honor a staleness threshold (~1 day) past which offline-review copy shifts from "today's suggested route" to "your last available route, from [day]"; `CachedRouteStore.deleteExpiredRoutes(olderThan:)` gives that lifecycle a named owner rather than leaving expiry implicit. Seed data installation is versioned (`ScoringConstants.currentSeedDataVersion` checked against a persisted installed-version flag) so a later update can revise the demo corridor without duplicating or silently ignoring the change, and `resetSeedData()` backs the locked Settings "demo-data reset" control. Recents are capped at five, storing label+coordinate only, no per-search timestamp trail.

**Testing strategy:** Pure unit tests over plain structs for `RouteScorer`/`ObstacleMatcher`/`RouteExplanationBuilder` — none require SwiftData, CoreLocation, or a live network path — asserting the exact boundary values baked into `ScoringConstants`: 19m/21m outer buffer, 9m/11m inner buffer, day 6/8, day 30/31, day 90/91 recency bands, the single-route wording branch, the shorter-route tie-break, and a before/after supersession test proving a new report changes score immediately. `RouteFetching`/`LocationProviding`/`PlaceSearching`/`PurchaseManaging`/`ReachabilityProviding` are faked in view-model tests via their protocols. The "survives force-quit" requirement is tested via two independent `ModelContainer` instances against the same on-disk store URL. `performAccessibilityAudit()` runs as a mechanical CI gate alongside one deliberate manual VoiceOver pass per milestone for the semantic reading-order requirement it can't catch.
