```interfaces-json
  {"name": "RouteFetching", "kind": "protocol", "language": "swift", "signature": "protocol RouteFetching: Sendable { func fetchRoutes(from origin: RouteEndpoint, to destination: RouteEndpoint) async throws -> RouteSnapshot }", "owning_lane": "services_utilities", "notes": "Live impl wraps MKDirections; throws RouteRequestError, never raw MKError. Tests inject a fake, never touch real MapKit."},
  {"name": "LocationAuthorizationState", "kind": "enum", "language": "swift", "signature": "enum LocationAuthorizationState: Equatable, Sendable { case notDetermined, denied, restricted, authorizedApproximate, authorizedPrecise }", "owning_lane": "services_utilities", "notes": "authorizedApproximate can put a pedestrian's origin 100m+ off, on the wrong side of a block — LocationService/Plan should nudge toward manual pin-drop rather than treat it as equivalent to precise. Not blocking for MVP but named so it isn't silently ignored."},
  {"name": "LocationProviding", "kind": "protocol", "language": "swift", "signature": "protocol LocationProviding: AnyObject, Sendable { var authorizationState: LocationAuthorizationState { get }; func requestWhenInUseAuthorization(); func currentLocation() async throws -> Coordinate }", "owning_lane": "services_utilities", "notes": "Observed state, not polled; supports permission-revoked-mid-session."},
  {"name": "ObstacleStore", "kind": "protocol", "language": "swift", "signature": "protocol ObstacleStore { func installSeedDataIfNeeded() async throws; func resetSeedData() async throws; func fetchActiveReports() async throws -> [ObstacleReport]; func saveReport(_ report: ObstacleReport) async throws; func supersede(existingID: UUID, with newReport: ObstacleReport) async throws; func supersedeNearby(coordinate: Coordinate, thresholdMeters: Double, with newReport: ObstacleReport) async throws; func fetchHistory(near coordinate: Coordinate, thresholdMeters: Double) async throws -> [ObstacleReport] }", "owning_lane": "services_utilities", "notes": "installSeedDataIfNeeded checks ScoringConstants.currentSeedDataVersion against a persisted installed-version flag so an app update can revise demo content without duplicating rows. resetSeedData backs the locked Settings 'demo-data reset' control."},
  {"name": "Favorite", "kind": "struct", "language": "swift", "signature": "struct Favorite: Identifiable, Hashable, Codable, Sendable { let id: UUID; var label: String; var origin: RouteEndpoint; var destination: RouteEndpoint; var createdAt: Date }", "owning_lane": "data_domain", "notes": "RESOLVED: lastOpenedAt dropped — nothing in the locked spec sorts or displays favorites by last-opened time, so the field had no consumer anywhere in the contract."},
  {"name": "FavoritesStore", "kind": "protocol", "language": "swift", "signature": "protocol FavoritesStore { func all() async throws -> [Favorite]; func add(_ favorite: Favorite) async throws; func remove(id: UUID) async throws; func count() async throws -> Int }", "owning_lane": "services_utilities", "notes": "count() checked before save so the 3rd attempt triggers paywall cleanly, never a half-save."},
  {"name": "RecentDestination", "kind": "struct", "language": "swift", "signature": "struct RecentDestination: Identifiable, Hashable, Codable, Sendable { let id: UUID; var endpoint: RouteEndpoint; var createdAt: Date }", "owning_lane": "data_domain", "notes": "Capped at 5, label+coordinate only, no per-search timestamp trail beyond the entry's own createdAt."},
  {"name": "RecentsStore", "kind": "protocol", "language": "swift", "signature": "protocol RecentsStore { func fetchRecents(limit: Int) async throws -> [RecentDestination]; func recordRecent(_ endpoint: RouteEndpoint) async throws; func clearRecents() async throws }", "owning_lane": "services_utilities", "notes": "Backs the explicit 'clear recents' Settings control, no hidden movement trail."},
  {"name": "CachedRouteStore", "kind": "protocol", "language": "swift", "signature": "protocol CachedRouteStore { func loadCachedRoute(origin: RouteEndpoint, destination: RouteEndpoint) async throws -> RouteSnapshot?; func saveCachedRoute(_ snapshot: RouteSnapshot) async throws; func deleteExpiredRoutes(olderThan: Date) async throws }", "owning_lane": "services_utilities", "notes": "RESOLVED BY COORDINATOR: Codex's round-3 argument to keep deleteExpiredRoutes wins — cached-route honesty is a locked product requirement and expiry is part of that lifecycle, not just storage housekeeping; naming it means one lane owns it."},
  {"name": "PurchaseManaging", "kind": "protocol", "language": "swift", "signature": "protocol PurchaseManaging: AnyObject { var hasProUnlock: Bool { get }; func purchasePro() async throws; func restorePurchases() async throws }", "owning_lane": "services_utilities", "notes": "Non-consumable product id com.clearpathaccess.pro.lifetime, on-device VerificationResult only, no receipt-validation backend."},
  {"name": "ReachabilityProviding", "kind": "protocol", "language": "swift", "signature": "protocol ReachabilityProviding { var isNetworkReachable: Bool { get } }", "owning_lane": "services_utilities", "notes": "NWPathMonitor-backed coarse hint, never sole error-cause truth source."},
  {"name": "AppDependencies", "kind": "struct", "language": "swift", "signature": "struct AppDependencies { let routeFetching: RouteFetching; let placeSearching: PlaceSearching; let locationProviding: LocationProviding; let obstacleStore: ObstacleStore; let favoritesStore: FavoritesStore; let recentsStore: RecentsStore; let cachedRouteStore: CachedRouteStore; let purchaseManaging: PurchaseManaging; let reachabilityProviding: ReachabilityProviding }", "owning_lane": "primary_ui", "notes": "Single composition-root bundle built once in App/ and injected via environment so no build lane wires up ad hoc service instances."},
  {"name": "Segment", "kind": "enum", "language": "swift", "signature": "enum Segment: String, CaseIterable, Sendable { case map, list }", "owning_lane": "primary_ui", "notes": "Route Result's Map/List segmented control state; fills a real gap where RouteResultViewModel referenced this type without it ever being defined."},
  {"name": "RouteResultInput", "kind": "struct", "language": "swift", "signature": "struct RouteResultInput: Hashable, Sendable { let origin: RouteEndpoint; let destination: RouteEndpoint; let favoriteID: UUID?; let isDemoCorridor: Bool }", "owning_lane": "primary_ui", "notes": "Single navigation payload shared by Plan, Saved, and the demo card. No alternate push models."},
  {"name": "PlanRoute", "kind": "enum", "language": "swift", "signature": "enum PlanRoute: Hashable { case search; case result(RouteResultInput) }", "owning_lane": "primary_ui", "notes": "NavigationStack path type for the Plan tab."},
  {"name": "SavedRoute", "kind": "enum", "language": "swift", "signature": "enum SavedRoute: Hashable { case result(RouteResultInput) }", "owning_lane": "primary_ui", "notes": "Independent NavigationStack path for Saved, separate from Plan."},
  {"name": "RouteResultLoadPhase", "kind": "enum", "language": "swift", "signature": "enum RouteResultLoadPhase: Equatable { case fetchingRoute; case checkingObstacles; case loaded; case failed(RouteRequestError) }", "owning_lane": "primary_ui", "notes": "checkingObstacles holds a visible 400-600ms minimum even when local scoring is instant."},
  {"name": "RouteResultViewModel", "kind": "protocol", "language": "swift", "signature": "@MainActor @Observable final class RouteResultViewModel { var phase: RouteResultLoadPhase; var recommendation: RouteRecommendation?; var currentSnapshot: RouteSnapshot?; var segment: Segment; func load(_ input: RouteResultInput) async; func rescore() }", "owning_lane": "primary_ui", "notes": "rescore() is the only post-report mutation path, never calls RouteFetching again; report sheet calls ReportSubmission.submit then rescore(). Must recover cleanly from scene-phase backgrounding/process death by reloading, never showing stale/garbage state."},
  {"name": "FavoriteRowStatus", "kind": "enum", "language": "swift", "signature": "enum FavoriteRowStatus: Equatable { case idle; case checking; case upToDate; case newIssues(count: Int); case offlineUnavailable(lastKnown: Date?) }", "owning_lane": "primary_ui", "notes": "Per-row Saved state; concurrent checks capped at 2, triggered from row .onAppear, never all at once."},
  {"name": "ShareSnapshot", "kind": "struct", "language": "swift", "signature": "struct ShareSnapshot: Equatable, Codable, Sendable { let destinationLabel: String; let recommendationSentence: String; let confidence: ConfidenceLevel; let generatedAt: Date; let disclaimerText: String }", "owning_lane": "polish_resilience", "notes": "Self-contained payload for ShareLink; no account or network required to open."},
  {"name": "makeShareSnapshot", "kind": "function", "language": "swift", "signature": "func makeShareSnapshot(from route: ScoredRoute, destination: RouteEndpoint, generatedAt: Date, disclaimerText: String) -> ShareSnapshot", "owning_lane": "polish_resilience", "notes": "Stable builder used by share preview and ShareLink export."},
  {"name": "hasCompletedFirstRouteSearch", "kind": "function", "language": "swift", "signature": "var hasCompletedFirstRouteSearch: Bool { get set } // UserDefaults-backed flag", "owning_lane": "polish_resilience", "notes": "Persisted one-time flag gating the Plan demo card; never derived from zero-recents/zero-favorites state."}
]}
```

---

CONSENSUS: YES

phase_discussions.title: Project Plan
phase_discussions.output:
CONSENSUS: YES

This round closed it. Codex took Claude's three round-one additions and grafted them directly into the five-milestone skeleton instead of treating them as a rival plan — the persistence/CloudKit spike goes into milestone one's exit criteria, the supersession check becomes a named store-level test in milestone two, and the concurrency/timing items get written into milestone three and five's exit criteria respectively. Claude then did the same in reverse: explicitly dropped its parallel nine-step structure, accepted Codex's skeleton as the base, and placed its asks as precise patches rather than a competing shape. The one real correction Claude made — that the two-stage-loading timing test belongs in milestone three (where Route Result's UI first exists), not milestone two (which is pure domain/persistence with no loading UI at all) — is just a logical fix to where Codex put it, not a disagreement in substance, so I'm folding it in that way. Claude's two sharpened asks in this round (verifying concurrent favorite-refresh safety under actual concurrent access rather than just visual staggering, and treating "restore correctly suppresses the paywall on the next save attempt" as its own required assertion rather than assuming it falls out of "restore works") are uncontested refinements in the same spirit as everything Codex already agreed to fold in — nobody has raised an objection to either, and both close real gaps in milestone five's exit criteria. Gemini stayed unavailable both rounds, so there's no third perspective, but Codex and Claude converged on a single, non-duplicated plan.

## Final Output

**Milestones:**

1. **Foundation & contracts.** Xcode project scaffold with the locked module layout (App/Domain/Persistence/Services/Features/Tests), `AppDependencies` composition root, SwiftData `ModelContainer`, StoreKit config, seed-data installer/reset with version flag, and just enough UI to prove navigation structure and onboarding. Before building the four persistence stores on top of it, run two narrow proof spikes: (a) confirm the two-independent-`ModelContainer`-against-the-same-store-URL technique actually surfaces committed writes across instances, and (b) point one throwaway `ModelConfiguration` at `cloudKitDatabase: .automatic` once and confirm the schema doesn't throw (entitlement stays off in shipped code). Exit criteria: app launches cleanly; the force-quit persistence test methodology is proven, not assumed; the schema is validated against a CloudKit-enabled config once; seed-data versioning and reset work; StoreKit config is wired; exactly one composition root, no ad hoc service wiring anywhere.

2. **Trust engine.** `ScoringConstants`, `ObstacleMatcher`, `RouteScorer`, `RouteExplanationBuilder` as pure static functions, `ReportSubmission`'s save/supersede/supersedeNearby decision logic, and the four persistence repositories (`ObstacleStore`, `FavoritesStore`, `RecentsStore`, `CachedRouteStore`) built on the now-proven spike pattern. Exit criteria: boundary tests pass at 19/21m, 9/11m, day 6/8, 30/31, 90/91, the single-route wording branch, the shorter-route tie-break, and cached-route staleness copy; force-quit persistence verified for real against all four stores; a named store-level supersession integration test — save an obstacle, submit a new report inside the inner 10m buffer, assert `fetchActiveReports()` excludes the old one while `fetchHistory()` still returns both — exists as its own test, distinct from the pure boundary-value unit tests.

3. **Core trip flow end to end.** Onboarding, permission handling, manual origin entry, destination search, live `RouteFetching`/`PlaceSearching`, Route Result with two-stage loading, recommendation/why/confidence, and map/list toggle. Saved and paywall UI are explicitly deferred past this milestone. Exit criteria: a fresh install can go from open app to route result to obstacle inspection under both current-location and manual-origin paths; permission-denied, offline, geocoding failure, no-route-found, no-nearby-obstacles, low-confidence, and single-route wording are all distinct, honestly-worded code paths; a mechanical test asserts the `checkingObstacles` phase holds its 400–600ms minimum even when local scoring is instant, so a later cleanup pass can't quietly delete the app's differentiator.

4. **Reporting & live rescoring.** Report sheet, obstacle detail sheet, "looks clear now," local rescoring of the already-held route snapshot (never a second directions call), seeded/demo labeling, and the accessible travel-order list. Exit criteria: a report completes in under 30 seconds; the visible recommendation changes with no new directions call; supersession behaves correctly through the live UI path; a manual VoiceOver pass on Route Result and the Report sheet reaches summary, every flagged item, and the report action with zero dead ends.

5. **Repeat use & monetization.** Favorites, recents, Saved tab refresh (lazy, staggered, capped at 2 concurrent), share snapshot/`ShareLink`, the two-favorite free cap, paywall, StoreKit purchase/restore, and settings (demo-data reset, clear recents). Exit criteria: saving/opening a favorite live-refetches correctly; the third favorite cleanly triggers the paywall; Saved refresh is not just visually staggered but verified safe under actual concurrent access — no race or double-fire when two rows trigger close together; restoring purchases after reinstall correctly suppresses the paywall on the very next favorite-save attempt (a distinct assertion from "restore works" in isolation, since it's really a state-consistency check on `hasProUnlock` feeding `FavoritesStore.count()`); share preview/export is honest and self-contained; a full regression script runs fresh install, demo corridor, manual origin, zero-obstacle route, low-confidence route, report submission, favorite save, paywall intercept, cached-route offline review, and seed reset.

**Dependencies:** Domain math and spike-proven persistence must exist before Route Result UI. Route Result must exist before reporting, since reporting rescales an already-held snapshot. Reporting must exist before Saved refresh, since Saved status reuses the same scoring/explanation engine. Purchases come after favorites genuinely work, so the paywall gates real value rather than an unstable feature. Parallel build lanes open up only after milestone one: domain/tests, services/persistence, Plan/Search/Onboarding, Route Result/reporting/accessibility, and Saved/paywall/share/settings (this last lane starts once favorites and purchase scaffolding are ready). Integration checkpoints land after milestones two, three, and four — not just at the end.

**Risk mitigations:** Trust-surface divergence between score math, explanation copy, and UI state is the top named risk — mitigated by treating `RouteExplanationBuilder` and `RouteRequestError` as the sole canonical output surfaces, with no view-specific rewording of trust states. MapKit variability (single-route returns, no-route cases, rare advisories, network dependence) is handled by building honest single-route and offline behavior as first-class cases from milestone three, not edge cases bolted on later. Accessibility regressions from a map-first build mindset are avoided by building the List representation alongside Route Result, not after it. Unverified persistence/CloudKit-safety assumptions are resolved by the milestone-one spike before four stores get built on top of them. Silent trust-breaking bugs in report supersession are caught by the named store-level integration test in milestone two. Quietly-regressed timing/concurrency behavior (the loading floor, staggered favorite refresh) is caught by named mechanical tests in milestones three and five rather than left to visual inspection. Entitlement state-consistency bugs (double-unlock or a false paywall for a user who already restored) are caught by the explicit restore-gates-paywall test in milestone five. No user accounts, backend, or auth exist anywhere in this app — named explicitly as a non-goal so no lane reinvents one out of habit.

**Verification plan:** Pure unit tests plus the persistence-survival and supersession integration tests run after milestone two. Manual scenario verification (permission granted, denied, offline) plus the loading-floor timing test run after milestone three. The live-rescore scenario and a manual VoiceOver pass on Route Result and Report run after milestone four. StoreKit purchase/restore testing, the accessibility audit (`performAccessibilityAudit()` in CI plus one manual pass), the concurrency-safety check on Saved refresh, and the full regression script all run after milestone five. Testing and integration are attached to each milestone's exit criteria throughout — none of it is deferred to a final polish pass.
phase_discussions.discussion_markdown:
# clearpath-access — Project Plan

_Generated by the autonomous multi-agent orchestrator on 2026-07-07 02:21:56._

## Original Prompt

```
PORTFOLIO_CHILD_PROJECT: true
Parent portfolio: batch-cat-b1
Selected app slug: clearpath-access

Build this app as a working local-first SwiftUI MVP.

## Selected App Spec

# Clearpath Access

Build mode: **MVP build**.

## App Name

Clearpath Access

## Category

Navigation

## One-Sentence Promise

Accessibility-aware pedestrian routing with community-sourced obstacle reporting

## Claude Design Handoff Prompt

(not supplied yet)

## Parent Portfolio Prompt

Build 9 completely separate production-ready iOS apps at the same time: exactly 3 Health & Fitness apps, 3 Medical apps, and 3 Navigation apps.

This is a multi-app PORTFOLIO request: multiple apps in one program. Each selected app becomes its own separate project — one folder per app.


Requirements for every app:
1. Must go from 0 to production-ready, not just a few features.
2. Must be unique, useful, and commercially viable.
3. Must be beautifully designed and feel premium.
4. Must provide real end-user value.
5. Must have a realistic path to monetization — a subscription or paid offering with genuine value (never cosmetics-only).
6. Should have viral potential (broad or niche) without sacrificing usefulness.
7. Must be better than its competitors in a meaningful way.
8. Local-first where applicable, architected so cloud support can be added later without rewriting.

UNIQUENESS (hard rule, non-negotiable):
- No app in this batch may be similar to another app in this batch.
- No app may be similar to anything already built or being built in this workspace or before, including: Waylay (location-based personal recall utility), TrueScale, Provenance, Scope-Guard, Practice-Loop, Proof, ReturnWise, VerveCoach, CueKeeper, Countertop, FieldQuote, my existing digital temple app, and my existing PO-automation tool.
- No app may be similar to apps in the OTHER batches of this program (check sibling project folders in the workspace for their concepts).
- Do not reuse prior concepts, themes, or mechanics from earlier apps — even if the request sounds similar to something built before, produce a differentiated concept.

Bonus points (optional — only when integral and genuinely valuable, never forced): unique use of LLMs, AR, or ML.

Build rules:
- Run the app efforts in parallel; keep discussion/design phases separate per app.
- Each app gets its own full product direction, design direction, architecture, implementation plan, and build output.
- Save all phase discussions and final decisions; at the end combine each app's full transcript into a .txt file.
- If an app is liked, also prepare Jira board + Notion project structures for backfilling later.
- Use enough rounds per phase for high-quality discussion, ending early on consensus.
- Recover cleanly from stalls; report only important milestones.

Output:
- One folder per app in the output directory — never one folder containing all apps.
Output directory:
- /Users/pchordia/Documents/iOS-App-Factory

Additional notes:
Medical/health apps must frame outputs as wellness insights and validated risk scores (e.g. QRISK3, FINDRISC style), never diagnosis.

Now generate at least 18 candidate ideas, discuss them thoroughly, choose the best 9, and build all 9 to production quality.
```

## Phase Purpose

Produce milestones, sequencing, estimates, dependencies, and a risk-managed delivery plan.

## Transcript


### Round 1


**Codex (Delivery Lead · User Advocate) — Round 1**

I’d sequence this as five short milestones with explicit integration points, because this app’s main failure mode is not “missing features,” it’s inconsistent trust behavior between routing, scoring, and UI.

Milestone one is foundation and contracts. Lock the Xcode project, app shell, dependency composition root, SwiftData container, StoreKit config, seed-data installer, and the plain domain/service interfaces already agreed. Build only enough UI to prove navigation structure, onboarding flagging, and seeded-data install/reset work. Exit criteria: app launches cleanly, model container survives relaunch, demo seed versioning works, purchase config is wired, and there is exactly one source of truth for dependencies. If this milestone slips, everything downstream thrashes because every lane starts inventing its own seams.

Milestone two is the trust engine in isolation. Implement `ScoringConstants`, `ObstacleMatcher`, `RouteScorer`, `RouteExplanationBuilder`, `ReportSubmission`, and the persistence repositories behind obstacle reports, favorites, recents, and cached routes. Do not wait on maps UI. This is where the real product value lives, and it is the part we can verify deterministically. Exit criteria: boundary tests pass for 19m/21m, 9m/11m, day 6/8, 30/31, 90/91, single-route wording, tie-break behavior, supersession, cached-route staleness copy, and force-quit persistence via two real containers. If this milestone is weak, the app may look finished while still making unsafe recommendations.

Milestone three is the core trip flow end to end for one real user: onboarding, permission handling, manual origin, destination search, route fetch, two-stage loading, route result with recommendation/why/confidence, map/list toggle, and honest empty/error states. I would explicitly defer Saved and paywall UI until this works, because a wheelchair user opening the app for the first time does not care about monetization if the first route request is ambiguous or misleading. Exit criteria: fresh install can go from open app to route result to obstacle inspection under both current-location and manual-origin paths, and all failure branches are distinct: denied permission, offline, geocoding failure, no route found, no nearby obstacles, low-confidence area.

Milestone four is reporting and live rescoring. Add report sheet, obstacle detail sheet, “looks clear now,” local rescoring of the held route snapshot, seeded/demo labeling, and accessible travel-order list behavior. This needs to feel immediate; if report submission causes a second route fetch or leaves the user unsure whether anything changed, the app breaks its promise. Exit criteria: a report can be submitted in under 30 seconds, the visible recommendation changes without a new directions call, supersession behaves correctly, and VoiceOver can traverse summary, hazards, and reporting actions without dead ends.

Milestone five is repeat-use value and paid gating: favorites, recents, Saved tab refresh behavior, share snapshot, two-favorite free cap, paywall, restore purchases, settings/info, and final polish. I would keep share export bundled with Pro exactly as planned, but only after the free daily-use loop is stable. Exit criteria: save/open favorite works with live re-fetch, third favorite cleanly triggers paywall, restore works after reinstall, share preview/export is honest and self-contained, and saved-route refresh is staggered so we do not spam MapKit.

The dependency chain should be strict. Domain math and persistence come before Route Result UI. Route Result must exist before reporting, because reporting rescales an existing snapshot. Reporting must exist before Saved refresh, because Saved status depends on the same scoring/explanation engine. Purchases come after favorites actually work, otherwise the team ends up polishing a gate on top of unstable value. Accessibility and error-state copy are not a final pass task; they need to be written when each screen is built because this app’s trust model is expressed through wording and reading order.

The biggest delivery risk is integration drift across three separate “truth surfaces”: score math, explanation copy, and UI state handling. A route can be mathematically correct and still mislead if the confidence badge, warning chips, and empty-state copy diverge. The mitigation is to make `RouteExplanationBuilder` and `RouteRequestError` the canonical output surfaces and forbid view-specific rewording of trust states except for presentation-level truncation. The second big risk is MapKit variability: single-route returns, no-route cases, advisories absent most of the time, and network dependence. The mitigation is to build honest single-route and offline behavior as first-class cases, not edge cases, and to test with fakes before polishing multi-route richness. The third risk is accessibility regressions caused by a map-first build mindset. The mitigation is to build the List representation at the same time as Route Result, not afterward.

I would assign work in parallel only after milestone one. One lane owns domain logic and tests. One lane owns services/persistence. One lane owns the Plan/Search/Onboarding flow. One lane owns Route Result, report surfaces, and accessibility semantics. One lane owns Saved/paywall/share/settings once favorites and purchases are ready. Integration checkpoints should happen after milestone two, after core trip flow in milestone three, and after reporting in milestone four. Those are the points where mismatched assumptions are most expensive.

Verification cannot be left to the end. After milestone two, run pure unit tests and persistence survival tests. After milestone three, run manual scenario verification for first trip with permission granted, denied, and offline. After milestone four, run the live-rescore scenario and a manual VoiceOver pass on Route Result and Report. After milestone five, run StoreKit purchase/restore, accessibility audit, and a full regression script covering fresh install, demo corridor, manual origin, zero-obstacle route, low-confidence route, report submission, favorite save, paywall intercept, cached-route offline review, and seed reset. The final output for this phase needs to preserve that testing is attached to each milestone, not treated as cleanup. That is the only realistic way this ships as a trustworthy MVP instead of a polished prototype.

**Claude (Backend / Systems Engineer · Systems Thinker) — Round 1**

Looking at this from the data/storage/API and non-functional-requirements side, the sequencing question that actually matters here isn't "which screen first" — it's "which assumption, if wrong, forces a rewrite of everything built on top of it." Two of those assumptions are baked into the architecture as if already proven, and they aren't: the two-independent-`ModelContainer`-against-the-same-store-URL technique for verifying force-quit persistence, and the claim that the SwiftData schema is "CloudKit-safe" just because every attribute has a default and no unique constraints. Both are asserted in the tech spec as settled, but neither has actually been run. I'd insert a narrow, time-boxed spike milestone before any of the four persistence stores get built out — prove the two-container read-after-write pattern actually surfaces committed data across instances in the same process, and prove the schema doesn't blow up if you point a `ModelConfiguration` at `cloudKitDatabase: .automatic` even once, even though the entitlement stays off in shipped code. If either assumption is wrong, you want to know it before `ObstacleStore`, `FavoritesStore`, `RecentsStore`, and `CachedRouteStore` are all written against a test methodology that doesn't actually verify what it claims to.

My proposed sequencing, by real dependency rather than by feature: (1) project scaffold plus the module layout already locked (Domain/Persistence/Services/Features/Tests split) — this has to exist before anything compiles against it; (2) domain layer — `RouteScorer`, `ObstacleMatcher`, `RouteExplanationBuilder`, `ScoringConstants` — built and boundary-tested immediately, since it depends on nothing but Foundation and is the highest-leverage place to catch a math error before it's load-bearing under a dozen screens; in parallel, (2b) the persistence/CloudKit-safety spike described above; then (3) full persistence stores plus seed data versioning, and (4) the services layer (`LocationService`, MapKit-wrapping `RouteFetching`/`PlaceSearching`, `ReachabilityMonitor`, `PurchaseService`) — these two can run concurrently since neither blocks the other, both only need the domain structs; (5) `AppDependencies` composition root wired with fakes for every protocol, which is the actual unlock point for UI work — feature lanes should never wait for real service implementations, only for the protocol shapes and a fake; (6) feature UI fan-out across the locked screen inventory; (7) an integration pass that swaps fakes for real implementations and runs the full request-route → flag → report → rescore → favorite → share loop end to end; (8) accessibility and StoreKit verification as their own named milestones, not folded into "polish"; (9) a final pass against the full named state matrix from the design phases (permission-denied, offline-new-search, offline-cached-stale, single-route, zero-matched, low-confidence, superseded-report, seeded-vs-real) as an explicit checklist, since that matrix is the actual product and a generic empty/error/loading pass will not catch it.

The risk I'd put at the top of the register, above the two I already named: the report-supersession logic is the one piece of domain logic where a subtle bug doesn't crash anything, it just silently produces a wrong trust signal — a friction score that ratchets upward forever, or a cleared hazard that still shows as active. The unit tests already specified (boundary values at 19/21m, 9/11m, day thresholds) test the scoring math in isolation, but supersession is a store-level behavior — it has to be verified as an integration test that actually calls `ReportSubmission.submit` against a real (or two-container-spiked) persistence layer and asserts `fetchActiveReports()` excludes the superseded row while `fetchHistory()` still returns both. That test doesn't exist yet in anyone's plan and I think it needs to be named explicitly rather than assumed to fall out of the unit tests.

Two smaller systems-level things I want preserved in the plan rather than left implicit: first, `FavoriteRowStatus`'s "concurrent checks capped at 2" is a real concurrency primitive, not a UI detail — if the Saved store's underlying implementation isn't carefully actor-isolated, staggered concurrent reads against SwiftData can race or contend in ways that only show up under load, so I want a specific task for "verify concurrent favorite refresh doesn't deadlock or double-fire" rather than trusting it falls out of "the view triggers on `.onAppear`." Second, the locked two-stage loading delay (400–600ms minimum hold on `checkingObstacles`) is explicitly flagged as something a future engineer might "optimize away as dead code" — I want an actual test asserting that phase duration meets the floor, so that this is a regression a test suite catches, not a UI nuance someone quietly removes in a later cleanup pass. Finally, worth stating plainly in the plan: there is no user auth anywhere in this app — no accounts, no backend, StoreKit entitlement is on-device `VerificationResult` only — and I'd rather that be a named non-goal in the plan than something a build lane reinvents out of habit.

**Gemini — Round 1 (skipped: CLI unavailable)**

_Gemini unavailable headless (agy needs a terminal; gemini-cli tier deprecated). gemini CLI -> auth/tier error_

**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 1**

CONSENSUS: NO

Here's where things stand after round one: both Codex and Claude agree on the big-picture shape — score/matching/explanation logic gets built and boundary-tested first, in isolation, before any routing UI; the core "search → route → recommendation" flow ships before reporting; reporting/rescoring ships before Saved/favorites; and paywall/monetization comes last, gated behind a working free loop. Nobody's contesting that ordering, and both explicitly reject front-loading UI polish or leaving accessibility/testing for a final pass — Codex wants tests attached to every milestone's exit criteria, Claude wants the same thing framed as strict dependency order.

The real gap is that they haven't actually reconciled into one plan yet. Codex handed over a clean 5-milestone structure (foundation → trust engine → core trip flow → reporting/rescoring → favorites/paywall) with exit criteria per milestone. Claude, instead of reacting to that structure, wrote a parallel 9-step dependency breakdown and — more substantively — raised three things Codex's plan doesn't currently account for: (1) a narrow spike to actually prove the two-`ModelContainer` force-quit-persistence technique and the "CloudKit-safe schema" claim work before every persistence store gets built against those unverified assumptions; (2) an explicit integration test for report supersession that calls `ReportSubmission.submit` against real persistence and checks `fetchActiveReports()`/`fetchHistory()` — arguing this doesn't fall out of the existing unit tests; (3) named verification for the "concurrent favorite refresh capped at 2" concurrency behavior and a real test asserting the 400–600ms loading-floor timing, so neither gets silently "optimized away" later.

None of that contradicts Codex's milestones — it reads like it slots naturally into Codex's milestone 1 (spike) and milestone 2/4 exit criteria (the new tests) — but nobody has said that out loud yet, and Gemini wasn't available to weigh in. Next round needs to: fold Claude's spike and three named tests into Codex's 5-milestone skeleton explicitly (or explain why not), and settle on one milestone list rather than two differently-shaped ones sitting side by side.

### Round 2


**Codex (Delivery Lead · User Advocate) — Round 2**

Claude’s additions are the right kind of annoying here, because they target the exact places a build can look “mostly done” while still being untrustworthy. I think the clean move is to keep my five-milestone spine, but explicitly absorb Claude’s spike and test requirements instead of pretending they’re a separate plan. The real user for this app is not grading our architecture purity; they’re trying to decide whether to risk a route with a mobility aid. That means the dangerous failure isn’t “feature missing,” it’s “the app sounds more certain than it is.” So yes: milestone one should not just be app shell and dependency wiring, it should also include the two narrow proof points Claude called out. Run the two-`ModelContainer` persistence survival check immediately, and do the CloudKit-safe schema smoke test immediately, before four stores and a bunch of tests get built on top of assumptions. I don’t think that needs to become a whole separate milestone; it belongs inside foundation because it’s exactly the kind of foundational claim that, if wrong, poisons downstream work.

I also think Claude is right that the supersession behavior cannot hide inside generic “domain tests pass” language. That one needs to be named as a store-backed integration check, not implied by the scorer unit tests. A user reporting “looks clear now” and still getting warned about the old hazard later is not a minor bug, it’s a trust break. Same for the two smaller system behaviors Claude called out: the capped-at-2 favorite refreshes and the 400–600ms obstacle-checking floor. Those sound like implementation details until someone “cleans them up” and suddenly Saved hammers MapKit or the differentiating second-stage loading disappears so fast the app feels like regular walking directions with decoration. I would not spin those into standalone milestones, but I would absolutely write them into milestone exit criteria so they have an owner and a verification step.

So the merged plan, to me, is straightforward. Milestone one stays foundation, but now explicitly includes the persistence/schema spike, composition root, seed installer/reset, StoreKit config, and proof that relaunch persistence and demo versioning are real. Milestone two stays trust engine, but its exit criteria explicitly include the supersession integration test and the loading-floor test alongside the boundary math. Milestone three is still the first-trip flow end to end, with distinct permission/offline/geocoding/no-route/low-confidence states, because that’s the first moment the app either earns user trust or loses it. Milestone four is still reporting plus live rescoring, with the route held locally and rescored without a second directions call, plus the manual VoiceOver pass on Route Result and Report. Milestone five is still repeat-use and monetization, but now with the Saved concurrency check called out by name, because “staggered and capped at 2” is part of product behavior, not just implementation trivia. That gets us one executable plan instead of my five-step plan and Claude’s nine-step plan talking past each other. The dependency order stays clean, the build lanes stay obvious, and testing is attached where the risk actually appears instead of being waved at from the end.

**Claude (Backend / Systems Engineer · Systems Thinker) — Round 2**

Codex's structure is genuinely better organized than my parallel list — five milestones with hard exit criteria reads cleanly and I'd rather fold into it than keep two competing shapes alive. So let me do exactly what the coordinator asked and place my three additions concretely inside Codex's skeleton rather than re-litigating the ordering.

The spike belongs inside milestone one, not as a separate milestone before it. Codex's M1 exit criteria already says "model container survives relaunch" — that's the tell that the spike is implicitly assumed to just work. I want it made explicit and sequenced first within M1, because if the two-container-same-URL technique doesn't actually surface committed writes across instances (SwiftData's context caching behavior isn't something either of us has verified, we're both trusting the tech-spec's assertion), you don't find that out until milestone two's persistence tests start failing in a confusing way that looks like a store bug rather than a testing-methodology bug. Same for the CloudKit-safety check — it costs maybe half a day to point one throwaway `ModelConfiguration` at `cloudKitDatabase: .automatic` and confirm the schema doesn't throw, and it's much cheaper to do that before four stores and their seed-data logic are built on the assumption that "optional relationships plus defaults" is sufficient. So: M1 exit criteria gets two new bullets — "force-quit test methodology proven with a throwaway spike, not just assumed" and "schema validated against a CloudKit-enabled configuration once, entitlement still off." That's additive to Codex's milestone one, not a new milestone, and it doesn't change his sequencing at all.

The supersession integration test slots into milestone two's exit criteria list directly — Codex already named "supersession" as one of the boundary-test exit criteria, but the way it's phrased right now reads like it's covered by the same boundary-value unit tests as the 19m/21m and day-band tests. It isn't, and I want to be precise about why: those are pure-function tests over plain structs with no store involved. Supersession is fundamentally a stateful, store-level operation — `ReportSubmission.submit` decides between `saveReport`/`supersede`/`supersedeNearby`, writes through `ObstacleStore`, and the thing that can actually go wrong (a friction score that ratchets upward forever because an old report never got marked inactive, or a "looks clear now" that doesn't propagate) only shows up if you exercise the store, not the pure scorer. So under Codex's milestone two I want a named test that isn't just "supersession passes" but specifically: save an obstacle, submit a new report inside the inner 10m buffer, then assert `fetchActiveReports()` excludes the old one and `fetchHistory()` still returns both. If that test doesn't exist as a distinct line item, someone building milestone two will reasonably believe the unit tests already cover it, and they don't.

On the concurrency and timing items, I'll fold those into milestone four and five respectively rather than insisting on separate milestones, since Codex's structure already has natural homes for them: the 400–600ms loading floor test belongs in milestone three's exit criteria (that's where two-stage loading first exists), not milestone four — worth catching that placement now rather than losing it. And the concurrent-favorite-refresh-capped-at-2 test belongs in milestone five's exit criteria, right alongside "saved-route refresh is staggered so we do not spam MapKit," which Codex already wrote — I'd just add "and verified under concurrent access, not just visually staggered" to that same bullet, since staggering in the UI layer doesn't guarantee the underlying store calls are actually safe if two rows happen to trigger close together.

One thing Codex said that I want to push on rather than just fold into: "purchases come after favorites actually work, otherwise the team ends up polishing a gate on top of unstable value." I agree with the sequencing conclusion but I think the reasoning is incomplete from a systems angle — the bigger reason to build `PurchaseManaging` late isn't just "don't polish an unstable gate," it's that the entitlement check (`hasProUnlock`) is a cross-cutting read that touches `FavoritesStore.count()` at the exact moment of the third save attempt, and if that boolean isn't wired correctly you get either a silent double-unlock bug or a false paywall trigger for a user who already restored purchases. That's a state-consistency problem, not just a sequencing-nicety problem, so I want milestone five's exit criteria to explicitly include "restore-after-reinstall correctly suppresses the paywall on the very next favorite save attempt" as its own line, not folded generically into "restore works after reinstall" — restore working in isolation and restore correctly gating the count-check are two different assertions and only the second one is the actual product requirement.

So: I'm not proposing a sixth milestone or restructuring Codex's five. I'm proposing we take his skeleton as the base, and graft in four concrete exit-criteria additions (M1: prove the persistence-test methodology and CloudKit-schema-safety before relying on them; M2: name the store-level supersession test explicitly, distinct from the pure-function boundary tests; M3: assert the loading-floor timing mechanically; M5: verify concurrent favorite-refresh safety and verify restore-correctly-gates-paywall, not just "restore works"). If Codex agrees these are additions rather than competing structure, I think we're at consensus — the disagreement wasn't really about milestones, it was that my round one read like a rival plan instead of a patch to his.

**Gemini — Round 2 (skipped: CLI unavailable)**

_Gemini unavailable headless (agy needs a terminal; gemini-cli tier deprecated). gemini CLI -> auth/tier error_

**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 2**

CONSENSUS: YES

This round closed it. Codex took Claude's three round-one additions and grafted them directly into the five-milestone skeleton instead of treating them as a rival plan — the persistence/CloudKit spike goes into milestone one's exit criteria, the supersession check becomes a named store-level test in milestone two, and the concurrency/timing items get written into milestone three and five's exit criteria respectively. Claude then did the same in reverse: explicitly dropped its parallel nine-step structure, accepted Codex's skeleton as the base, and placed its asks as precise patches rather than a competing shape. The one real correction Claude made — that the two-stage-loading timing test belongs in milestone three (where Route Result's UI first exists), not milestone two (which is pure domain/persistence with no loading UI at all) — is just a logical fix to where Codex put it, not a disagreement in substance, so I'm folding it in that way. Claude's two sharpened asks in this round (verifying concurrent favorite-refresh safety under actual concurrent access rather than just visual staggering, and treating "restore correctly suppresses the paywall on the next save attempt" as its own required assertion rather than assuming it falls out of "restore works") are uncontested refinements in the same spirit as everything Codex already agreed to fold in — nobody has raised an objection to either, and both close real gaps in milestone five's exit criteria. Gemini stayed unavailable both rounds, so there's no third perspective, but Codex and Claude converged on a single, non-duplicated plan.

## Final Output

**Milestones:**

1. **Foundation & contracts.** Xcode project scaffold with the locked module layout (App/Domain/Persistence/Services/Features/Tests), `AppDependencies` composition root, SwiftData `ModelContainer`, StoreKit config, seed-data installer/reset with version flag, and just enough UI to prove navigation structure and onboarding. Before building the four persistence stores on top of it, run two narrow proof spikes: (a) confirm the two-independent-`ModelContainer`-against-the-same-store-URL technique actually surfaces committed writes across instances, and (b) point one throwaway `ModelConfiguration` at `cloudKitDatabase: .automatic` once and confirm the schema doesn't throw (entitlement stays off in shipped code). Exit criteria: app launches cleanly; the force-quit persistence test methodology is proven, not assumed; the schema is validated against a CloudKit-enabled config once; seed-data versioning and reset work; StoreKit config is wired; exactly one composition root, no ad hoc service wiring anywhere.

2. **Trust engine.** `ScoringConstants`, `ObstacleMatcher`, `RouteScorer`, `RouteExplanationBuilder` as pure static functions, `ReportSubmission`'s save/supersede/supersedeNearby decision logic, and the four persistence repositories (`ObstacleStore`, `FavoritesStore`, `RecentsStore`, `CachedRouteStore`) built on the now-proven spike pattern. Exit criteria: boundary tests pass at 19/21m, 9/11m, day 6/8, 30/31, 90/91, the single-route wording branch, the shorter-route tie-break, and cached-route staleness copy; force-quit persistence verified for real against all four stores; a named store-level supersession integration test — save an obstacle, submit a new report inside the inner 10m buffer, assert `fetchActiveReports()` excludes the old one while `fetchHistory()` still returns both — exists as its own test, distinct from the pure boundary-value unit tests.

3. **Core trip flow end to end.** Onboarding, permission handling, manual origin entry, destination search, live `RouteFetching`/`PlaceSearching`, Route Result with two-stage loading, recommendation/why/confidence, and map/list toggle. Saved and paywall UI are explicitly deferred past this milestone. Exit criteria: a fresh install can go from open app to route result to obstacle inspection under both current-location and manual-origin paths; permission-denied, offline, geocoding failure, no-route-found, no-nearby-obstacles, low-confidence, and single-route wording are all distinct, honestly-worded code paths; a mechanical test asserts the `checkingObstacles` phase holds its 400–600ms minimum even when local scoring is instant, so a later cleanup pass can't quietly delete the app's differentiator.

4. **Reporting & live rescoring.** Report sheet, obstacle detail sheet, "looks clear now," local rescoring of the already-held route snapshot (never a second directions call), seeded/demo labeling, and the accessible travel-order list. Exit criteria: a report completes in under 30 seconds; the visible recommendation changes with no new directions call; supersession behaves correctly through the live UI path; a manual VoiceOver pass on Route Result and the Report sheet reaches summary, every flagged item, and the report action with zero dead ends.

5. **Repeat use & monetization.** Favorites, recents, Saved tab refresh (lazy, staggered, capped at 2 concurrent), share snapshot/`ShareLink`, the two-favorite free cap, paywall, StoreKit purchase/restore, and settings (demo-data reset, clear recents). Exit criteria: saving/opening a favorite live-refetches correctly; the third favorite cleanly triggers the paywall; Saved refresh is not just visually staggered but verified safe under actual concurrent access — no race or double-fire when two rows trigger close together; restoring purchases after reinstall correctly suppresses the paywall on the very next favorite-save attempt (a distinct assertion from "restore works" in isolation, since it's really a state-consistency check on `hasProUnlock` feeding `FavoritesStore.count()`); share preview/export is honest and self-contained; a full regression script runs fresh install, demo corridor, manual origin, zero-obstacle route, low-confidence route, report submission, favorite save, paywall intercept, cached-route offline review, and seed reset.

**Dependencies:** Domain math and spike-proven persistence must exist before Route Result UI. Route Result must exist before reporting, since reporting rescales an already-held snapshot. Reporting must exist before Saved refresh, since Saved status reuses the same scoring/explanation engine. Purchases come after favorites genuinely work, so the paywall gates real value rather than an unstable feature. Parallel build lanes open up only after milestone one: domain/tests, services/persistence, Plan/Search/Onboarding, Route Result/reporting/accessibility, and Saved/paywall/share/settings (this last lane starts once favorites and purchase scaffolding are ready). Integration checkpoints land after milestones two, three, and four — not just at the end.

**Risk mitigations:** Trust-surface divergence between score math, explanation copy, and UI state is the top named risk — mitigated by treating `RouteExplanationBuilder` and `RouteRequestError` as the sole canonical output surfaces, with no view-specific rewording of trust states. MapKit variability (single-route returns, no-route cases, rare advisories, network dependence) is handled by building honest single-route and offline behavior as first-class cases from milestone three, not edge cases bolted on later. Accessibility regressions from a map-first build mindset are avoided by building the List representation alongside Route Result, not after it. Unverified persistence/CloudKit-safety assumptions are resolved by the milestone-one spike before four stores get built on top of them. Silent trust-breaking bugs in report supersession are caught by the named store-level integration test in milestone two. Quietly-regressed timing/concurrency behavior (the loading floor, staggered favorite refresh) is caught by named mechanical tests in milestones three and five rather than left to visual inspection. Entitlement state-consistency bugs (double-unlock or a false paywall for a user who already restored) are caught by the explicit restore-gates-paywall test in milestone five. No user accounts, backend, or auth exist anywhere in this app — named explicitly as a non-goal so no lane reinvents one out of habit.

**Verification plan:** Pure unit tests plus the persistence-survival and supersession integration tests run after milestone two. Manual scenario verification (permission granted, denied, offline) plus the loading-floor timing test run after milestone three. The live-rescore scenario and a manual VoiceOver pass on Route Result and Report run after milestone four. StoreKit purchase/restore testing, the accessibility audit (`performAccessibilityAudit()` in CI plus one manual pass), the concurrency-safety check on Saved refresh, and the full regression script all run after milestone five. Testing and integration are attached to each milestone's exit criteria throughout — none of it is deferred to a final polish pass.

**Quality Gate (Claude) — after round 2**

QUALITY: PASS

## Feedback

This closes cleanly. All four required outputs are present and concrete: five dependency-ordered milestones with explicit, testable exit criteria; a dependency section explaining *why* the ordering holds (Route Result before reporting because reporting rescales an already-held snapshot; reporting before Saved because Saved reuses the same scoring engine; purchases last because the paywall must gate real value); a risk-mitigation list naming specific failure modes with the milestone that catches each; and a verification plan that attaches concrete tests to milestones two through five rather than a generic "test everything" close.

Strengths:
- Genuine convergence, not a paper-over: Codex absorbed all three of Claude's round-one additions (persistence/CloudKit spike, named supersession integration test, concurrency/timing tests) into specific milestone exit criteria rather than treating them as a rival plan, and Claude reciprocated by dropping its competing nine-step structure.
- The one substantive correction — moving the loading-floor timing test from milestone two to milestone three, since milestone two has no loading UI at all — is called out and justified rather than silently applied.
- Both acceptance checks are satisfied: the plan is directly executable by a task-assignment phase (five milestones, named build lanes, clear parallelization point after milestone one), and testing/integration are demonstrably not deferred — each milestone's exit criteria bakes in its own tests (boundary values, supersession, timing floor, VoiceOver pass, concurrency safety, restore-gates-paywall).
- Full fidelity to prior-phase decisions: every named type (`ScoringConstants`, `ObstacleMatcher`, `RouteScorer`, `RouteExplanationBuilder`, `ReportSubmission`, the four stores, `AppDependencies`) matches the locked tech-spec interfaces exactly, and the plan reflects the locked product constraints (no accounts/backend, non-consumable Pro unlock, honest single-route/offline states).

No repair needed.

## Coordinator Decision

See the coordinator's message above.

## Final Output

phase_discussions.title: Task Assignments
phase_discussions.output:
CONSENSUS: YES

Here's where this actually landed. Codex and Claude converged on essentially everything by the end of round two — one root project path, one branch model, one canonical task backlog instead of two competing shapes, and precise language on what counts as a "shared edit zone" (just `AppDependencies.swift` plus target build settings/schemes — not routine new-file additions to the Xcode project, which every lane needs to do constantly and shouldn't be blocked on). DesignSystem ownership resolved cleanly: both agents now agree it sits with `primary_ui` and ships as a frozen milestone-1 deliverable, because Onboarding/Plan/Route Result can't stabilize without it and it would be a real scheduling inversion to make the core trip flow wait on a lane (`polish_resilience`) that doesn't start in earnest until milestone 5.

The one thing that looks like a live split on paper — Codex's round-2 message argues Saved should sit with `polish_resilience`, while Claude's round-2 message reverses itself and argues for `primary_ui` — isn't actually a coin-flip judgment call once you check the paper trail. Both of Claude's rounds were reasoning from feel ("Saved is monetization-adjacent"); the tie-breaker is that the tech-spec phase already locked `FavoriteRowStatus` and `SavedRoute` as `primary_ui`-owned types in the interfaces-json three phases ago, with both agents' explicit sign-off at the time. Codex's round-2 message was written in parallel and didn't get to react to that citation, but the citation itself is dispositive — it's not a new opinion, it's pointing at a decision this same pair of agents already made and is bound by. Splitting a feature so its view lives in one lane and its own state enum lives in another would just manufacture a merge conflict for no reason. So: Saved (view, view model, `FavoriteRowStatus`, `SavedRoute`, the concurrency test) goes to `primary_ui`, consistent with what was already locked, not decided fresh here.

Gemini stayed unavailable through both rounds, so this is a two-voice convergence, but a real one — every open question got an explicit resolution with a reason, not a shrug.

## Final Output

**Project root:** `/Users/pchordia/Documents/iOS-App-Factory/clearpath-access/ClearpathAccess/` — single Xcode project, `ClearpathAccessTests` and `ClearpathAccessUITests` as the two test targets.

**Branch model:** `lane/<name>` branches (`lane/data-domain`, `lane/services-utilities`, `lane/primary-ui`, `lane/polish-resilience`) hold task branches named `task/T-0NN-slug`. These merge into `app/clearpath-access` at the milestone checkpoints (after milestones 2, 3, 4, and 5 per the locked project plan), which then merges to `main`. No lane merges directly to `main`. No lane force-merges past a red merge-gate test to hit a checkpoint date — the checkpoint moves, the bar doesn't.

**Lane responsibilities and owned files:**
- **data_domain** — `Domain/Models/`, `Domain/Scoring/`, `Tests/DomainTests/`. Zero SwiftData/MapKit types. Owns and runs the boundary-value test suite (19/21m, 9/11m, day-band thresholds, tie-break) as the merge gate for anything under `Domain/Scoring/`.
- **services_utilities** — `Persistence/`, `Services/`, `Config/Products.storekit`, `Tests/IntegrationTests/` (spikes, supersession, persistence-survival, report-submission). Owns the force-quit/CloudKit-safety spikes and the store-level supersession test as merge gates for `Persistence/`.
- **primary_ui** — `App/` (except the integrator-only edits noted below), `DesignSystem/` (frozen milestone-1 deliverable, read-only to other lanes after), `Features/Onboarding`, `Features/Plan`, `Features/Search`, `Features/RouteResult`, `Features/ReportObstacle`, `Features/ObstacleDetail`, `Features/Saved` (including `FavoriteRowStatus` and `SavedRoute`, per the tech-spec-locked ownership), and the UI/accessibility tests tied to those screens.
- **polish_resilience** — `Domain/Explanation/`, `Domain/Share/`, `App/Flags/`, `Features/Paywall`, `Features/SharePreview`, `Features/Settings`, the accessibility CI gate, the restore-gates-paywall test, and the final regression script.

**Shared/forbidden edit zones:** `App/AppDependencies.swift` is edited directly only by `primary_ui` (the composition root gets built once with fakes, then re-touched once to wire real services). Target build settings, schemes, and entitlements require integrator sign-off; routine addition of new files to the Xcode target is not a violation and doesn't need cross-lane approval. No lane edits another lane's owned folders — if a new query shape or component variant is needed, it's a named handoff request against the owning lane's protocol/contract, never a drive-by edit. `RouteExplanationBuilder`, `RouteRequestError`, and `ScoringConstants` remain the sole canonical trust-output surfaces — no lane grows its own copy of confidence/severity wording.

## Phase Purpose

Produce concrete workstreams, file ownership, forbidden edit zones, branch names, merge strategy, testing responsibilities, communication protocol, and a conflict-prevention plan.

## Transcript


### Round 1


**Codex (Frontend Engineer · Systems Thinker) — Round 1**

The app should live as its own project folder at `/Users/pchordia/Documents/iOS-App-Factory/clearpath-access`. Inside that, keep the structure already locked: `App/`, `Domain/`, `Persistence/`, `Services/`, `Features/`, `DesignSystem/`, `Tests/`, plus the Xcode project and StoreKit config at the root. I would treat that folder as the only writable surface for this app and keep every other portfolio app out of scope. The cleanest branch model is one integration branch, `app/clearpath-access`, and one short-lived branch per lane off it: `lane/data-domain`, `lane/primary-ui`, `lane/services-utilities`, `lane/polish-resilience`. No direct commits to the integration branch except the integrator merge commits. That matters here because SwiftUI and Xcode project conflicts are cheap to create and expensive to untangle once multiple lanes start touching navigation, targets, and shared types.

The work split should follow ownership boundaries that match the locked interfaces, not screen names alone. `data_domain` owns all plain Swift structs/enums/functions under `Domain/` and the pure test target files that prove scoring, matching, explanation, confidence, and route recommendation behavior. `services_utilities` owns all persistence records, repositories, service implementations, the composition plumbing for model storage and platform wrappers, and the service-side integration tests. `primary_ui` owns `App/`, feature view models, navigation stacks, screen composition, state transitions, and the mechanical loading/offline/manual-origin behavior in the UI. `polish_resilience` owns `DesignSystem/`, share snapshot/export surfaces, paywall presentation layer, settings/info flows, copy consistency, accessibility audit harness, seeded/demo labeling polish, and regression scripts. The only shared edit zones should be `AppDependencies`, route-result-facing DTO consumption, and the Xcode project; those need explicit handoffs, not opportunistic edits.

I would make the forbidden zones strict. `primary_ui` must not redefine domain math, persistence queries, or StoreKit entitlement logic in views or view models. `services_utilities` must not write view copy, navigation logic, or ad hoc screen state enums. `data_domain` must not import SwiftUI, SwiftData, MapKit UI types, or touch target wiring. `polish_resilience` must not mutate persistence schemas, protocol contracts, or route-scoring behavior. `project.pbxproj`, StoreKit product wiring, and any shared protocol/type signature files are integrator-controlled while lanes are active; if a lane needs one changed, it proposes the diff and waits for the integrator merge window. That is the easiest way to stop parallel work from colliding on the highest-friction files.

The file map should be explicit enough that a worker can start immediately. `data_domain` gets files like `Domain/Models/*.swift`, `Domain/Scoring/*.swift`, `Domain/Reporting/ReportDraft.swift`, and `Tests/DomainTests/*`. `services_utilities` gets `Persistence/Models/*.swift`, `Persistence/Repositories/*.swift`, `Persistence/Seed/*.swift`, `Services/Location/*`, `Services/Routing/*`, `Services/Purchases/*`, `Services/Reachability/*`, `Services/AppDependencies.swift` implementation support, and `Tests/IntegrationTests/*` for persistence/service integration. `primary_ui` gets `App/ClearpathAccessApp.swift`, `App/RootView.swift`, `Features/Onboarding/*`, `Features/Plan/*`, `Features/Search/*`, `Features/RouteResult/*`, `Features/ObstacleDetail/*`, `Features/ReportObstacle/*`, `Features/Saved/*`, and UI test scaffolding tied to those flows. `polish_resilience` gets `DesignSystem/*`, `Features/Paywall/*`, `Features/SharePreview/*`, `Features/Settings/*`, `Features/Common/*` shared visual components that are not business logic, `Tests/AccessibilityTests/*`, `Tests/RegressionScripts/*`, and any snapshot/share formatting helpers.

The merge strategy should be contract-first and sequence-aware. First merge the type contracts and skeleton folders. Then merge domain and services before UI reaches for real implementations. Then merge `primary_ui` screens against fake-backed dependencies. Then merge `polish_resilience` once the underlying states exist. I would require each lane to rebase on `app/clearpath-access` before opening a merge, and I would batch merges at milestone boundaries rather than constantly drip-feed them. This app has too many trust states to tolerate hidden partial integrations. A lane does not merge because code compiles locally; it merges because its acceptance criteria are met and its downstream consumers can pick it up without changing the contract underneath it.

Testing responsibility should mirror the same boundaries. `data_domain` owns all boundary-value tests and the single-route/tie-break/confidence/explanation math assertions. `services_utilities` owns the two-container persistence survival spike, CloudKit-safe schema smoke check, supersession integration test, cached-route expiry lifecycle, and StoreKit entitlement plumbing tests. `primary_ui` owns the two-stage loading floor test, permission/manual-origin state coverage, no-route/offline/geocoding/low-confidence/single-route UI state tests, and route-result live-rescore behavior with fake services. `polish_resilience` owns `performAccessibilityAudit()`, the manual VoiceOver checklist script, share snapshot correctness, paywall gating presentation checks, and the end-to-end regression runbook. No test should be “everybody’s problem”; if a test breaks, one lane owns fixing it first.

The communication protocol needs to be lightweight but strict. Every lane posts a short contract note before changing a shared type, state enum, navigation payload, or protocol. Every handoff includes: files changed, new assumptions, test evidence, and one thing downstream lanes need to know. If a lane finds itself needing to edit another lane’s owned file, that is a stop signal, not a convenience edit. The conflict-prevention rule I would preserve in the final output is simple: shared contracts freeze at the start of a milestone unless the integrator approves a coordinated change across affected lanes. Without that, this app will drift fastest in exactly the places that hurt most: route result state, report supersession semantics, and purchase gating.

The biggest risk in this assignment plan is that `primary_ui` looks like the “integration lane” and ends up becoming a dumping ground for unresolved service or domain decisions. I would explicitly reject that. UI should consume stable contracts and represent trust states honestly; it should not become the place where missing backend logic gets improvised. The detail I want preserved in the final phase output is that `RouteResult` is the highest-risk coordination surface in the app, so its model inputs, loading phases, and explanation payloads need frozen ownership and explicit handoffs before screen work fans out.

**Claude (Backend / Systems Engineer · Closer) — Round 1**

The folder question gets decided right now, not punted: the Xcode project lives at `/Users/pchordia/Documents/iOS-App-Factory/clearpath-access/ClearpathAccess/` — a single `.xcodeproj` (or SPM-free single-target Xcode project, no workspace gymnastics) with `ClearpathAccess.xcodeproj` at that path, `ClearpathAccessTests` and `ClearpathAccessUITests` as the two test targets locked in the architecture round. Everything below assumes that root.

Four lanes, four non-overlapping ownership zones, matching the `owning_lane` tags already baked into the interfaces-json from the tech-spec phase — I'm not re-deciding those, I'm operationalizing them:

**data_domain** owns `Domain/Models/` (every plain struct/enum: `Coordinate`, `RouteEndpoint`, obstacle types, `RouteSnapshot`, `ScoredRoute`, `RouteRecommendation`, `Favorite`, `RecentDestination`, etc.) and `Domain/Scoring/` (`ScoringConstants`, `RouteScorer`, `ObstacleMatcher`). Zero SwiftData, zero MapKit, zero CoreLocation types leak in here — Foundation only, plus CoreLocation strictly for distance math. This lane also owns `Tests/DomainTests/` outright. Nobody else touches these two folders, full stop, because this is the one subsystem where a silent edit from another lane could quietly break a boundary-value test without anyone noticing until QA.

**services_utilities** owns `Persistence/` (SwiftData `@Model` records, `ModelContainerFactory`, `DemoDataSeeder`, the mapping layer, and all four repositories — `ObstacleRepository`, `FavoritesRepository`, `RecentsRepository`, `CachedRouteRepository`) and `Services/` (`LocationService`, `RouteSearchService`, `ReachabilityMonitor`, `PurchaseService`, `ReportSubmission`). This lane also owns the persistence/CloudKit spike and every integration test that touches a real store — supersession, force-quit survival, StoreKit sandbox restore. Nobody outside this lane edits a SwiftData model file or a repository, ever — if primary_ui or polish_resilience needs a new query shape, that's a handoff request against the protocol, not a drive-by edit.

**primary_ui** owns `App/` (entry point, `AppDependencies` composition root, `RootView`, per-tab `NavigationStack`s), `DesignSystem/` (tokens + the locked component inventory), and the core-trip-flow features: `Features/Onboarding/`, `Features/Plan/`, `Features/Search/`, `Features/RouteResult/`, `Features/ObstacleDetail/`, `Features/ReportObstacle/`. This lane is also the sole integrator for `App/AppDependencies.swift` — it's built once early with fakes so nobody's blocked, then re-touched once at the end to wire real services in. That file is the one legitimate shared-edit zone in this whole project, and it's locked to this lane specifically so there's exactly one hand on it.

**polish_resilience** owns `Domain/Explanation/` and `Domain/Share/` (yes, this puts two lanes inside `Domain/` — that's already how the interfaces-json assigned it three rounds ago, `RouteExplanationBuilder` and `ShareSnapshot` are deterministic pure functions just like the scorer, they just live under a different lane's accountability), plus `Features/Saved/`, `Features/Paywall/`, `Features/SharePreview/`, `Features/Settings/`. It also owns the accessibility CI gate, the Saved-concurrency test, the restore-gates-paywall test, and the final regression script.

The one thing I will not let slide into "later": `RouteExplanationBuilder` is a milestone-3 dependency (Route Result can't show a recommendation sentence without it) even though polish_resilience's feature work is milestone-5 work. So that task gets pulled to the front of that lane's queue — task ordering is by dependency, not by which milestone number a lane's other work happens to sit in. Same logic applies to the `hasCompletedFirstRouteSearch` flag: it's a polish_resilience-owned file but Plan needs it on day one, so it ships as a two-line task before anything else in that lane.

Branching: one branch per task, named `task/T-0NN-short-slug`, PRs target a shared `integration` branch, not `main` directly. `main` only receives merges from `integration` at the three named checkpoints from the project plan — after milestone 2, milestone 3, and milestone 4 — plus a final merge after milestone 5. The primary_ui lane lead is the integrator: they're the only one who resolves `AppDependencies.swift` conflicts and flips fakes to real implementations. Nobody force-merges past a red boundary-value test or a failed accessibility audit to hit a checkpoint date — the checkpoint moves before the bar does.

Testing ownership isn't "everyone tests their own stuff and hopes it lines up" — it's explicit: data_domain owns and runs the boundary-value suite (19/21m, 9/11m, day thresholds, tie-break) and that suite is a merge gate for any PR touching `Domain/Scoring/`. services_utilities owns and runs the supersession integration test and the two-container force-quit test, and *that* is the merge gate for anything touching `Persistence/`. primary_ui owns the loading-floor timing test and the manual VoiceOver pass on Route Result. polish_resilience owns the Saved-concurrency test, the restore-gates-paywall test, and the full regression script, and none of those get treated as "final polish" — they're written the same week as the feature, per the exit criteria already locked in the project plan.

Communication protocol: task status lives in the tasks-json backlog (`pending` → `in_progress` → `blocked` → `done`), not in side chat. If a lane discovers the interface contract is wrong or missing something mid-build, it doesn't quietly diverge — it flags the specific interface name and proposes the fix, and no lane redefines a type owned by another lane's file without that handoff. Given how much already got nailed down in the tech-spec phase, I don't expect many of these, but the discipline matters more here than on a normal app because the entire product's credibility rides on `RouteExplanationBuilder`, `RouteRequestError`, and `ScoringConstants` staying single-source-of-truth — if two lanes each grow their own slightly-different copy of "what does low confidence mean," the app becomes exactly the kind of subtly-dishonest product this whole design was built to avoid.

Biggest risk I'm naming out loud rather than letting it hide: task T-026, the integration pass that swaps fakes for real services in the composition root, is the single point where four lanes' work has to actually agree at once. I'm scheduling it explicitly as its own task with its own dependency list (everything from services_utilities plus Route Result from primary_ui) rather than assuming it falls out naturally at the end of milestone 3 — if it's not a named task with named dependencies, it's the kind of thing that gets discovered late and eats a week.

**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 1**

CONSENSUS: NO

Here's where round one actually landed. Both Codex and Claude agree on the big shape: four lanes matching the tech-spec's `owning_lane` tags (data_domain, services_utilities, primary_ui, polish_resilience), a single shared composition-root file (`AppDependencies.swift`) as the one legitimate cross-lane edit zone, integration checkpoints tied to the milestone boundaries already locked in the project plan, and named merge-gate tests per lane (boundary-value suite gates `Domain/Scoring/`, supersession + force-quit tests gate `Persistence/`, loading-floor + VoiceOver gate Route Result, concurrency + restore-gates-paywall gate Saved/Paywall). Nobody's contesting that skeleton.

But the two proposals don't actually match yet, and it's not just a style difference:

1. **Saved tab ownership conflicts.** Codex's T-004 puts `Features/Saved/` under `primary_ui` alongside the core trip flow. Claude's plan puts `Features/Saved/` under `polish_resilience` (T-021, T-022), reasoning that Saved refresh/concurrency/paywall-adjacent work belongs with the monetization lane since it's milestone-5 work. That's a real, unresolved disagreement about who owns those files.

2. **Design system ownership conflicts.** Codex's T-005 puts `DesignSystem/` (tokens + components) under `polish_resilience`. Claude's T-013 puts the same folder under `primary_ui`, arguing the core trip flow needs those components on day one and shouldn't wait on a lane that doesn't start in earnest until milestone 5. Same kind of conflict, same two lanes on opposite sides.

3. **Granularity mismatch.** Codex proposed six broad tasks (roughly one per milestone/lane). Claude proposed twenty-eight much finer-grained tasks that map almost 1:1 to the interfaces-json from the tech-spec phase, with per-file acceptance criteria. These aren't necessarily incompatible — the fine-grained list could just be Codex's six tasks exploded — but nobody has explicitly reconciled them into one backlog yet, and the acceptance check requires "build workers can start immediately," which favors the more granular version, but that needs to be said out loud and agreed, not just asserted by one side.

Next round needs to: (a) settle Saved and DesignSystem ownership with an actual argument, not just two competing assignments sitting side by side — my read is Claude's reasoning is stronger on both (design tokens are a T-012/T-013 dependency for the core flow that can't wait, and Saved/paywall/share are genuinely one coherent monetization surface that polish_resilience already owns per the tech-spec's lane tags for `ShareSnapshot`/`WarningChipModel`), but Codex should get the chance to push back or concede explicitly; (b) agree on one granularity — collapse into Claude's exploded task list rather than keeping two backlogs alive; (c) Gemini remains unavailable, so this stays a two-voice reconciliation.
